column3: What notes make up a C major scale?
column4: C D E F G A B
column5: C D E F# G A B
column6: C D E♭ F G A B
column7: C D E F G A B♭
column11: The C major scale uses the pattern W-W-H-W-W-W-H (whole and half steps), producing the natural notes C D E F G A B with no sharps or flats.
column12: Show on a piano keyboard diagram with scale degrees marked and half steps indicated. Have students sing the scale on solfège (Do-Re-Mi).
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory, Books 1-2. ABRSM Theory Grade 1-3. Practice: sing scales on solfège (Do-Re-Mi). Tenuto app (musictheory.net) — scale exercises.

column3: How many whole steps separate the 1st and 3rd notes of a major scale?
column4: 2 whole steps (a major 3rd)
column5: 1 whole step
column6: 1.5 whole steps
column7: 2.5 whole steps
column11: The 1st to 3rd degree of a major scale spans W-W (two whole steps), creating a major 3rd of 4 semitones — the defining interval that gives major scales their bright quality.
column12: Include a real song example: identify a well-known melody that uses this scale and point out where the scale notes appear.
column14: Hal Leonard Music Theory Workbook, Level 1. ABRSM Grade 2-3. Recommended: Adam Neely YouTube — scale fundamentals. Practice all scales from C, G, F, D, A, E.

column3: What is the step pattern of a natural minor scale?
column4: W-H-W-W-H-W-W
column5: W-W-H-W-W-W-H
column6: W-H-W-W-W-H-W
column7: H-W-W-H-W-W-W
column11: The natural minor scale (Aeolian mode) follows W-H-W-W-H-W-W, giving it a darker, sadder quality compared to the major scale pattern W-W-H-W-W-W-H.
column12: Add a practical exercise: write out this scale from three different root notes (C, G, F) to reinforce the interval pattern.
column14: Music Theory for Beginners (Robert Wallis, Rockschool). ABRSM Grade 2-4 scale requirements. Alfred's Music Theory Workbook.

column3: What is the 6th mode of the major scale called?
column4: Aeolian
column5: Dorian
column6: Phrygian
column7: Mixolydian
column11: The 7 modes of the major scale in order are: Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, Locrian. The 6th mode, Aeolian, is identical to the natural minor scale.
column12: Connect to instrument: show the scale as a fingering pattern on both piano keyboard and bass guitar neck for practical context.
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory. Practice: begin every lesson with a 5-minute scale routine in C, G, F, and A minor. Rockschool Grade 2-4.

column3: How many notes are in a pentatonic scale?
column11: Penta' means five. The major pentatonic omits the 4th and 7th degrees of the major scale, removing all semitone relationships and creating an open, versatile sound.
column12: Add ear training: play the scale and have students identify it from two options by sound before revealing the answer.
column14: Music Theory: The Complete Guide (Jake Jackson). ABRSM Grade 2-4. Teach scales in the context of songs students already know and love.

column3: The harmonic minor scale differs from natural minor by raising which degree?
column4: The 7th degree
column5: The 6th degree
column6: The 5th degree
column7: The 3rd degree
column11: The harmonic minor raises the 7th degree by a half step to create a leading tone that strongly resolves to the tonic. This also creates an augmented 2nd between the 6th and 7th degrees.
column12: Show on a piano keyboard diagram with scale degrees marked and half steps indicated. Have students sing the scale on solfège (Do-Re-Mi).
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory, Books 1-2. ABRSM Theory Grade 1-3. Practice: sing scales on solfège (Do-Re-Mi). Tenuto app (musictheory.net) — scale exercises.

column3: What is a whole tone scale?
column4: A 6-note scale where every interval is a whole step
column5: A scale using only whole notes
column6: A major scale without half steps
column7: A 7-note symmetric scale
column11: The whole tone scale has 6 notes, each a whole step apart (e.g. C D E F# G# A#). Because of its total symmetry, there are only 2 unique whole tone scales. It creates a dreamy, ambiguous sound.
column12: Include a real song example: identify a well-known melody that uses this scale and point out where the scale notes appear.
column14: Hal Leonard Music Theory Workbook, Level 1. ABRSM Grade 2-3. Recommended: Adam Neely YouTube — scale fundamentals. Practice all scales from C, G, F, D, A, E.

column3: What does 'relative minor' mean?
column4: A minor key that shares the same key signature as a major key
column5: A minor scale a half step below a major scale
column6: A minor scale built on the 5th degree
column7: A minor scale with the same tonic note
column11: Every major key has a relative minor starting on its 6th degree with the same key signature. For example, A minor is the relative minor of C major — both use no sharps or flats.
column12: Add a practical exercise: write out this scale from three different root notes (C, G, F) to reinforce the interval pattern.
column14: Music Theory for Beginners (Robert Wallis, Rockschool). ABRSM Grade 2-4 scale requirements. Alfred's Music Theory Workbook.

column3: Which scale is C major's relative minor?
column4: A natural minor
column5: E minor
column6: D minor
column7: G minor
column11: The relative minor of any major key starts on the 6th degree. The 6th degree of C major is A, so A natural minor is the relative minor of C major. Both use the same notes (no sharps or flats).
column12: Connect to instrument: show the scale as a fingering pattern on both piano keyboard and bass guitar neck for practical context.
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory. Practice: begin every lesson with a 5-minute scale routine in C, G, F, and A minor. Rockschool Grade 2-4.

column3: What is the blues scale?
column4: A minor pentatonic scale with an added ♭5 (the blue note)
column5: A major scale with a ♭3
column6: A whole tone scale with added notes
column7: A diminished scale with 6 notes
column11: The blues scale adds a ♭5 (flat five, the 'blue note') to the minor pentatonic. In C: C E♭ F G♭ G B♭. The ♭5 creates the characteristic blues tension between the ♭5 and the natural 5th.
column12: Add ear training: play the scale and have students identify it from two options by sound before revealing the answer.
column14: Music Theory: The Complete Guide (Jake Jackson). ABRSM Grade 2-4. Teach scales in the context of songs students already know and love.

column3: What is a tetrachord?
column4: A group of 4 consecutive scale notes spanning a perfect 4th
column5: A 4-note chord
column6: A 4-beat rhythmic pattern
column7: A scale with exactly 4 notes
column11: A major scale can be split into two identical tetrachords (W-W-H), each spanning a perfect 4th. E.g. C major: [C D E F] + [G A B C]. This tetrachord relationship connects adjacent keys on the circle of fifths.
column12: Show on a piano keyboard diagram with scale degrees marked and half steps indicated. Have students sing the scale on solfège (Do-Re-Mi).
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory, Books 1-2. ABRSM Theory Grade 1-3. Practice: sing scales on solfège (Do-Re-Mi). Tenuto app (musictheory.net) — scale exercises.

column3: How many sharps does G major have?
column11: G major has one sharp: F#. The scale is G A B C D E F# G. Without F#, there would be a whole step between the 6th and 7th degrees (E-F) instead of the required half step.
column12: Include a real song example: identify a well-known melody that uses this scale and point out where the scale notes appear.
column14: Hal Leonard Music Theory Workbook, Level 1. ABRSM Grade 2-3. Recommended: Adam Neely YouTube — scale fundamentals. Practice all scales from C, G, F, D, A, E.

column3: What is the melodic minor scale?
column4: A scale that raises both the 6th and 7th degrees ascending, and returns to natural minor descending
column5: A scale that only raises the 7th degree
column6: A scale identical to harmonic minor
column7: A major scale with a ♭3
column11: The melodic minor (classical form) raises both the 6th and 7th degrees ascending (to smooth over the augmented 2nd of harmonic minor), then reverts to natural minor descending. In jazz, the ascending form is used in both directions.
column12: Add a practical exercise: write out this scale from three different root notes (C, G, F) to reinforce the interval pattern.
column14: Music Theory for Beginners (Robert Wallis, Rockschool). ABRSM Grade 2-4 scale requirements. Alfred's Music Theory Workbook.

column3: How many semitones are in a perfect 5th?
column11: A perfect 5th spans 7 semitones. C to G: C-C#-D-D#-E-F-F#-G = 7 half steps. The perfect 5th is one of the most consonant intervals and is the basis of the circle of fifths.
column12: Add a melodic memory hook: identify the opening interval of a famous melody to anchor the sound in the ear (e.g., P5 = Star Wars theme).
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory, Book 1. ABRSM Theory Grade 1-2. Tenuto app — interval identification exercises. Melodic hooks: 'Happy Birthday' = M2, 'Twinkle Twinkle' = P5.

column3: What interval spans exactly one half step?
column4: Minor 2nd
column5: Major 2nd
column6: Perfect unison
column7: Diminished 3rd
column11: A minor 2nd (m2) is the smallest standard interval, spanning 1 semitone (e.g. C to C# or E to F). It creates strong dissonance and tension.
column12: Include a keyboard visual: highlight both notes on a one-octave piano diagram so students can see the span clearly.
column14: Paul Hindemith, Elementary Training for Musicians (Schott). ABRSM Grade 2-3. Interval memory hooks: P5 = 'Star Wars,' M3 = 'When the Saints,' m3 = 'Greensleeves.'

column3: What is a tritone?
column4: An interval of 3 whole steps (6 semitones), also called an augmented 4th or diminished 5th
column5: An interval of 3 half steps
column6: A chord built in 3rds
column7: A scale with 3 modes
column11: The tritone divides the octave exactly in half at 6 semitones. It can be written as an augmented 4th (C to F#) or diminished 5th (C to G♭). Historically called 'diabolus in musica' for its extreme dissonance.
column12: Add a singing exercise: have students sing this interval both ascending and descending to develop active aural recognition.
column14: Hal Leonard Essential Music Theory, Level 1. ABRSM Grade 2-3. EarMaster 7 app for interval drilling with instant feedback and progress tracking.

column3: What is the inversion of a major 3rd?
column4: Minor 6th
column5: Major 6th
column6: Minor 3rd
column7: Perfect 5th
column11: To invert a simple interval, subtract from 9: 9 - 3 = 6. Major inverts to minor, so a major 3rd (4 semitones) inverts to a minor 6th (8 semitones). Inverted intervals always sum to an octave (12 semitones).
column12: Include a half-step counting exercise: ask students to count the semitones between two note pairs and name the interval.
column14: Music Theory Workbook (Gary Ewer, Easy Music Theory). Practice both ascending and descending versions. EarMaster 7 or Perfect Ear app.

column3: How many semitones are in a major 2nd?
column11: A major 2nd spans 2 semitones (a whole step), e.g. C to D. It's the most common step size in major and minor scales, occurring 5 times in a major scale.
column12: Connect to chords: explain which chord this interval most prominently defines (e.g., minor 3rd = root to 3rd of any minor chord).
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory. ABRSM Grade 1-3. Teach intervals through singing: major scale = series of M2 intervals with two M3 intervals (1-3 and 5-7).

column3: What is the interval from C to E?
column4: Major 3rd
column5: Minor 3rd
column6: Perfect 4th
column7: Major 2nd
column11: C to E spans 4 semitones (C-C#-D-D#-E), which is a major 3rd (M3). Major 3rds give major chords and scales their characteristic bright, happy quality.
column12: Add a melodic memory hook: identify the opening interval of a famous melody to anchor the sound in the ear (e.g., P5 = Star Wars theme).
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory, Book 1. ABRSM Theory Grade 1-2. Tenuto app — interval identification exercises. Melodic hooks: 'Happy Birthday' = M2, 'Twinkle Twinkle' = P5.

column3: What is a 'compound interval'?
column4: An interval larger than an octave
column5: An interval made of two different notes played together
column6: A chord with 3 notes
column7: An interval smaller than a perfect 5th
column11: Compound intervals exceed one octave. A major 9th (e.g. C to D an octave above) is a compound version of a major 2nd. To find the simple equivalent, subtract 7 from the compound interval number.
column12: Include a keyboard visual: highlight both notes on a one-octave piano diagram so students can see the span clearly.
column14: Paul Hindemith, Elementary Training for Musicians (Schott). ABRSM Grade 2-3. Interval memory hooks: P5 = 'Star Wars,' M3 = 'When the Saints,' m3 = 'Greensleeves.'

column3: What is an enharmonic equivalent?
column4: Two notes that sound the same but are written differently (e.g. F# and G♭)
column5: Two notes that are an octave apart
column6: Two notes with the same letter name
column7: An interval that inverts to itself
column11: Enharmonic notes share the same pitch but have different names and spellings. F# and G♭ are the same key on a piano but are notated differently depending on the key or harmonic context.
column12: Add a singing exercise: have students sing this interval both ascending and descending to develop active aural recognition.
column14: Hal Leonard Essential Music Theory, Level 1. ABRSM Grade 2-3. EarMaster 7 app for interval drilling with instant feedback and progress tracking.

column3: What is the interval from C to G♭?
column4: Diminished 5th
column5: Augmented 4th
column6: Perfect 5th
column7: Minor 5th
column11: C to G is a perfect 5th (7 semitones). Lowering G to G♭ reduces this by one semitone to 6, creating a diminished 5th. Note: a diminished 5th and augmented 4th are enharmonically the same (both 6 semitones) but are spelled differently.
column12: Include a half-step counting exercise: ask students to count the semitones between two note pairs and name the interval.
column14: Music Theory Workbook (Gary Ewer, Easy Music Theory). Practice both ascending and descending versions. EarMaster 7 or Perfect Ear app.

column3: How many semitones are in a major 7th?
column11: A major 7th spans 11 semitones, one half step below an octave (C to B). It creates a bright tension that wants to resolve up to the octave. This is the interval that gives maj7 chords their characteristic dreamy sound.
column12: Connect to chords: explain which chord this interval most prominently defines (e.g., minor 3rd = root to 3rd of any minor chord).
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory. ABRSM Grade 1-3. Teach intervals through singing: major scale = series of M2 intervals with two M3 intervals (1-3 and 5-7).

column3: What is the inversion of a perfect 4th?
column4: Perfect 5th
column5: Perfect 4th
column6: Major 6th
column7: Minor 5th
column11: A perfect 4th (5 semitones) inverts to a perfect 5th (7 semitones). 9 - 4 = 5, and perfect inverts to perfect. Together they sum to an octave: 5 + 7 = 12 semitones.
column12: Add a melodic memory hook: identify the opening interval of a famous melody to anchor the sound in the ear (e.g., P5 = Star Wars theme).
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory, Book 1. ABRSM Theory Grade 1-2. Tenuto app — interval identification exercises. Melodic hooks: 'Happy Birthday' = M2, 'Twinkle Twinkle' = P5.

column3: How many semitones are in a minor 3rd?
column11: A minor 3rd spans 3 semitones, e.g. C to E♭. It's the defining interval of minor chords and scales. The difference between a major and minor chord is one note: the major 3rd (4 semitones) vs. minor 3rd (3 semitones).
column12: Include a keyboard visual: highlight both notes on a one-octave piano diagram so students can see the span clearly.
column14: Paul Hindemith, Elementary Training for Musicians (Schott). ABRSM Grade 2-3. Interval memory hooks: P5 = 'Star Wars,' M3 = 'When the Saints,' m3 = 'Greensleeves.'

column3: What notes make up a C major triad?
column4: C E G
column5: C E♭ G
column6: C E G#
column7: C F G
column11: A major triad is built with a major 3rd then a minor 3rd above the root: C (root) + E (major 3rd, 4 semitones) + G (minor 3rd above E, 3 semitones). Total: root, M3, P5.
column12: Show on both piano and guitar with a chord diagram. Include a pop or rock song where this chord type is central to the progression.
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory, Books 1-2. ABRSM Grade 3-4. Hal Leonard Chord Theory for Guitarists. Practice: build each triad type from C, G, F, D, Bb.

column3: What makes a chord 'diminished'?
column4: A minor 3rd and diminished 5th — two stacked minor 3rds
column5: A major 3rd and a diminished 5th
column6: It has all minor intervals
column7: It is missing the 5th
column11: A diminished triad (e.g. B D F) has a root, minor 3rd (3 semitones), and diminished 5th (6 semitones). It can be seen as two stacked minor 3rds. It has a very tense, unstable sound.
column12: Add a voice leading note: which note resolves and where does it go when this chord moves to the next chord in a typical progression?
column14: Music Theory for Guitarists (Tom Kolb, Hal Leonard). Piano chord charts: Alfred's Piano Encyclopedia. ABRSM Grade 3-4 chord construction.

column3: What is a dominant 7th chord?
column4: A major triad with a minor 7th added (e.g. G7 = G B D F)
column5: A major triad with a major 7th added
column6: A minor triad with a minor 7th
column7: A diminished triad with a 7th added
column11: The dominant 7th chord (e.g. G7 = G B D F) is built on the 5th scale degree. Its major triad + minor 7th creates strong tension that pulls toward the tonic. It's the most tension-generating chord in tonal music.
column12: Include a construction exercise: build this chord type from roots C, G, D, and F using the interval formula as a guide.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book, Ch. 1-3 (introductory chord section). Hal Leonard Music Theory Workbook. Recommended: chordie.com for chord diagrams.

column3: What is a major 7th chord?
column4: A major triad with a major 7th added (e.g. Cmaj7 = C E G B)
column5: A major triad with a minor 7th added
column6: A dominant 7th chord
column7: A minor triad with a major 7th
column11: Cmaj7 = C E G B. The major 7th (B) sits a half step below the octave, giving the chord a warm, lush quality. It differs from G7 (dominant 7th) which uses a ♭7.
column12: Add a listening exercise: play a I-IV-V-I and ask students to identify which chord is this type by ear before checking.
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory. ABRSM Grade 3. Tenuto app — chord identification exercises. Teach chords by building them on piano from known notes.

column3: What are the 4 basic triad types?
column4: Major, minor, diminished, augmented
column5: Major, minor, dominant, suspended
column6: Major, minor, diminished, half-diminished
column7: Perfect, major, minor, diminished
column11: The four triads are: major (M3+m3=P5), minor (m3+M3=P5), diminished (m3+m3=d5), and augmented (M3+M3=A5). All other chords are extensions or alterations of these.
column12: Connect to a real musical moment: name a classic song intro or chorus that opens with this specific chord type.
column14: Hal Leonard Essential Music Theory. ABRSM Grade 3-4. Recommended YouTube: Ben Levin — chord theory for beginners.

column3: What does 'chord inversion' mean?
column4: A chord where a note other than the root is in the bass
column5: Playing a chord backwards
column6: Playing a chord in a different octave
column7: Changing the chord quality
column11: A chord is in first inversion when the 3rd is in the bass (e.g. C/E = C major with E bass), second inversion when the 5th is in the bass (C/G). Inversions create smoother bass lines and different voicing colours.
column12: Show on both piano and guitar with a chord diagram. Include a pop or rock song where this chord type is central to the progression.
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory, Books 1-2. ABRSM Grade 3-4. Hal Leonard Chord Theory for Guitarists. Practice: build each triad type from C, G, F, D, Bb.

column3: What is a sus4 chord?
column4: A chord where the 3rd is replaced by a perfect 4th
column5: A chord with an added 4th alongside the 3rd
column6: A chord suspended on beat 4
column7: A chord missing the 5th
column11: In a sus4 chord (e.g. Csus4 = C F G), the major or minor 3rd is replaced by a perfect 4th. The suspended 4th creates tension that typically resolves down to the 3rd, giving a characteristic 'pull and release' effect.
column12: Add a voice leading note: which note resolves and where does it go when this chord moves to the next chord in a typical progression?
column14: Music Theory for Guitarists (Tom Kolb, Hal Leonard). Piano chord charts: Alfred's Piano Encyclopedia. ABRSM Grade 3-4 chord construction.

column3: What is an augmented triad?
column4: A chord with a root, major 3rd, and augmented 5th (two stacked major 3rds)
column5: A major triad raised by a half step
column6: A chord with a root, major 3rd, and major 6th
column7: A major triad with an added sharp
column11: An augmented triad (e.g. C+ = C E G#) has two stacked major 3rds. Its symmetrical structure gives it an eerie, unstable sound. It appears naturally on the 3rd degree of the harmonic minor scale.
column12: Include a construction exercise: build this chord type from roots C, G, D, and F using the interval formula as a guide.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book, Ch. 1-3 (introductory chord section). Hal Leonard Music Theory Workbook. Recommended: chordie.com for chord diagrams.

column3: What is the symbol for a minor 7th chord?
column4: m7 (e.g. Dm7)
column5: maj7
column7: dim7
column11: A minor 7th chord (m7) has a minor triad plus a minor 7th. Dm7 = D F A C. It's one of the most common chords in jazz and pop, and acts as the ii chord in ii-V-I progressions.
column12: Add a listening exercise: play a I-IV-V-I and ask students to identify which chord is this type by ear before checking.
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory. ABRSM Grade 3. Tenuto app — chord identification exercises. Teach chords by building them on piano from known notes.

column3: Which chord type is found on the 7th degree of a major scale?
column4: Half-diminished (m7♭5)
column5: Diminished triad
column6: Diminished 7th
column7: Minor 7th
column11: The 7th degree chord in a major scale (e.g. Bm7♭5 in C major) has a minor 3rd, diminished 5th, and minor 7th — making it half-diminished. It's also written as ø7, and acts as a pre-dominant chord in minor keys.
column12: Connect to a real musical moment: name a classic song intro or chorus that opens with this specific chord type.
column14: Hal Leonard Essential Music Theory. ABRSM Grade 3-4. Recommended YouTube: Ben Levin — chord theory for beginners.

column3: What does 'add9' mean in a chord symbol like Cadd9?
column4: Add the 9th (2nd) to the triad without including the 7th
column5: Add a 9th and 7th
column6: Raise the 5th by a step
column7: Play the chord 9 frets higher
column11: Cadd9 = C E G D. The 'add' means the 9th is added directly to the triad without a 7th. This differs from Cmaj9 (which includes the 7th: C E G B D). Add9 chords are common in pop and gospel.
column12: Show on both piano and guitar with a chord diagram. Include a pop or rock song where this chord type is central to the progression.
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory, Books 1-2. ABRSM Grade 3-4. Hal Leonard Chord Theory for Guitarists. Practice: build each triad type from C, G, F, D, Bb.

column3: What is a slash chord (e.g. G/B)?
column4: A chord over a specific bass note different from the root
column5: A chord played very quickly
column6: A chord written across two bars
column7: A chord with a diminished quality
column11: G/B means a G major chord with B in the bass — this is a first inversion G chord. Slash chords are used to create smooth bass lines or specific voicings. They're very common in pop, gospel, and jazz.
column12: Add a voice leading note: which note resolves and where does it go when this chord moves to the next chord in a typical progression?
column14: Music Theory for Guitarists (Tom Kolb, Hal Leonard). Piano chord charts: Alfred's Piano Encyclopedia. ABRSM Grade 3-4 chord construction.

column3: How many notes are in a triad?
column11: Tri' means three — a triad has root, 3rd, and 5th. Adding a 7th makes it a 7th chord (4 notes). Adding the 9th makes it a 9th chord (5 notes), and so on for extended chords.
column12: Include a construction exercise: build this chord type from roots C, G, D, and F using the interval formula as a guide.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book, Ch. 1-3 (introductory chord section). Hal Leonard Music Theory Workbook. Recommended: chordie.com for chord diagrams.

column3: What does Roman numeral 'I' indicate in music theory?
column4: The chord built on the 1st degree (tonic) of the scale
column5: The first inversion of a chord
column6: A chord played once
column7: The interval of a unison
column11: Roman numerals represent chords by scale degree. Uppercase = major (I, IV, V), lowercase = minor (ii, iii, vi). In C major, I = C major, ii = D minor, V = G major. This allows analysis independent of specific key.
column12: Add a visual example: show this concept in standard notation in C major with all four voices (SATB) written out correctly.
column14: Stefan Kostka & Dorothy Payne, Tonal Harmony, 8th ed. (McGraw-Hill). Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory, Book 3. ABRSM Grade 4-5.

column3: What is a perfect authentic cadence?
column4: V resolving to I, both in root position, with the tonic in the soprano
column5: Any V-I progression
column6: IV-I (plagal cadence)
column7: A cadence ending on a minor chord
column11: The perfect authentic cadence (PAC) is the strongest possible cadence: V to I, both in root position, tonic note in the top voice. It gives a definitive, conclusive sense of ending.
column12: Include a singing exercise: have students sing the soprano and bass lines of this progression to physically feel the resolution.
column14: Walter Piston, Harmony, 5th ed. (Norton). ABRSM Theory Grade 4-5. Practice: play each cadence type on piano, singing the soprano voice above it.

column3: What is a plagal cadence?
column4: IV resolving to I (also called the 'Amen cadence')
column5: V-I
column6: ii-V
column7: I-IV
column11: The plagal cadence (IV-I) has a softer, more settled quality than the authentic cadence. It's often called the 'Amen cadence' because it's used at the end of hymns. In C major: F major → C major.
column12: Add a real-world example: identify a specific song, hymn, or jingle where this cadence or harmonic device is clearly audible.
column14: Steven Laitz, The Complete Musician, 4th ed. (Oxford). ABRSM Grade 4-5. Recommended YouTube: Adam Neely — 'Why the V-I resolution works.'

column3: What is a deceptive cadence?
column4: V resolving to vi instead of the expected I
column5: I-IV
column6: A cadence that goes to the wrong key
column7: V-IV
column11: In a deceptive cadence, the V chord resolves 'deceptively' to vi instead of I. In C major: G7 → Am. The listener expects resolution to C but gets Am instead — surprising but still consonant.
column12: Include a Roman numeral labeling exercise: give students an unmarked passage and ask them to identify each chord by function.
column14: Aldwell, Schachter & Cadwallader, Harmony and Voice Leading, 4th ed. (Cengage). ABRSM Grade 5. Identify cadences in familiar hymns and folk songs.

column3: What is a half cadence?
column4: A phrase ending on the V chord (creates an open, questioning feeling)
column5: A cadence using half notes only
column6: A cadence lasting exactly 2 beats
column7: IV-I
column11: A half cadence ends on V without resolving to I, creating an open, questioning feeling — like a musical comma. It typically appears at the end of an antecedent (question) phrase, expecting a consequent (answer) to follow.
column12: Add a play-along exercise: have students play this progression on piano using block chords, then try it in two other keys.
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory, Book 2-3. Harmony workbook (Stefan Kostka). ABRSM Grade 4. Play cadences in multiple keys to build physical memory.

column3: In a ii-V-I progression in C major, what are the chords?
column4: Dm7 – G7 – Cmaj7
column5: Em7 – Am7 – Cmaj7
column6: Dm – G – C
column7: Dm7 – G7 – C7
column11: In C major: ii = Dm7, V = G7, I = Cmaj7. This is the most fundamental chord progression in jazz. The ii prepares the V, which creates tension that resolves to I. Found in virtually every jazz standard.
column12: Add a visual example: show this concept in standard notation in C major with all four voices (SATB) written out correctly.
column14: Stefan Kostka & Dorothy Payne, Tonal Harmony, 8th ed. (McGraw-Hill). Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory, Book 3. ABRSM Grade 4-5.

column3: What is the 'dominant' in a key?
column4: The 5th degree of the scale and its chord
column5: The loudest chord in a progression
column6: The 4th degree
column7: The leading tone
column11: The dominant (V) is the chord built on the 5th degree of the scale. In C major, V = G (or G7). The dominant creates strong tension through its tritone (F and B in G7) that pulls toward resolution on I.
column12: Include a singing exercise: have students sing the soprano and bass lines of this progression to physically feel the resolution.
column14: Walter Piston, Harmony, 5th ed. (Norton). ABRSM Theory Grade 4-5. Practice: play each cadence type on piano, singing the soprano voice above it.

column3: What is the 'subdominant'?
column4: The 4th degree of the scale and its chord
column5: The 5th degree
column6: The note below the dominant
column7: The 6th degree
column11: The subdominant (IV) is the chord built on the 4th degree. In C major, IV = F major. It's called 'subdominant' because it lies a perfect 5th below the tonic — symmetrically opposite to the dominant (a 5th above).
column12: Add a real-world example: identify a specific song, hymn, or jingle where this cadence or harmonic device is clearly audible.
column14: Steven Laitz, The Complete Musician, 4th ed. (Oxford). ABRSM Grade 4-5. Recommended YouTube: Adam Neely — 'Why the V-I resolution works.'

column3: What is 'voice leading'?
column4: The smooth movement of individual melodic lines within a chord progression
column5: Adding vocal harmonies to a melody
column6: Writing lyrics for chords
column7: The register of a singer
column11: Voice leading describes how each individual voice (soprano, alto, tenor, bass) moves from chord to chord. Good voice leading favors step-wise motion and avoids parallel perfect 5ths or octaves between outer voices.
column12: Include a Roman numeral labeling exercise: give students an unmarked passage and ask them to identify each chord by function.
column14: Aldwell, Schachter & Cadwallader, Harmony and Voice Leading, 4th ed. (Cengage). ABRSM Grade 5. Identify cadences in familiar hymns and folk songs.

column3: What is a 'secondary dominant'?
column4: A dominant 7th chord that resolves to a chord other than I (e.g. V/V resolves to V)
column5: Any chord on the 5th degree
column6: A chord borrowed from the parallel minor
column7: A chord that appears twice in a progression
column11: Secondary dominants temporarily tonicize non-tonic chords. V/V (five of five) is the most common — in C major, V/V = D7, which resolves to G (V). They add chromatic color and harmonic momentum.
column12: Add a play-along exercise: have students play this progression on piano using block chords, then try it in two other keys.
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory, Book 2-3. Harmony workbook (Stefan Kostka). ABRSM Grade 4. Play cadences in multiple keys to build physical memory.

column3: What is 'modulation'?
column4: A key change within a piece of music
column5: A dynamic change
column6: A tempo change
column7: Playing the same notes in a different octave
column11: Modulation is the process of moving from one key to another within a piece. The most common modulation is to the dominant key (e.g. C major to G major). Modulations can be brief (tonicization) or long-term.
column12: Add a visual example: show this concept in standard notation in C major with all four voices (SATB) written out correctly.
column14: Stefan Kostka & Dorothy Payne, Tonal Harmony, 8th ed. (McGraw-Hill). Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory, Book 3. ABRSM Grade 4-5.

column3: What function does the ii chord serve in a major key?
column4: Pre-dominant — it prepares and leads to the V chord
column5: Tonic — it acts as a resting point
column6: Mediant — it bridges I and IV
column7: Dominant — it creates the most tension
column11: The ii chord (e.g. Dm7 in C major) is pre-dominant. It commonly progresses to V, building harmonic tension before the dominant. The ii-V-I is the backbone of jazz harmony and is found in all tonal styles.
column12: Include a singing exercise: have students sing the soprano and bass lines of this progression to physically feel the resolution.
column14: Walter Piston, Harmony, 5th ed. (Norton). ABRSM Theory Grade 4-5. Practice: play each cadence type on piano, singing the soprano voice above it.

column3: What does the top number of a time signature tell you?
column4: The number of beats per measure
column5: The note value that gets one beat
column6: The tempo in BPM
column7: The number of measures in a phrase
column11: In 4/4, the top number (4) = 4 beats per measure. The bottom number indicates the note value. The time signature defines the metric framework for the entire piece unless changed.
column12: Add a practical hands-on exercise so students can apply this concept with an instrument immediately.
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory (Alfred Music). ABRSM Theory Grade 1-3. musictheory.net — free exercises and reference charts.

column3: What does the bottom number '4' in a time signature mean?
column4: The quarter note gets one beat
column5: There are 4 measures in a phrase
column6: The tempo is 4 beats per minute
column7: There are 4 eighth notes per beat
column11: The bottom number represents a note value: 4 = quarter note, 8 = eighth note, 2 = half note, 1 = whole note. In 6/8, there are 6 eighth notes per measure.
column12: Include a real-world musical example from a song students already know and love.
column14: Hal Leonard Music Theory Workbook. ABRSM Grade 2-3. Tenuto app (musictheory.net) for drilling and self-assessment.

column3: How many beats are in one measure of 3/4 time?
column11: In 3/4, the top number = 3 beats per measure, each worth a quarter note. Waltz is the classic example of 3/4. It has a strong beat-1 with lighter beats 2 and 3.
column12: Add a visual diagram or notation example to make the abstract concept concrete.
column14: Rockschool Grade 2-4 Theory. Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory. Find this concept in sheet music students are currently practising.

column3: What is 6/8 time?
column4: Compound duple meter — 6 eighth notes per bar, felt as 2 beats with 3 subdivisions each
column5: Simple triple meter
column6: A time signature with 6 separate beats
column7: The same as 3/4
column11: 6/8 is compound duple — two main beats per bar, each divided into three eighth notes (dotted quarter = one beat). It sounds very different from 3/4 and has a lilting, flowing feel perfect for jigs and lullabies.
column12: Pair with an ear training component — can students recognize this concept by listening alone?
column14: AB Guide to Music Theory, Part 1 (ABRSM). musictheory.net — exercises and reference. Create an Anki flashcard set for this concept.

column3: How many eighth notes fit in one measure of 4/4?
column11: In 4/4, there are 4 beats and 2 eighth notes per beat: 4 × 2 = 8 eighth notes. Eighth notes divide each quarter-note beat in half.
column12: Include a comparison to the most commonly confused related concept.
column14: Music Theory for the 21st-Century Classroom (Robert Hutchinson, free online). ABRSM Grade 1-3. musictheory.net quick reference.

column3: What is syncopation?
column4: Placing emphasis on weak beats or between beats, creating rhythmic tension
column5: Playing in an irregular time signature
column6: Gradually increasing tempo
column7: A sudden change from loud to quiet
column11: Syncopation accents the 'off-beats' or weak parts of the beat. In 4/4, syncopating might accent beat 2, beat 4, or the 'and' of beats. It's fundamental to jazz, funk, reggae, and Latin music.
column12: Add a practical hands-on exercise so students can apply this concept with an instrument immediately.
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory (Alfred Music). ABRSM Theory Grade 1-3. musictheory.net — free exercises and reference charts.

column3: What is a 'dotted quarter note'?
column4: A quarter note extended by half its value, equal to 1.5 beats in 4/4
column5: A quarter note played staccato
column6: A quarter note with an accent
column7: Two tied eighth notes
column11: A dot adds half the original note's value. Dotted quarter = quarter (1) + eighth (0.5) = 1.5 beats. The dotted quarter note is the standard beat unit in compound meters like 6/8 and 9/8.
column12: Include a real-world musical example from a song students already know and love.
column14: Hal Leonard Music Theory Workbook. ABRSM Grade 2-3. Tenuto app (musictheory.net) for drilling and self-assessment.

column3: What is a triplet?
column4: 3 notes played evenly in the space of 2 notes (a type of tuplet)
column5: A group of 3 notes forming a chord
column6: 3 beats in a measure
column7: A trill between 3 notes
column11: An eighth-note triplet divides one beat into 3 equal parts instead of 2. Written with a '3' bracket over the notes. Triplets give music a rolling, swinging quality and are central to jazz phrasing.
column12: Add a visual diagram or notation example to make the abstract concept concrete.
column14: Rockschool Grade 2-4 Theory. Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory. Find this concept in sheet music students are currently practising.

column3: What does 'common time' mean?
column4: 4/4 time
column5: The most commonly used tempo
column6: Any standard time signature
column7: 2/2 time
column11: Common time is 4/4 — 4 quarter-note beats per measure. It's often notated with a 'C' symbol instead of 4/4. It's the most widely used time signature in Western music.
column12: Pair with an ear training component — can students recognize this concept by listening alone?
column14: AB Guide to Music Theory, Part 1 (ABRSM). musictheory.net — exercises and reference. Create an Anki flashcard set for this concept.

column3: What is 'cut time' (alla breve)?
column4: 2/2 time — 2 half-note beats per measure
column5: 4/4 played at double speed
column6: 3/2 time
column7: A time signature with no defined beat
column11: Cut time (2/2) has 2 beats per measure, each beat a half note. It gives a lighter, faster feel and is common in marches and fast orchestral passages. It's notated with a 'C' with a vertical line through it.
column12: Include a comparison to the most commonly confused related concept.
column14: Music Theory for the 21st-Century Classroom (Robert Hutchinson, free online). ABRSM Grade 1-3. musictheory.net quick reference.

column3: How many sixteenth notes fit in one beat of 4/4?
column11: A sixteenth note is 1/4 of a beat (quarter note) in 4/4, so there are 4 sixteenth notes per beat: 4 × 4 = 16 per measure. Sixteenth notes are counted as '1 e and a 2 e and a...'
column12: Add a practical hands-on exercise so students can apply this concept with an instrument immediately.
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory (Alfred Music). ABRSM Theory Grade 1-3. musictheory.net — free exercises and reference charts.

column3: What is a 'rest'?
column4: A symbol indicating silence for a specific duration
column5: A pause between songs
column6: A symbol to slow down
column7: An accent mark
column11: Rests are the silent counterpart to notes — they indicate that no note should be played for a specific duration. Every note value (whole, half, quarter, eighth, sixteenth) has a corresponding rest symbol.
column12: Include a real-world musical example from a song students already know and love.
column14: Hal Leonard Music Theory Workbook. ABRSM Grade 2-3. Tenuto app (musictheory.net) for drilling and self-assessment.

column3: What is polyrhythm?
column4: Two or more different rhythmic patterns played simultaneously (e.g. 3 against 2)
column5: A rhythm that changes time signature every bar
column6: Playing in multiple keys at once
column7: A very fast rhythmic pattern
column11: Polyrhythm layers different rhythmic groupings simultaneously, such as 3 notes against 2 (hemiola). It's fundamental to African music, Brazilian rhythms, jazz, and is used extensively by composers like Chopin and Brahms.
column12: Add a visual diagram or notation example to make the abstract concept concrete.
column14: Rockschool Grade 2-4 Theory. Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory. Find this concept in sheet music students are currently practising.

column3: What is 'AABA' form?
column4: A 32-bar form with two 8-bar A sections, an 8-bar bridge (B), and a final 8-bar A
column5: A 4-bar repeating phrase
column6: A form with 4 identical sections
column7: A 12-bar blues form
column11: AABA (American Songbook form) is a 32-bar structure used in jazz standards like 'I Got Rhythm' and 'Misty'. The B section (bridge) provides contrast. Each section is typically 8 bars.
column12: Add a practical hands-on exercise so students can apply this concept with an instrument immediately.
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory (Alfred Music). ABRSM Theory Grade 1-3. musictheory.net — free exercises and reference charts.

column3: What is the 12-bar blues form?
column4: A repeating 12-bar chord progression using I, IV, and V chords
column5: A 12-beat rhythmic pattern
column6: A blues scale with 12 notes
column7: A form with 12 different sections
column11: The 12-bar blues is foundational to blues, rock, and jazz. Standard progression: 4 bars of I, 2 bars of IV, 2 bars of I, 1 bar V, 1 bar IV (or V), 2 bars I. Countless songs are built on this framework.
column12: Include a real-world musical example from a song students already know and love.
column14: Hal Leonard Music Theory Workbook. ABRSM Grade 2-3. Tenuto app (musictheory.net) for drilling and self-assessment.

column3: What is a 'verse-chorus' form?
column4: A song structure alternating between narrative verses and a repeated emotional chorus
column5: A 32-bar jazz form
column6: A form where every section is different
column7: A classical form with exposition and development
column11: Verse-chorus form is the most common structure in pop and rock. Verses develop the story; the chorus delivers the main emotional hook and is repeated. Optional sections include pre-chorus, bridge, and outro.
column12: Add a visual diagram or notation example to make the abstract concept concrete.
column14: Rockschool Grade 2-4 Theory. Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory. Find this concept in sheet music students are currently practising.

column3: What is a 'coda'?
column4: A concluding section added at the end of a piece
column5: The opening theme
column6: A middle section of contrast
column7: A repeat sign
column11: A coda (Italian for 'tail') is an ending section that brings the piece to a definitive close. It may summarize themes or build to a final climax. The coda symbol ⊕ marks where to jump after following D.S. or D.C.
column12: Pair with an ear training component — can students recognize this concept by listening alone?
column14: AB Guide to Music Theory, Part 1 (ABRSM). musictheory.net — exercises and reference. Create an Anki flashcard set for this concept.

column3: What is a 'bridge' in a song?
column4: A contrasting section providing relief from the main theme, before returning
column5: A passage connecting two notes
column6: The introduction
column7: The final chorus
column11: A bridge provides melodic, harmonic, and sometimes lyrical contrast. In AABA, the B section is the bridge. In verse-chorus form, the bridge typically appears once (usually after the 2nd chorus) before a final chorus.
column12: Include a comparison to the most commonly confused related concept.
column14: Music Theory for the 21st-Century Classroom (Robert Hutchinson, free online). ABRSM Grade 1-3. musictheory.net quick reference.

column3: What does 'Da Capo' (D.C.) mean?
column4: Go back to the beginning of the piece
column5: Go to the sign (%)
column6: Go to the coda
column7: Slow down
column11: Da Capo' is Italian for 'from the head.' D.C. al Fine means return to the beginning and play until 'Fine' (the end). D.C. al Coda means return to the start and play until the coda symbol, then jump to the coda.
column12: Add a practical hands-on exercise so students can apply this concept with an instrument immediately.
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory (Alfred Music). ABRSM Theory Grade 1-3. musictheory.net — free exercises and reference charts.

column3: What is a 'motif' (motive)?
column4: A short melodic or rhythmic idea that forms the basis for development
column5: A full melody
column6: A chord progression
column7: A complete phrase
column11: A motif is the smallest meaningful musical unit. Beethoven's famous short-short-short-long opening of his 5th Symphony is one of music's most famous motifs. Motifs are developed through repetition, transposition, and transformation.
column12: Include a real-world musical example from a song students already know and love.
column14: Hal Leonard Music Theory Workbook. ABRSM Grade 2-3. Tenuto app (musictheory.net) for drilling and self-assessment.

column3: What is 'binary form'?
column4: A two-part (AB) musical structure
column5: A form using two instruments
column6: A form with a repeat sign
column7: A form with two identical sections
column11: Binary form has two sections (A and B), usually repeated. Many Baroque dances (minuets, gigues) use binary form. 'Rounded binary' has A-B-A' where the second half briefly returns to A material.
column12: Add a visual diagram or notation example to make the abstract concept concrete.
column14: Rockschool Grade 2-4 Theory. Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory. Find this concept in sheet music students are currently practising.

column3: What is 'ternary form'?
column4: A three-part (ABA) structure where the first section returns after a contrasting middle
column5: A form with three verses
column6: A form with three different themes
column7: A form specific to 3/4 meter
column11: Ternary form (ABA) is found in countless classical pieces, songs, and arias. The return of A after contrasting B creates balance and satisfaction. Schubert's songs and classical minuets with trios often use ABA form.
column12: Pair with an ear training component — can students recognize this concept by listening alone?
column14: AB Guide to Music Theory, Part 1 (ABRSM). musictheory.net — exercises and reference. Create an Anki flashcard set for this concept.

column3: What is a 'phrase' in music?
column4: A musical unit comparable to a sentence — typically 4-8 bars with a sense of beginning and end
column5: A single note
column6: A chord progression
column7: A group of notes without a cadence
column11: A musical phrase is a coherent unit ending with a cadence, usually 4-8 bars. Phrases come in antecedent (question) and consequent (answer) pairs — the antecedent ends with a half cadence, the consequent with an authentic cadence.
column12: Include a comparison to the most commonly confused related concept.
column14: Music Theory for the 21st-Century Classroom (Robert Hutchinson, free online). ABRSM Grade 1-3. musictheory.net quick reference.

column3: What is a 'riff'?
column4: A short, repeated melodic or rhythmic figure used as the basis of a piece
column5: An improvised solo
column6: A chord change
column7: A type of cadence
column11: Riffs are short, catchy, repeated patterns fundamental to rock, blues, and jazz. Think of the guitar riff in 'Smoke on the Water' or the piano intro to 'Let It Be.' A riff gives a piece its hook and identity.
column12: Add a practical hands-on exercise so students can apply this concept with an instrument immediately.
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory (Alfred Music). ABRSM Theory Grade 1-3. musictheory.net — free exercises and reference charts.

column3: What is 'sonata form'?
column4: A three-section structure: Exposition (themes presented), Development (themes transformed), Recapitulation (themes return)
column5: A two-part form for sonatas
column6: A 32-bar song form
column7: A form used only in solo pieces
column11: Sonata form (also called first-movement form) is the most important large-scale form in classical music. The exposition introduces contrasting themes, the development manipulates them, and the recapitulation brings them back, resolving tension.
column12: Include a real-world musical example from a song students already know and love.
column14: Hal Leonard Music Theory Workbook. ABRSM Grade 2-3. Tenuto app (musictheory.net) for drilling and self-assessment.

column3: What is a ii-V-I progression?
column4: The core jazz progression: minor 7th → dominant 7th → major 7th
column5: A blues progression
column6: A progression using only 3 chords
column7: A bass line pattern
column11: ii-V-I (e.g. Dm7-G7-Cmaj7 in C major) is the foundation of jazz harmony. The ii prepares the V, which creates tension resolved by I. Every jazz musician must know this progression in all 12 keys.
column12: Pair with a classic recording: recommend a specific jazz album (Kind of Blue, Giant Steps, Maiden Voyage) where this concept is heard clearly.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book (Sher Music). The Real Book, 6th ed. (Hal Leonard). Recommended recording: Miles Davis 'Kind of Blue' — essential first listening.

column3: What is 'swing rhythm'?
column4: Eighth notes played unevenly — long-short, based on a triplet feel
column5: Eighth notes played exactly even
column6: A rhythm in 3/4 time
column7: Playing everything slightly behind the beat
column11: In swing, pairs of eighth notes use a long-short ratio (roughly 2:1, based on triplet subdivision). This 'swinging' eighth-note feel is fundamental to jazz phrasing and distinguishes jazz from straight-feel music.
column12: Include a Real Book reference: find a jazz standard in the Real Book where students can see and play this concept in a real chart.
column14: Jerry Coker, Improvising Jazz (Simon & Schuster). Aebersold Play-Along Vol. 1 'How to Play Jazz and Improvise.' iReal Pro app for backing tracks.

column3: What does 'comping' mean in jazz?
column4: Accompanying a soloist with rhythmically varied chords
column5: Writing a composition
column6: Playing the melody
column7: Counting beats for the ensemble
column11: Comping (short for 'accompanying' or 'complementing') is the art of supportive harmonic accompaniment. A pianist or guitarist comps behind a soloist with rhythmically varied, well-voiced chords that support without overwhelming.
column12: Add a basic play-along: suggest an iReal Pro or Aebersold backing track where students can practice this concept at slow tempos.
column14: David Baker, Jazz Improvisation, A Comprehensive Method (Alfred). Recommended listening: Dave Brubeck 'Take Five,' Charlie Parker 'Yardbird Suite.' iReal Pro.

column3: What is a 'turnaround' in jazz?
column4: A short chord progression at the end of a section that leads back to the beginning (e.g. I-vi-ii-V)
column5: A key change
column6: A modulation to a new key
column7: Returning to a previous tempo
column11: A turnaround smoothly brings a section back to its start. The classic jazz turnaround is I-vi-ii-V7 (e.g. Cmaj7-Am7-Dm7-G7 in C), sending the listener back to the top of the form with harmonic momentum.
column12: Include a vocabulary note: clarify any jazz-specific terminology that might confuse students coming from classical training.
column14: Ted Pease, Jazz Composition Theory and Practice (Berklee Press). The Real Book — find and play examples of this concept in at least 3 different standards.

column3: What is 'the head' in a jazz performance?
column4: The melody of the song, played at the beginning and end
column5: The drummer
column6: The highest note of a chord
column7: The first beat of a measure
column11: In jazz, 'playing the head' means playing the composed melody. A typical jazz performance: head in → solos (improvisation over the chord changes) → head out. The head establishes the form and mood.
column12: Add a listening checklist: give students 2-3 specific things to listen for in a recording to identify this concept by ear.
column14: Berklee Jazz Standards for Piano (Jonathan Feist). iReal Pro chord charts. Recommended YouTube: Jens Larsen — jazz theory explained clearly for beginners.

column3: What is 'modal jazz'?
column4: Jazz based on scales/modes rather than rapid chord changes, pioneered by Miles Davis
column5: Jazz that uses church modes exclusively
column6: Jazz in odd time signatures
column7: Jazz with no improvisation
column11: Miles Davis's 'Kind of Blue' (1959) defined modal jazz. Instead of rapid ii-V-I changes, soloists improvise over sustained modes (e.g. D Dorian for many bars), giving more freedom and a more spacious sound.
column12: Pair with a classic recording: recommend a specific jazz album (Kind of Blue, Giant Steps, Maiden Voyage) where this concept is heard clearly.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book (Sher Music). The Real Book, 6th ed. (Hal Leonard). Recommended recording: Miles Davis 'Kind of Blue' — essential first listening.

column3: What is a 'jazz standard'?
column4: A well-known jazz composition that is part of the common jazz repertoire
column5: A 4/4 time signature
column6: A chord chart format
column7: A major key song
column11: Jazz standards are widely known pieces every jazz musician is expected to know. Examples: 'Autumn Leaves,' 'All the Things You Are,' 'There Will Never Be Another You.' They provide a common language for improvisation sessions.
column12: Include a Real Book reference: find a jazz standard in the Real Book where students can see and play this concept in a real chart.
column14: Jerry Coker, Improvising Jazz (Simon & Schuster). Aebersold Play-Along Vol. 1 'How to Play Jazz and Improvise.' iReal Pro app for backing tracks.

column3: What is a 'lead sheet'?
column4: A notation showing only the melody and chord symbols (also called a 'fake book' entry)
column5: A full orchestral score
column6: A bass line notation
column7: A drum pattern chart
column11: Lead sheets show the essentials — melody in standard notation plus chord symbols above. Jazz musicians use lead sheets (compiled in 'Real Books') to play any tune with their own harmonic interpretation and improvisation.
column12: Add a basic play-along: suggest an iReal Pro or Aebersold backing track where students can practice this concept at slow tempos.
column14: David Baker, Jazz Improvisation, A Comprehensive Method (Alfred). Recommended listening: Dave Brubeck 'Take Five,' Charlie Parker 'Yardbird Suite.' iReal Pro.

column3: What time signature is most common in jazz?
column11: The vast majority of jazz is in 4/4. Swing, bebop, bossa nova, and most jazz standards use 4/4. Jazz in 3/4 ('jazz waltz') and odd meters exist but are less common.
column12: Include a vocabulary note: clarify any jazz-specific terminology that might confuse students coming from classical training.
column14: Ted Pease, Jazz Composition Theory and Practice (Berklee Press). The Real Book — find and play examples of this concept in at least 3 different standards.

column3: What is 'call and response' in jazz?
column4: A musical conversation where a phrase (call) is answered by another phrase (response)
column5: A crescendo followed by a decrescendo
column6: Repeating the same phrase twice
column7: A question mark in music notation
column11: Call and response is fundamental in African-American music — one phrase 'calls' and another 'responds.' In jazz, it can be between soloist and rhythm section, or between melody phrases. It drives the music forward dynamically.
column12: Add a listening checklist: give students 2-3 specific things to listen for in a recording to identify this concept by ear.
column14: Berklee Jazz Standards for Piano (Jonathan Feist). iReal Pro chord charts. Recommended YouTube: Jens Larsen — jazz theory explained clearly for beginners.

column3: What is 'improvisation' in jazz?
column4: Creating and performing music spontaneously in the moment, usually over chord changes
column5: Playing without reading music
column6: Playing a memorized solo
column7: Changing the key during a song
column11: Improvisation is the art of spontaneous musical composition. Jazz soloists improvise melodic lines over the song's chord changes, using scales, arpeggios, motifs, and rhythmic ideas to create a unique performance every time.
column12: Pair with a classic recording: recommend a specific jazz album (Kind of Blue, Giant Steps, Maiden Voyage) where this concept is heard clearly.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book (Sher Music). The Real Book, 6th ed. (Hal Leonard). Recommended recording: Miles Davis 'Kind of Blue' — essential first listening.

column3: What is a 'blues' in jazz?
column4: A 12-bar form using I7, IV7, and V7 chords, often with jazz extensions and substitutions
column5: A sad song
column6: A minor key composition
column7: A type of scale
column11: The jazz blues uses the 12-bar blues form but with jazz harmony — dominant 7th chords on all three degrees (I7, IV7, V7), often enriched with extensions, substitutions, and chromatic chords like the ♭II7 or #IV dim7.
column12: Include a Real Book reference: find a jazz standard in the Real Book where students can see and play this concept in a real chart.
column14: Jerry Coker, Improvising Jazz (Simon & Schuster). Aebersold Play-Along Vol. 1 'How to Play Jazz and Improvise.' iReal Pro app for backing tracks.

column3: How many sharps are in G major?
column11: G major has one sharp: F#. The scale is G A B C D E F# G. Without F#, there would be a whole step between degrees 6 and 7 (E-F) instead of the required half step for a major scale.
column12: Reference the circle of fifths: mark this key's position and show its closest neighbours (dominant and subdominant keys).
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory, Book 2. ABRSM Theory Grade 1-3. musictheory.net — interactive key signature tool. Circle of Fifths poster.

column3: How many flats are in F major?
column11: F major has one flat: B♭. The scale is F G A B♭ C D E F. Without B♭, there would be a whole step between degrees 3 and 4 (A-B) instead of the required half step.
column12: Include a mnemonic: for sharps — Father Charles Goes Down And Ends Battle; for flats — Battle Ends And Down Goes Charles' Father.
column14: Hal Leonard Music Theory Workbook. ABRSM Grade 2-3. Mnemonic: 'Father Charles Goes Down And Ends Battle' (sharps). Staff paper key signature drills.

column3: What key has no sharps or flats?
column4: C major (and A minor)
column5: G major
column6: F major
column7: D major
column11: C major uses only natural notes C D E F G A B, requiring no accidentals. Its relative minor, A natural minor, shares the same key signature. C major is the simplest key to read and is often taught first.
column12: Add a writing exercise: ask students to notate this key signature on a treble and bass clef staff from memory.
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory. Key signature flashcards (Hal Leonard). ABRSM Grade 2-4. Tenuto app — key signature identification exercises.

column3: What is the order of sharps in key signatures?
column4: F# C# G# D# A# E# B#
column5: C# F# G# D# A# B# E#
column6: F# G# A# B# C# D# E#
column7: B# E# A# D# G# C# F#
column11: Sharps follow the fixed order F C G D A E B (remembered as 'Father Charles Goes Down And Ends Battle'). Each new sharp key adds one more sharp from this sequence. G major has F#, D major has F# C#, etc.
column12: Include a scale connection: have students write the complete major scale for this key signature, marking the two half steps.
column14: Rockschool Grade 2-4 Theory. ABRSM Theory of Music. Practice: fill in a blank circle of fifths with key names and accidentals from memory.

column3: What is the order of flats in key signatures?
column4: B♭ E♭ A♭ D♭ G♭ C♭ F♭
column5: E♭ B♭ A♭ D♭ G♭ C♭ F♭
column6: F♭ C♭ G♭ D♭ A♭ E♭ B♭
column7: A♭ B♭ E♭ D♭ G♭ C♭ F♭
column11: Flats follow B E A D G C F (reverse of sharps, remembered as 'Battle Ends And Down Goes Charles' Father'). Each new flat key adds one more flat: F major has B♭, B♭ major has B♭ E♭, E♭ major has B♭ E♭ A♭.
column12: Add a relative minor link: identify the relative minor key and explain how to find it (6th degree of the major scale).
column14: Music Theory Workbook for All Instruments (Mark Sarnecki). ABRSM Grade 3. Practice: identify the key signature in an excerpt before analyzing the melody.

column3: How do you identify a major key from its sharps?
column4: The last sharp is the 7th degree — go up a half step to find the tonic
column5: Count the sharps and go to that scale degree
column6: The first sharp names the key
column7: Add up all the sharps
column11: The last sharp in a key signature is the leading tone (7th degree). Go up one half step to find the tonic. Example: last sharp is F# → up a half step = G → the key is G major. This works for all sharp keys.
column12: Reference the circle of fifths: mark this key's position and show its closest neighbours (dominant and subdominant keys).
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory, Book 2. ABRSM Theory Grade 1-3. musictheory.net — interactive key signature tool. Circle of Fifths poster.

column3: How do you identify a major key from its flats?
column4: The second-to-last flat names the major key (or F for one flat)
column5: Count the flats and go to that degree
column6: The last flat names the key
column7: The first flat names the key
column11: For flat keys, the second-to-last flat gives the key name. With 3 flats (B♭ E♭ A♭), the second-to-last is E♭ → key is E♭ major. Special case: F major has only 1 flat (B♭), so you just memorize it.
column12: Include a mnemonic: for sharps — Father Charles Goes Down And Ends Battle; for flats — Battle Ends And Down Goes Charles' Father.
column14: Hal Leonard Music Theory Workbook. ABRSM Grade 2-3. Mnemonic: 'Father Charles Goes Down And Ends Battle' (sharps). Staff paper key signature drills.

column3: What is the relative minor of G major?
column4: E minor
column5: D minor
column6: B minor
column7: A minor
column11: The relative minor starts on the 6th degree of its major key. The 6th degree of G major is E, so E minor is the relative minor of G major. Both share one sharp (F#) in their key signature.
column12: Add a writing exercise: ask students to notate this key signature on a treble and bass clef staff from memory.
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory. Key signature flashcards (Hal Leonard). ABRSM Grade 2-4. Tenuto app — key signature identification exercises.

column3: What are 'enharmonic keys'?
column4: Two keys that sound the same but are written differently (e.g. F# major and G♭ major)
column5: Keys that share the same tonic
column6: Keys a tritone apart
column7: Keys with opposite quality
column11: Enharmonic keys are like enharmonic notes — same pitch, different notation. F# major (6 sharps) and G♭ major (6 flats) sound identical. Which you use depends on musical context and convenience.
column12: Include a scale connection: have students write the complete major scale for this key signature, marking the two half steps.
column14: Rockschool Grade 2-4 Theory. ABRSM Theory of Music. Practice: fill in a blank circle of fifths with key names and accidentals from memory.

column3: How many keys are in the circle of fifths?
column4: 12 major + 12 minor (with 3 enharmonic pairs at the bottom)
column11: There are 12 major keys and 12 minor keys, arranged in a circle where adjacent keys differ by one sharp or flat. Moving clockwise adds a sharp; counterclockwise adds a flat. F#/G♭, C#/D♭, and B/C♭ are enharmonic pairs.
column12: Add a relative minor link: identify the relative minor key and explain how to find it (6th degree of the major scale).
column14: Music Theory Workbook for All Instruments (Mark Sarnecki). ABRSM Grade 3. Practice: identify the key signature in an excerpt before analyzing the melody.

column3: What is a 'parallel key'?
column4: A major and minor key sharing the same tonic (e.g. C major and C minor)
column5: Two keys sharing the same key signature
column6: Keys a perfect 5th apart
column7: Two keys on the same instrument string
column11: Parallel keys share the same tonic but different key signatures. C major (no flats) and C minor (3 flats: B♭ E♭ A♭) are parallel. Composers frequently borrow chords between parallel keys for expressive color.
column12: Reference the circle of fifths: mark this key's position and show its closest neighbours (dominant and subdominant keys).
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory, Book 2. ABRSM Theory Grade 1-3. musictheory.net — interactive key signature tool. Circle of Fifths poster.

column3: How many sharps does D major have?
column4: 2 (F# and C#)
column11: D major has two sharps: F# and C#. The scale is D E F# G A B C# D. Both sharps are needed to maintain the W-W-H-W-W-W-H pattern, with the C# acting as the leading tone to D.
column12: Include a mnemonic: for sharps — Father Charles Goes Down And Ends Battle; for flats — Battle Ends And Down Goes Charles' Father.
column14: Hal Leonard Music Theory Workbook. ABRSM Grade 2-3. Mnemonic: 'Father Charles Goes Down And Ends Battle' (sharps). Staff paper key signature drills.

column3: How many flats does E♭ major have?
column11: E♭ major has three flats: B♭, E♭, and A♭. The scale is E♭ F G A♭ B♭ C D E♭. E♭ major is a very common key in jazz and is the home key for many brass instruments.
column12: Add a writing exercise: ask students to notate this key signature on a treble and bass clef staff from memory.
column14: Alfred's Essentials of Music Theory. Key signature flashcards (Hal Leonard). ABRSM Grade 2-4. Tenuto app — key signature identification exercises.

column3: What characteristic note distinguishes Dorian mode from natural minor?
column4: A natural 6th (major 6th)
column5: A raised 7th
column11: Dorian is like natural minor but with a raised (natural) 6th. D Dorian = D E F G A B C D. The natural 6th gives Dorian a brighter, more sophisticated sound than pure Aeolian. It's the basis of much jazz and modal music.
column12: Add an improvisation task: give a drone backing track and have students create a 4-bar phrase using only these scale tones.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book (Sher Music) — Ch. 1-3 on scales and modes. ABRSM Grade 5-6. iReal Pro app for modal backing tracks in all keys.

column3: What is the Lydian mode?
column4: The 4th mode of the major scale — like major but with a raised 4th (#4/♯11)
column5: The 4th mode with a lowered 7th
column6: A minor mode with a raised 6th
column7: The major scale played from the 5th degree
column11: Lydian is built from the 4th degree of a major scale (e.g. F Lydian = F G A B C D E F). The raised 4th (#4) gives it a bright, floating, 'otherworldly' quality. It's the brightest of all modes and is used in film music, jazz, and fusion.
column12: Include a genre connection: name two musical styles that commonly use this scale/mode and provide a listening example for each.
column14: Jerry Coker, Improvising Jazz (Touchstone). ABRSM Grade 4-5. Recommended listening: Miles Davis 'Kind of Blue' for modal scale application in practice.

column3: What is the Mixolydian mode?
column4: The 5th mode of the major scale — like major but with a ♭7
column5: The major scale with a raised 5th
column6: The 5th mode with a ♭3 and ♭7
column7: The Dorian mode transposed to the 5th
column11: Mixolydian is major with a ♭7 (e.g. G Mixolydian = G A B C D E F G). It's the scale of the dominant 7th chord and is essential in blues, rock, and folk. It sounds major but with a bluesy edge due to the ♭7.
column12: Add a comparison question: list the specific degrees altered vs. the major scale and describe the mood change this produces.
column14: Ted Pease, Jazz Composition (Berklee Press). Modes in compositional context. ABRSM Grade 5. Aebersold Vol. 3 — The ii-V-I Progression for practice.

column3: What makes the Phrygian mode distinctive?
column4: A ♭2 (minor 2nd above the tonic) — it starts with a half step
column5: A raised 4th
column6: A ♭6 and ♭7 but brighter than Aeolian
column7: A raised 6th like Dorian
column11: Phrygian (3rd mode of major, e.g. E F G A B C D E) starts with a half step from the tonic. The ♭2 gives it a Spanish, flamenco, or dark/exotic character. It's the darkest of the major-derived modes.
column12: Include a chord pairing: identify which chord type this scale is most naturally paired with in jazz or pop.
column14: David Baker, Jazz Improvisation (Frangipani Press). Scale-chord relationships. Recommended: Paul Davids YouTube — modal scales with guitar context.

column3: What is the Locrian mode and why is it rarely used as a tonal center?
column4: The 7th mode of major — it has a ♭2 and ♭5, making the tonic chord diminished and unstable
column5: It has a major 3rd that clashes with the key
column6: It is identical to the chromatic scale
column7: It lacks a perfect 4th
column11: Locrian (7th mode of major, e.g. B C D E F G A B) has a ♭2 and ♭5. The diminished chord on the tonic is too unstable to establish a real home base, making true Locrian pieces extremely rare. It does appear as a chord-scale over m7♭5 chords.
column12: Add a composition task: write a 4-bar melody using this scale over a simple I-IV-V backing in two different keys.
column14: Berklee Music Theory Books 1-2 (Paul Schmeling). ABRSM Grade 5. iReal Pro — modal play-along tracks in all 12 keys and multiple tempos.

column3: What are the modes of the major scale in order?
column4: Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, Locrian
column5: Ionian, Lydian, Phrygian, Dorian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, Locrian
column6: Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Ionian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, Locrian
column7: Ionian, Dorian, Lydian, Phrygian, Aeolian, Mixolydian, Locrian
column11: The 7 modes in order: Ionian (1st), Dorian (2nd), Phrygian (3rd), Lydian (4th), Mixolydian (5th), Aeolian (6th), Locrian (7th). Mnemonic: 'I Don't Particularly Like Modes A Lot.'
column12: Add an improvisation task: give a drone backing track and have students create a 4-bar phrase using only these scale tones.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book (Sher Music) — Ch. 1-3 on scales and modes. ABRSM Grade 5-6. iReal Pro app for modal backing tracks in all keys.

column3: What is the Lydian Dominant scale?
column4: The 4th mode of melodic minor — Lydian with a ♭7 (raised 4th + lowered 7th)
column5: Lydian mode with an added ♭3
column6: A Mixolydian scale with a #4
column7: The whole tone scale starting on the 4th degree
column11: Lydian Dominant (4th mode of melodic minor, e.g. F G A B C D E♭) combines the raised 4th of Lydian with the ♭7 of Mixolydian. It's used over dominant 7th chords with a #11 (♯4) extension — very common in jazz reharmonization.
column12: Include a genre connection: name two musical styles that commonly use this scale/mode and provide a listening example for each.
column14: Jerry Coker, Improvising Jazz (Touchstone). ABRSM Grade 4-5. Recommended listening: Miles Davis 'Kind of Blue' for modal scale application in practice.

column3: What is the diminished (octatonic) scale?
column4: An 8-note scale alternating whole and half steps, symmetric at every minor 3rd
column5: A scale with only diminished intervals
column6: A 7-note scale with a ♭5
column7: A scale built entirely of minor 3rds
column11: The diminished scale alternates W-H (or H-W) for 8 notes. Its symmetry means it repeats every minor 3rd, so only 3 unique diminished scales exist. Used over diminished 7th chords and dominant 7♭9 chords in jazz and classical music.
column12: Add a comparison question: list the specific degrees altered vs. the major scale and describe the mood change this produces.
column14: Ted Pease, Jazz Composition (Berklee Press). Modes in compositional context. ABRSM Grade 5. Aebersold Vol. 3 — The ii-V-I Progression for practice.

column3: What is the difference between jazz melodic minor and classical melodic minor?
column4: Jazz melodic minor uses raised 6th and 7th in both directions; classical reverts to natural minor descending
column5: Jazz only raises the 7th; classical raises both 6th and 7th
column6: They are identical
column7: Classical melodic minor raises only the 6th
column11: Classical melodic minor ascends with ♯6 & ♯7, descends as natural minor. Jazz melodic minor (real melodic minor) uses ♯6 & ♯7 in both directions. All melodic minor modes — including the altered scale — derive from the jazz version.
column12: Include a chord pairing: identify which chord type this scale is most naturally paired with in jazz or pop.
column14: David Baker, Jazz Improvisation (Frangipani Press). Scale-chord relationships. Recommended: Paul Davids YouTube — modal scales with guitar context.

column3: What is the pentatonic minor scale?
column4: root ♭3 4 5 ♭7 — omits the 2nd and 6th of natural minor
column5: root 2 3 5 6 — same as major pentatonic
column6: root ♭3 ♭5 5 ♭7 — the blues scale
column7: root 2 ♭3 5 ♭7 — Dorian without the 4th
column11: Minor pentatonic (root ♭3 4 5 ♭7) omits the 2nd and 6th of natural minor. It's a relative of the major pentatonic — C major pentatonic (C D E G A) and A minor pentatonic (A C D E G) share the same 5 pitches.
column12: Add a composition task: write a 4-bar melody using this scale over a simple I-IV-V backing in two different keys.
column14: Berklee Music Theory Books 1-2 (Paul Schmeling). ABRSM Grade 5. iReal Pro — modal play-along tracks in all 12 keys and multiple tempos.

column3: What is the 'altered scale' (super Locrian)?
column4: The 7th mode of melodic minor — contains ♭2 ♭3 ♭4 ♭5 ♭6 ♭7 (every non-tonic note altered)
column5: Locrian with a raised 4th
column6: A diminished scale starting on the 7th degree
column7: A whole-tone scale with a leading tone
column11: The altered scale = 7th mode of melodic minor (e.g. G altered = G A♭ B♭ C♭ D♭ E♭ F). It contains every alteration: ♭9, #9, #11, ♭13 — all tensions available over a G7alt chord. Used for maximum tension before resolving to tonic.
column12: Add an improvisation task: give a drone backing track and have students create a 4-bar phrase using only these scale tones.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book (Sher Music) — Ch. 1-3 on scales and modes. ABRSM Grade 5-6. iReal Pro app for modal backing tracks in all keys.

column3: What is the 'Dorian ♭2' scale?
column4: The 2nd mode of melodic minor — Dorian with a ♭2
column5: Natural minor with a raised 6th
column6: Phrygian with a raised 6th
column7: Dorian starting on the 2nd degree
column11: Dorian ♭2 (2nd mode of melodic minor) has a minor 2nd above the root but a natural 6th. For example: D E♭ F G A B C D. It combines a dark Phrygian opening with a brighter Dorian upper register. Used over m7 chords in minor ii-V contexts.
column12: Include a genre connection: name two musical styles that commonly use this scale/mode and provide a listening example for each.
column14: Jerry Coker, Improvising Jazz (Touchstone). ABRSM Grade 4-5. Recommended listening: Miles Davis 'Kind of Blue' for modal scale application in practice.

column3: What is the 'Lydian Augmented' scale?
column4: The 3rd mode of melodic minor — Lydian with a raised 5th (#5) as well as #4
column5: Lydian mode with an added 9th
column6: The whole tone scale
column7: Augmented scale transposed to Lydian
column11: Lydian Augmented is the 3rd mode of melodic minor (e.g. E♭ F G A B C D). It has both a raised 4th AND raised 5th, creating a very bright, ambiguous, almost floating sound. Used over maj7#5 chords.
column12: Add a comparison question: list the specific degrees altered vs. the major scale and describe the mood change this produces.
column14: Ted Pease, Jazz Composition (Berklee Press). Modes in compositional context. ABRSM Grade 5. Aebersold Vol. 3 — The ii-V-I Progression for practice.

column3: What is a compound major 9th and how many semitones does it span?
column4: A major 2nd extended beyond an octave — 14 semitones (12 + 2)
column5: A chord with a major 9th — 12 semitones
column6: An interval of exactly 2 octaves — 24 semitones
column7: A major 7th plus a minor 2nd — 12 semitones
column11: A major 9th = octave (12) + major 2nd (2) = 14 semitones. Compound intervals are named by adding 7 to the simple interval number (2nd + 7 = 9th). They appear frequently in jazz extended chords like maj9, dom9, and m9.
column12: Add a harmonic context question: how does this interval function within a dominant 7th chord and within a major 7th chord?
column14: Steven Laitz, The Complete Musician, 4th ed. (Oxford). Stefan Kostka & Dorothy Payne, Tonal Harmony (McGraw-Hill). ABRSM Grade 5-6.

column3: How does the tritone in G7 resolve when moving to Cmaj7?
column4: F moves down a half step to E; B moves up a half step to C — the tritone contracts to a major 3rd
column5: Both notes move down by a step
column6: The tritone expands outward to a perfect 5th
column7: One note stays, the other moves an octave
column11: In G7 (B-D-F), the tritone F-B is the engine of resolution. B (leading tone) rises a half step to C; F (chord 7th) falls a half step to E — the tritone contracts to a major 3rd (E-C). This smooth voice leading is the basis of all dominant-to-tonic resolution.
column12: Include an inversion exercise: state the inversion of this interval and confirm using the rule (interval numbers sum to 9).
column14: EarMaster 7 — interval ear training with melodic and harmonic modes. Paul Hindemith, Elementary Training for Musicians. ABRSM Grade 5.

column3: How many semitones are in a minor 6th?
column11: A minor 6th spans 8 semitones (e.g. C to A♭). It's the inversion of a major 3rd (4 semitones): 4 + 8 = 12. Minor 6ths have a warm, bittersweet sound and appear frequently in Romantic melodies and jazz ballads.
column12: Add a voice leading application: in which direction does this interval typically resolve in a tonal or jazz progression?
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book, Ch. 1 — intervals in jazz chord construction. Recommended listening: Coltrane 'Giant Steps' for major 3rd relationships.

column3: What is an 'enharmonic interval'?
column4: Two intervals that sound the same but are spelled differently (e.g. augmented 4th and diminished 5th)
column5: Two intervals that sound completely different
column6: An interval that can only be heard, not written
column7: An interval that inverts to itself
column11: Enharmonic intervals are spelled differently but are acoustically identical. The tritone is the classic example: augmented 4th (C to F#) = diminished 5th (C to G♭) = 6 semitones. Their spelling determines how they resolve in voice leading.
column12: Include an enharmonic spelling exercise: can this interval be spelled differently? Describe how the function changes with context.
column14: Aldwell & Schachter, Harmony and Voice Leading, 4th ed. Interval inversion chart practice. ABRSM Grade 6-7 interval recognition.

column3: What is the 'rule of 9' for interval inversion?
column4: Interval number + its inversion = 9 (e.g. 3rd + 6th = 9, 4th + 5th = 9)
column5: Interval number + its inversion = 12
column6: Multiply semitones by 9
