column7: Invert by subtracting from an octave in semitones
column11: For simple intervals: original number + inverted number = 9. Quality also inverts: major↔minor, augmented↔diminished, perfect↔perfect. A major 6th (M6) inverts to a minor 3rd (m3). This rule applies to all diatonic intervals.
column12: Add a chord-tone identification task: find this interval between two chord tones in each of Cmaj7, Dm7, and G7.
column14: Berklee Music Theory Book 1. Interval relationships to chord quality. Practice: identify intervals in recordings before checking the score.

column3: How many semitones are in a major 6th?
column11: A major 6th spans 9 semitones (e.g. C to A). It inverts to a minor 3rd (3 semitones): 9 + 3 = 12. Major 6ths have a bright, open sound. The maj6 interval is the top 2 notes of a major triad in second inversion.
column12: Add a harmonic context question: how does this interval function within a dominant 7th chord and within a major 7th chord?
column14: Steven Laitz, The Complete Musician, 4th ed. (Oxford). Stefan Kostka & Dorothy Payne, Tonal Harmony (McGraw-Hill). ABRSM Grade 5-6.

column3: What interval is formed between the 3rd and 7th of a dominant 7th chord?
column4: A tritone
column5: A perfect 5th
column6: A major 6th
column7: A minor 3rd
column11: In G7 (G B D F), the 3rd (B) and 7th (F) form a tritone (6 semitones) — the interval of instability that drives resolution. B wants to move up to C; F wants to move down to E. This is what makes V7→I so harmonically powerful.
column12: Include an inversion exercise: state the inversion of this interval and confirm using the rule (interval numbers sum to 9).
column14: EarMaster 7 — interval ear training with melodic and harmonic modes. Paul Hindemith, Elementary Training for Musicians. ABRSM Grade 5.

column3: What is the difference between major 7th and minor 7th intervals?
column4: Major 7th = 11 semitones; minor 7th = 10 semitones
column5: They are enharmonically the same
column6: Major 7th = 10 semitones; minor 7th = 9 semitones
column7: Major 7th spans an octave and a 7th
column11: The major 7th (C to B) spans 11 semitones — one half step below the octave. The minor 7th (C to B♭) spans 10 semitones. This one-semitone difference determines whether a chord is maj7 (warm/jazz) or dominant 7 (tense/bluesy).
column12: Add a voice leading application: in which direction does this interval typically resolve in a tonal or jazz progression?
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book, Ch. 1 — intervals in jazz chord construction. Recommended listening: Coltrane 'Giant Steps' for major 3rd relationships.

column3: How many semitones are in an augmented 5th?
column11: A perfect 5th = 7 semitones. Augmented means raised by one semitone, so augmented 5th = 8 semitones (e.g. C to G#). This interval appears in augmented triads and is enharmonically equivalent to a minor 6th.
column12: Include an enharmonic spelling exercise: can this interval be spelled differently? Describe how the function changes with context.
column14: Aldwell & Schachter, Harmony and Voice Leading, 4th ed. Interval inversion chart practice. ABRSM Grade 6-7 interval recognition.

column3: What is a 'diminished 7th' interval?
column4: 9 semitones — a minor 7th reduced by one half step (enharmonic to a major 6th)
column5: 10 semitones
column6: A tritone plus a minor 3rd
column7: 7 semitones
column11: A diminished 7th (d7) spans 9 semitones, enharmonically equivalent to a major 6th. Spelled as a 7th because of its theoretical context — in Cdim7 = C E♭ G♭ B♭♭, the B♭♭ is the diminished 7th. The symmetry of dim7 means every note can be reinterpreted as the root.
column12: Add a chord-tone identification task: find this interval between two chord tones in each of Cmaj7, Dm7, and G7.
column14: Berklee Music Theory Book 1. Interval relationships to chord quality. Practice: identify intervals in recordings before checking the score.

column3: What is an 'augmented 6th' interval?
column4: 10 semitones — one semitone larger than a major 6th (enharmonic to a minor 7th)
column5: 9 semitones
column6: A 6th with an added chromatic neighbor
column7: An interval from the augmented scale
column11: The augmented 6th (A6) spans 10 semitones, enharmonically a minor 7th but spelled differently. It appears in augmented 6th chords (Italian, French, German) where it resolves outward by half steps to a perfect octave on the dominant — a powerful pre-dominant gesture.
column12: Add a harmonic context question: how does this interval function within a dominant 7th chord and within a major 7th chord?
column14: Steven Laitz, The Complete Musician, 4th ed. (Oxford). Stefan Kostka & Dorothy Payne, Tonal Harmony (McGraw-Hill). ABRSM Grade 5-6.

column3: What is a 'unison' vs. an 'octave'?
column4: Unison = same pitch (0 semitones); octave = same pitch class but 12 semitones higher or lower
column5: They are the same thing
column6: Unison = 12 semitones; octave = 0 semitones
column7: Unison is a chord; octave is an interval
column11: A perfect unison (P1) = 0 semitones — both voices on the exact same pitch. A perfect octave (P8) = 12 semitones — same pitch class, one octave apart. Both are 'perfect' intervals because they appear in both major and minor scales.
column12: Include an inversion exercise: state the inversion of this interval and confirm using the rule (interval numbers sum to 9).
column14: EarMaster 7 — interval ear training with melodic and harmonic modes. Paul Hindemith, Elementary Training for Musicians. ABRSM Grade 5.

column3: What is a fully diminished 7th chord?
column4: A chord built of three stacked minor 3rds: root, ♭3, ♭5, ♭♭7 (diminished 7th) — e.g. Cdim7 = C E♭ G♭ A
column5: A minor chord with a ♭7
column6: A chord with a minor 3rd and diminished 5th only
column7: A dominant 7th chord without the 5th
column11: Cdim7 = C E♭ G♭ A (enharmonic to B♭♭). Four notes, each a minor 3rd apart. It's symmetrical — every note can act as the root, giving 4 possible interpretations. Found on the 7th degree of harmonic minor and used for chromatic passing motion.
column12: Add a voice leading exercise: write the smoothest connection from this chord to Cmaj7 using inversions and common tones.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book, Ch. 4-8 (chord theory). Ted Greene, Chord Chemistry (Alfred). ABRSM Grade 5-6. iReal Pro for practicing chord progressions.

column3: What is the difference between a half-diminished (m7♭5) and a fully diminished 7th chord?
column4: Half-diminished has a minor 7th (♭7); fully diminished has a diminished 7th (♭♭7 = enharmonic major 6th)
column5: Half-diminished has a major 3rd; fully diminished has a minor 3rd
column6: They are enharmonically identical in all contexts
column7: Half-diminished is 4 notes; fully diminished is 3 notes
column11: Both share root, ♭3, and ♭5. The 7th differs: m7♭5 (half-dim) = minor 7th (10 semitones); dim7 (fully dim) = diminished 7th (9 semitones). In C: Cm7♭5 = C E♭ G♭ B♭ vs. Cdim7 = C E♭ G♭ A.
column12: Include a functional analysis: identify which scale degree this chord lives on and what harmonic function it serves in major and minor.
column14: Jerry Coker, Jazz Keyboard Harmony (Studio P/R). Ted Pease, Jazz Composition (Berklee). ABRSM Grade 5. Recommended: Herbie Hancock 'Maiden Voyage' for voicings.

column3: What is a 'major 9th' chord (e.g. Cmaj9)?
column4: A major 7th chord with an added 9th: C E G B D
column5: A major triad with only the 9th (no 7th)
column6: A dominant 9th chord
column7: The root raised by a 9th
column11: Cmaj9 = C E G B D. It's a Cmaj7 with the 9th (D) stacked on top. Extended chords build by 3rds: root-3rd-5th-7th-9th-11th-13th. The 9th adds lush color without changing the chord's tonic major function.
column12: Add a substitution note: identify which chord type can substitute for this one due to shared tritone or guide tones.
column14: Berklee Music Theory Book 2 (Paul Schmeling). Chord substitution and voice leading. ABRSM Grade 5. iReal Pro — progressions with various chord qualities.

column3: What is a 'dominant 7#9' chord (the 'Hendrix chord')?
column4: A dominant 7th with a raised 9th — contains both major 3rd and implied minor 3rd (♭10) in the same chord
column5: A dominant 7th with a natural major 9th
column6: A minor 7th with an augmented 9th
column7: A major chord with ♯9 replacing the 5th
column11: G7#9 = G B D F A# (enharmonically A#=B♭). The ♯9 sounds like a ♭3 an octave higher, so the chord contains both a major 3rd (B) and implied minor 3rd (B♭/A#). This ambiguity creates the iconic 'Hendrix sound' — blues and rock in one chord.
column12: Include a jazz example: name a jazz standard where this chord type creates a memorable harmonic color or pivot point.
column14: Adam Neely YouTube: 'Why jazz chord voicings work.' Mark Levine, The Jazz Piano Book (Sher). ABRSM Grade 5-6. Hookpad for chord analysis.

column3: What are 'chord voicings'?
column4: The specific arrangement and spacing of notes in a chord — same chord, different registers and inversions
column5: The dynamic level of chord playing
column6: Different chord qualities
column7: The inversions of a chord only
column11: Voicing determines how a chord sounds by choosing which octave each note occupies. Close voicings pack notes tightly; open voicings spread them out. Jazz pianists use 'shell voicings' (root + 3rd + 7th) for clarity, then add color tones (9th, 13th) on top.
column12: Add a notation exercise: write this chord in root position and both inversions, then label with figured bass symbols.
column14: Stefan Kostka, Tonal Harmony Ch. 12-15 (extended chords). ABRSM Grade 5. Analyze Bill Evans recordings for voicing construction examples.

column3: What is a 'quartal chord'?
column4: A chord built by stacking perfect 4ths instead of 3rds (e.g. C-F-B♭-E♭)
column5: A chord with 4 notes in root position
column6: A chord built on the 4th scale degree
column7: A diminished chord in second inversion
column11: Quartal harmony stacks 4ths rather than 3rds, creating an ambiguous, modern sound. For example: C-F-B♭-E♭. Favored by McCoy Tyner, Herbie Hancock, and McCoy Tyner. Quartal chords often don't sound clearly major or minor, making them harmonically flexible.
column12: Add a voice leading exercise: write the smoothest connection from this chord to Cmaj7 using inversions and common tones.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book, Ch. 4-8 (chord theory). Ted Greene, Chord Chemistry (Alfred). ABRSM Grade 5-6. iReal Pro for practicing chord progressions.

column3: What is a 'sus2' chord?
column4: A chord where the 3rd is replaced by a major 2nd (e.g. Csus2 = C D G)
column5: A chord suspended on beat 2
column6: A chord with added 2nd alongside the 3rd
column7: A chord where the 5th is raised to a 6th
column11: Sus2 replaces the 3rd with the 2nd above the root (e.g. Csus2 = C D G). Like sus4, it avoids a clear major/minor quality. Note: Csus2 and Gsus4/C contain the same notes (C D G), making them enharmonically related inversions.
column12: Include a functional analysis: identify which scale degree this chord lives on and what harmonic function it serves in major and minor.
column14: Jerry Coker, Jazz Keyboard Harmony (Studio P/R). Ted Pease, Jazz Composition (Berklee). ABRSM Grade 5. Recommended: Herbie Hancock 'Maiden Voyage' for voicings.

column3: What is a 'dominant 7♭9' chord and where does it resolve best?
column4: A dominant 7th with a ♭9 (e.g. G7♭9 = G B D F A♭) — resolves most naturally to a minor tonic
column5: A dominant 7th that resolves only to major
column6: A minor 7th with a flat 9
column7: A diminished chord with dominant function
column11: G7♭9 = G B D F A♭. The ♭9 (A♭) adds chromatic pull and resolves best to Gm or Cm. The chord contains a diminished 7th (B-A♭) as its characteristic interval. Used extensively in minor ii-V-i progressions in jazz.
column12: Add a substitution note: identify which chord type can substitute for this one due to shared tritone or guide tones.
column14: Berklee Music Theory Book 2 (Paul Schmeling). Chord substitution and voice leading. ABRSM Grade 5. iReal Pro — progressions with various chord qualities.

column3: What is a 'major 6th' chord (e.g. C6) and what chord does it share notes with?
column4: A major triad with an added 6th: C E G A — shares all notes with Am7 (A C E G)
column5: A major chord with the 5th raised to a 6th
column6: A major 7th chord enharmonic to a 6th chord
column7: An inverted dominant 7th chord
column11: C6 = C E G A. Interestingly, this is the same 4 notes as Am7 (A C E G) — they're mutual inversions. Which chord you hear depends on the bass note and harmonic context. C6 functions as tonic; Am7 functions as the vi or ii chord.
column12: Include a jazz example: name a jazz standard where this chord type creates a memorable harmonic color or pivot point.
column14: Adam Neely YouTube: 'Why jazz chord voicings work.' Mark Levine, The Jazz Piano Book (Sher). ABRSM Grade 5-6. Hookpad for chord analysis.

column3: What is a tritone substitution chord (♭II7)?
column4: A dominant 7th chord built on the tritone (♭II) of V — e.g. D♭7 substituting for G7 in C major
column5: A minor chord on the ♭II degree
column6: A half-diminished chord on ♭II
column7: A chord borrowed from the Lydian mode
column11: D♭7 and G7 share the same tritone: C♭ (=B) and F. This means D♭7 has the same resolution tendencies as G7 but with a chromatic half-step bass descent (D♭→C). Tritone subs create smooth voice leading and harmonic variety in jazz reharmonization.
column12: Add a notation exercise: write this chord in root position and both inversions, then label with figured bass symbols.
column14: Stefan Kostka, Tonal Harmony Ch. 12-15 (extended chords). ABRSM Grade 5. Analyze Bill Evans recordings for voicing construction examples.

column3: What is a 'polychord'?
column4: Two separate chords stacked on top of each other (e.g. E major over C major triad)
column5: A chord played by multiple instruments
column6: A chord using notes from two different scales
column7: A cluster chord of adjacent notes
column11: A polychord combines two complete chords. E.g. E/C = E major triad over C major = C E G + E G# B, creating a Cmaj7#11 type sound. Polychords are used in jazz, 20th century classical (Stravinsky, Bartók), and film music for complex harmonic textures.
column12: Add a voice leading exercise: write the smoothest connection from this chord to Cmaj7 using inversions and common tones.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book, Ch. 4-8 (chord theory). Ted Greene, Chord Chemistry (Alfred). ABRSM Grade 5-6. iReal Pro for practicing chord progressions.

column3: What is the difference between Cm7 and CmMaj7?
column4: CmMaj7 has a major 7th (C E♭ G B natural); Cm7 has a minor 7th (C E♭ G B♭)
column5: CmMaj7 has a major 3rd; Cm7 has a minor 3rd
column6: They are the same chord
column7: CmMaj7 is a major chord with minor quality
column11: CmMaj7 = C E♭ G B natural. Cm7 = C E♭ G B♭. CmMaj7 has a striking, dramatic quality — the minor triad combined with a major 7th creates an unexpected brightness. It appears naturally as the tonic chord in harmonic and jazz melodic minor.
column12: Include a functional analysis: identify which scale degree this chord lives on and what harmonic function it serves in major and minor.
column14: Jerry Coker, Jazz Keyboard Harmony (Studio P/R). Ted Pease, Jazz Composition (Berklee). ABRSM Grade 5. Recommended: Herbie Hancock 'Maiden Voyage' for voicings.

column3: What is a 'dominant 13th' chord?
column4: A dominant 7th with extensions up to the 13th (typically voiced as root-3rd-7th-13th minimum)
column5: A major chord with a 13th added
column6: A chord containing all 13 diatonic notes
column7: A dominant 7th in 13th position on the neck
column11: G13 theoretically = G B D F A C E (all scale tones stacked). In practice pianists use root-3rd-♭7-13th (G B F E), omitting the 5th and often the 11th (which clashes with the 3rd). Very common in jazz and R&B as a sophisticated dominant chord.
column12: Add a substitution note: identify which chord type can substitute for this one due to shared tritone or guide tones.
column14: Berklee Music Theory Book 2 (Paul Schmeling). Chord substitution and voice leading. ABRSM Grade 5. iReal Pro — progressions with various chord qualities.

column3: What is a 'secondary dominant'?
column4: A V7 chord applied to a chord other than I — temporarily tonicizing it (e.g. V/V = D7 in C major)
column5: The dominant chord in the secondary key
column6: A chord borrowed from the parallel minor
column7: The ii chord acting as a pre-dominant
column11: Secondary dominants create local tonicization. In C major: V/V = D7 (leads to G), V/ii = A7 (leads to Dm), V/vi = E7 (leads to Am). They add chromatic color and harmonic momentum by briefly making any diatonic chord feel like a temporary 'I'.
column12: Include a jazz standard reference: identify two jazz standards where this harmonic concept appears and analyze the specific bars.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book, Ch. 10-14 (harmonic function). Stefan Kostka, Tonal Harmony Ch. 18-22. ABRSM Grade 6.

column3: What is 'modal interchange' (modal mixture)?
column4: Borrowing chords from the parallel minor (or other modes) to enrich major key harmony
column5: Changing from one mode to another mid-song
column6: Using two different keys simultaneously
column7: Reharmonizing with modes instead of chords
column11: Modal interchange borrows chords from parallel modes. In C major, common borrowed chords from C minor: ♭VII (B♭maj), ♭VI (A♭maj), iv (Fm), ♭III (E♭maj). These create expressive harmonic darkening in an otherwise major context.
column12: Add a reharmonization task: replace one chord in a I-vi-ii-V with this concept and describe the difference in feeling.
column14: Jerry Coker, Improvising Jazz. ABRSM Grade 5-6. Recommended: analyze the harmony of 'Autumn Leaves' (jazz standard) bar by bar for function.

column3: What is a 'pivot chord modulation'?
column4: Using a chord diatonic to both the original and target keys to smoothly transition between keys
column5: A sudden, unprepared key change
column6: A modulation using only the cycle of 5ths
column7: Repeating a chord until the new key feels established
column11: Pivot chord modulation finds a chord that functions in both keys. E.g. modulating C major → G major: the chord Am7 is vi in C major and ii in G major — it acts as the pivot. After the pivot, you resolve it in the new key's context.
column12: Include a voice-leading analysis: trace the movement of the soprano and bass through this progression step by step.
column14: Berklee Online: Harmony 3-4 (harmonic function, voice leading). Ted Pease, Jazz Composition Ch. 1-5. ABRSM Grade 6. iReal Pro for harmonic practice.

column3: Why are parallel perfect 5ths traditionally avoided in voice leading?
column4: They cause two voices to lose independence and sound like one merged voice
column5: They create too much harmonic tension
column6: They are physically impossible on the piano
column7: They violate the rules of time signatures
column11: Parallel perfect 5ths were banned in common practice counterpoint because they fuse two voices acoustically. Medieval polyphony sought independent lines; parallel 5ths undermine that. Modern music uses them freely — rock power chords, Debussy's parallelism, and organum all use parallel 5ths intentionally.
column12: Add a comparison question: contrast this cadence or concept with the similar one students most commonly confuse it with.
column14: Heinrich Schenker, Harmony (MIT Press). Voice leading analysis of Bach chorales. 12tone YouTube — harmony analysis series.

column3: What is a 'pedal point'?
column4: A sustained or repeated note in one voice (usually bass) while harmonies change above it
column5: A note held throughout the entire piece
column6: A bass pattern that repeats every bar
column7: The tonic note ringing under a chord
column11: A pedal point (organ point) holds one note — usually tonic or dominant in the bass — while harmonies above change, sometimes creating dissonances that resolve. Tonic pedals create stability; dominant pedals build tremendous tension before final resolution.
column12: Include a composition task: write an 8-bar phrase in C major featuring this harmonic device used at least twice.
column14: Alan Belkin, Musical Form and Analysis (belkin-online.ca, free). ABRSM Grade 5-6. Analyze four-voice SATB Bach Chorales (Riemenschneider edition).

column3: What is a 'Neapolitan chord' (♭II)?
column4: A major chord built on the ♭2 degree, typically in first inversion — pre-dominant function
column5: A minor chord on the 2nd degree
column6: A diminished chord on the 7th degree
column7: A borrowed chord from the dominant key
column11: The Neapolitan chord (♭II, e.g. D♭ major in C minor) is a major triad on the lowered 2nd degree, typically in first inversion (♭II6). It has a pre-dominant function — it leads dramatically to V, with a half-step descent in the bass (D♭→C or D♭→G). Prevalent in Classical and Romantic music.
column12: Include a jazz standard reference: identify two jazz standards where this harmonic concept appears and analyze the specific bars.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book, Ch. 10-14 (harmonic function). Stefan Kostka, Tonal Harmony Ch. 18-22. ABRSM Grade 6.

column3: What is a 'circle of fifths progression'?
column4: A progression where each chord root descends a perfect 5th (or ascends a perfect 4th)
column5: A progression moving by major 3rds
column6: A progression using only the 3 main chords
column7: A progression staying entirely within one key
column11: Circle of fifths motion (e.g. C-F-B♭-E♭...) is the most fundamental harmonic motion in Western music. The ii-V-I is a 3-chord segment of this circle. Sequences moving in 5ths create strong harmonic momentum and were the basis of Baroque and Classical harmony.
column12: Add a reharmonization task: replace one chord in a I-vi-ii-V with this concept and describe the difference in feeling.
column14: Jerry Coker, Improvising Jazz. ABRSM Grade 5-6. Recommended: analyze the harmony of 'Autumn Leaves' (jazz standard) bar by bar for function.

column3: What is 'figured bass'?
column4: A Baroque notation system using numbers below bass notes to indicate upper chord intervals
column5: A bass line written with note-head shapes
column6: A rhythmic bass pattern notation
column7: A staff reserved only for bass instruments
column11: Figured bass uses numbers to indicate intervals above the bass: 6/3 = first inversion, 6/4 = second inversion, 7 = seventh chord. Baroque keyboard players improvised harmonies from this shorthand. It's the historical precursor to modern chord symbols.
column12: Include a voice-leading analysis: trace the movement of the soprano and bass through this progression step by step.
column14: Berklee Online: Harmony 3-4 (harmonic function, voice leading). Ted Pease, Jazz Composition Ch. 1-5. ABRSM Grade 6. iReal Pro for harmonic practice.

column3: What is 'contrary motion' in counterpoint?
column4: Two voices moving in opposite directions simultaneously
column5: Two voices moving in the same direction
column6: One voice sustaining while another moves
column7: Two voices moving by identical intervals in the same direction
column11: Contrary motion is the most independent and safest type of voice motion — one voice goes up while the other goes down. It naturally avoids parallel perfect intervals and maintains voice independence. The other types are: similar, oblique, and parallel motion.
column12: Add a comparison question: contrast this cadence or concept with the similar one students most commonly confuse it with.
column14: Heinrich Schenker, Harmony (MIT Press). Voice leading analysis of Bach chorales. 12tone YouTube — harmony analysis series.

column3: What is a 'chromatic mediant' relationship?
column4: Two chords a major or minor 3rd apart that include chromatic notes not in the key — e.g. C major to E♭ major
column5: Two chords a perfect 5th apart
column6: Chords borrowed from the parallel minor only
column7: Chords sharing only one common tone
column11: Chromatic mediants create vivid harmonic color through 3rd-relationships involving chromatic alteration. E.g. C major → E♭ major (down a minor 3rd, chromatic). Extremely common in Romantic music (Schubert, Wagner) and Hollywood film scores.
column12: Include a composition task: write an 8-bar phrase in C major featuring this harmonic device used at least twice.
column14: Alan Belkin, Musical Form and Analysis (belkin-online.ca, free). ABRSM Grade 5-6. Analyze four-voice SATB Bach Chorales (Riemenschneider edition).

column3: What is a 'German Augmented 6th' chord?
column4: An augmented 6th chord with an extra major 3rd above the bass — contains a doubly augmented 4th and resolves to V
column5: An augmented chord from the German Romantic period
column6: A chord with minor 6th and augmented 5th
column7: A borrowed dominant chord from another key
column11: The German Aug6 (in C minor: A♭-C-E♭-F#) is enharmonically equivalent to E♭7. The interval of an augmented 6th (A♭ to F#) expands outward to a perfect octave on G (the dominant). It's the most chromatic of the three augmented 6th chords (Italian, French, German).
column12: Include a jazz standard reference: identify two jazz standards where this harmonic concept appears and analyze the specific bars.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book, Ch. 10-14 (harmonic function). Stefan Kostka, Tonal Harmony Ch. 18-22. ABRSM Grade 6.

column3: What is a 'suspension' in voice leading?
column4: A note held over from a previous chord that creates dissonance, then resolves by step
column5: A chord that avoids the tonic
column6: A chord that prolongs dominant function
column7: A tied note between two measures
column11: A suspension (e.g. 4-3 suspension) holds a note from the previous chord into the new chord, creating dissonance, before resolving by a step (usually downward) to the chord tone. The 4-3 and 7-6 suspensions are the most common in common practice writing.
column12: Add a reharmonization task: replace one chord in a I-vi-ii-V with this concept and describe the difference in feeling.
column14: Jerry Coker, Improvising Jazz. ABRSM Grade 5-6. Recommended: analyze the harmony of 'Autumn Leaves' (jazz standard) bar by bar for function.

column3: What is 5/4 time and how is it typically felt?
column4: An asymmetric simple meter with 5 quarter-note beats, felt as 3+2 or 2+3
column5: Five eighth-note beats per measure
column6: The same as 5/8 but slower
column7: A compound triple meter
column11: 5/4 has 5 quarter-note beats typically grouped as 3+2 or 2+3. Dave Brubeck's 'Take Five' (3+2) is the most famous example. The asymmetric grouping gives 5/4 its distinctive forward-propelling 'limping' quality.
column12: Add a harmonic context exercise placing this concept within a complete musical progression.
column14: Stefan Kostka & Dorothy Payne, Tonal Harmony, 8th ed. (McGraw-Hill). ABRSM Theory Grade 5. musictheory.net advanced exercises.

column3: What is '7/8' and how does it differ from '7/4'?
column4: 7/8 has 7 eighth-note beats (fast and driving); 7/4 has 7 quarter-note beats (broader and more spacious)
column5: They are identical in feel
column6: 7/8 is compound; 7/4 is simple
column7: 7/8 moves in groups of 2; 7/4 in groups of 3
column11: 7/8 groups as 2+2+3, 2+3+2, or 3+2+2 eighth notes — fast and urgent. 7/4 has the same beat count but broader quarter-note beats. Both appear in Balkan folk music, progressive rock (Tool, Genesis), and contemporary jazz.
column12: Include a transcription or analysis task using a professional recording.
column14: Steven Laitz, The Complete Musician (Oxford). ABRSM Grade 5-6. Berklee Music Theory Books 1-2 (Paul Schmeling). Practise in at least 3 keys.

column3: What is 'hemiola'?
column4: A rhythmic device creating a 3:2 feel by grouping 3 groups of 2 where 2 groups of 3 are expected
column5: A triplet in compound meter
column6: Playing 6/8 with a 3/4 feel throughout
column7: A change of time signature mid-piece
column11: Hemiola creates a 3:2 polyrhythm within one passage. In 6/8 (2 beats of 3), accenting every 2 eighth notes implies 3/4. Brahms used hemiola extensively; it appears in cadences in Renaissance music, giving rhythmic interest at phrase endings.
column12: Add a composition or arranging exercise requiring creative application of this concept.
column14: Berklee Online Music Theory series. ABRSM Grade 5. iReal Pro — apply harmonic concepts with backing tracks in all 12 keys.

column3: What is an 'additive rhythm'?
column4: A rhythm built by adding beat groupings (e.g. 2+3+2 = 7 beats) rather than dividing a fixed pulse
column5: A rhythm that speeds up progressively
column6: A rhythm with many tied notes
column7: Adding one extra beat each measure
column11: Additive rhythms build measures by combining groups of 2s and 3s (e.g. 2+3+2 = 7). Unlike 7/4 which divides 7 evenly, additive meter feels uneven with longer and shorter beat groups. Common in Balkan, Indian, and African music; used by Bartók and Steve Reich.
column12: Include cross-genre examples showing how this appears in multiple musical styles.
column14: Alan Belkin, Tonal Counterpoint (belkin-online.ca, free). ABRSM Grade 5-6. Adam Neely YouTube for contemporary context.

column3: What is 'rhythmic displacement'?
column4: Shifting a rhythmic or melodic pattern to start on a different beat or subdivision than its original position
column5: Playing a rhythm backwards
column6: Doubling all note values
column7: Playing a rhythm in a higher register
column11: Rhythmic displacement shifts a pattern to start on a different beat (e.g. a motif normally on beat 1 now starts on beat 2 or the 'and' of beat 1). This creates cross-rhythms and metric ambiguity. It's a key technique in jazz, modern composition, and Steve Reich's phase music.
column12: Add a comparison to the advanced version to preview upcoming material.
column14: Walter Piston, Harmony, 5th ed. (Norton). ABRSM Grade 6. Analyse a piece from the standard repertoire to find this concept in context.

column3: What is 'metric modulation'?
column4: A tempo change where a note value from the old tempo equals a different note value in the new tempo
column5: Changing time signature without changing tempo
column6: Gradually accelerating the tempo throughout
column7: A modulation that changes both key and meter
column11: Metric modulation (associated with Elliott Carter) bridges two tempos using a mathematical relationship. Example: in ♩=120, a triplet eighth note = 80 BPM. If that triplet eighth becomes the new quarter note, you've modulated to ♩=80 via a 3:2 ratio.
column12: Add a harmonic context exercise placing this concept within a complete musical progression.
column14: Stefan Kostka & Dorothy Payne, Tonal Harmony, 8th ed. (McGraw-Hill). ABRSM Theory Grade 5. musictheory.net advanced exercises.

column3: What is the difference between 'simple' and 'compound' meter?
column4: Simple: each beat divides into 2; Compound: each beat divides into 3
column5: Simple has fewer beats; Compound has more
column6: Simple is always 4/4; Compound is always 6/8
column7: Simple uses quarter notes; Compound always uses eighth notes
column11: In simple meter (2/4, 3/4, 4/4), each beat divides into 2 equal parts. In compound meter (6/8, 9/8, 12/8), each beat is a dotted note value dividing into 3 equal parts. The bottom number in compound time represents the subdivision unit, not the beat.
column12: Include a transcription or analysis task using a professional recording.
column14: Steven Laitz, The Complete Musician (Oxford). ABRSM Grade 5-6. Berklee Music Theory Books 1-2 (Paul Schmeling). Practise in at least 3 keys.

column3: What is 'cross-rhythm'?
column4: Two rhythmic patterns with genuinely different implied meters occurring simultaneously
column5: A rhythm that changes feel every bar
column6: A syncopated rhythm in 4/4
column7: Two instruments playing the same rhythm at different volumes
column11: Cross-rhythm creates two or more conflicting implied meters simultaneously (e.g. 3 against 4). It's deeper than syncopation — actual competing metric frameworks. Fundamental to West African drumming, Brazilian choro, and jazz. Think 3:2 clave patterns over 4/4.
column12: Add a composition or arranging exercise requiring creative application of this concept.
column14: Berklee Online Music Theory series. ABRSM Grade 5. iReal Pro — apply harmonic concepts with backing tracks in all 12 keys.

column3: What is 'tempo rubato'?
column4: Flexible, expressive tempo where the performer freely pushes and pulls against the strict pulse
column5: Strictly following the metronome marking
column6: A gradual ritardando at end of a phrase
column7: A rhythm with notes of unequal value
column11: Rubato (Italian: 'robbed time') allows expressive tempo flexibility. Chopin's music is famous for rubato. Some theorists distinguish 'melodic rubato' (melody flexes, accompaniment stays steady) from 'agogic rubato' (overall tempo shifts for expression).
column12: Include cross-genre examples showing how this appears in multiple musical styles.
column14: Alan Belkin, Tonal Counterpoint (belkin-online.ca, free). ABRSM Grade 5-6. Adam Neely YouTube for contemporary context.

column3: What is an 'isorhythm' (in medieval/20th century contexts)?
column4: A repeating rhythmic pattern (talea) that cycles independently of the repeating pitch pattern (color)
column5: A rhythm that stays the same throughout a piece
column6: A rhythm derived from a mode
column7: A symmetric rhythmic palindrome
column11: Isorhythm (from medieval motets) uses a repeating rhythmic pattern (talea) and repeating pitch series (color) that are different lengths, creating a slowly shifting composite pattern. Messiaen revived the concept in '20th Century works like 'Quartet for the End of Time.'
column12: Add a comparison to the advanced version to preview upcoming material.
column14: Walter Piston, Harmony, 5th ed. (Norton). ABRSM Grade 6. Analyse a piece from the standard repertoire to find this concept in context.

column3: What is a 'tuplet'?
column4: A group of notes performed in the time of a different number of the same note value (e.g. triplet, quintuplet, septuplet)
column5: A note that is too short for the meter
column6: A grace note
column7: A tied note in compound meter
column11: Tuplets divide a beat non-standardly: a triplet (3 in space of 2), quintuplet (5 in space of 4), septuplet (7 in space of 4). They create rhythmic tension against the regular pulse. Brahms and Chopin used complex tuplets expressively; jazz pianists use them in solos.
column12: Add a harmonic context exercise placing this concept within a complete musical progression.
column14: Stefan Kostka & Dorothy Payne, Tonal Harmony, 8th ed. (McGraw-Hill). ABRSM Theory Grade 5. musictheory.net advanced exercises.

column3: What is 'polymeter'?
column4: Two or more parts playing in different time signatures simultaneously (e.g. one part in 3/4, another in 4/4)
column5: Two rhythmic patterns in the same meter
column6: A single measure with changing time signatures
column7: Playing at two different tempos simultaneously
column11: Polymeter layers different time signatures simultaneously. E.g. if a piano plays in 3/4 while a drum plays in 4/4 at the same pulse speed, they'll realign every 12 beats. Different from polyrhythm (same meter, different groupings) — polymeter uses genuinely different meters.
column12: Include a transcription or analysis task using a professional recording.
column14: Steven Laitz, The Complete Musician (Oxford). ABRSM Grade 5-6. Berklee Music Theory Books 1-2 (Paul Schmeling). Practise in at least 3 keys.

column3: What is a 'clave' rhythm?
column4: A foundational two-bar rhythmic pattern in Afro-Cuban music (3-2 or 2-3 son clave, rumba clave)
column5: A Cuban instrument
column6: Any repeating bass pattern
column7: A syncopated jazz rhythm
column11: The clave is a foundational 2-bar pattern in Cuban music (son clave, rumba clave, bossa nova clave). 3-2 clave: 3 notes in bar 1, 2 in bar 2. All instruments relate to the clave — it's the rhythmic anchor of the ensemble. Essential knowledge for Latin jazz musicians.
column12: Add a composition or arranging exercise requiring creative application of this concept.
column14: Berklee Online Music Theory series. ABRSM Grade 5. iReal Pro — apply harmonic concepts with backing tracks in all 12 keys.

column3: What is a 'sonata form' and what are its three main sections?
column4: Exposition (themes introduced), Development (themes transformed), Recapitulation (themes return in tonic)
column5: Verse, Chorus, Bridge
column6: Introduction, Theme, Coda
column7: A, B, A' (ternary form)
column11: Sonata form (first-movement form) is the most important large-scale structure in classical music. Exposition: contrasting themes in tonic and dominant. Development: themes fragmented and transformed through various keys. Recapitulation: themes return, both now in the tonic.
column12: Add a harmonic context exercise placing this concept within a complete musical progression.
column14: Stefan Kostka & Dorothy Payne, Tonal Harmony, 8th ed. (McGraw-Hill). ABRSM Theory Grade 5. musictheory.net advanced exercises.

column3: What is 'rondo form'?
column4: A form where a main theme (A) alternates with contrasting episodes: ABACADA...
column5: A form with only one theme repeated
column6: A variation form
column7: A binary form with a coda
column11: In rondo form, the main theme (A) returns after each contrasting episode. A-B-A-C-A-D-A is the pattern. It's common in Classical-era final movements (Beethoven, Mozart). The recurring refrain gives unity while episodes provide variety.
column12: Include a transcription or analysis task using a professional recording.
column14: Steven Laitz, The Complete Musician (Oxford). ABRSM Grade 5-6. Berklee Music Theory Books 1-2 (Paul Schmeling). Practise in at least 3 keys.

column3: What is a 'theme and variations' form?
column4: A main theme followed by multiple variations that transform it while maintaining recognizable structural features
column5: A form where the theme is different every time
column6: A form with no structured repetition
column7: A form where each section is in a new key
column11: Theme and variations present a theme, then transform it through various techniques: changing key, mode, meter, tempo, texture, or ornamentation, while keeping the harmonic or melodic skeleton recognizable. Examples: Beethoven's 'Diabelli Variations,' Brahms' 'Handel Variations.'
column12: Add a composition or arranging exercise requiring creative application of this concept.
column14: Berklee Online Music Theory series. ABRSM Grade 5. iReal Pro — apply harmonic concepts with backing tracks in all 12 keys.

column3: What is a 'da capo aria' (ABA form)?
column4: A Baroque vocal form where the first section (A) returns after a contrasting middle section (B), often with improvised ornamentation
column5: A form with three different sections
column6: A form with a final cadenza
column7: A two-section form with no repeats
column11: The da capo aria (D.C. = 'from the beginning') returns to the opening section after a contrasting middle, creating ABA form. In Baroque performance practice, singers were expected to ornament the returning A section. Handel and Bach used this extensively.
column12: Include cross-genre examples showing how this appears in multiple musical styles.
column14: Alan Belkin, Tonal Counterpoint (belkin-online.ca, free). ABRSM Grade 5-6. Adam Neely YouTube for contemporary context.

column3: What is 'through-composed' form?
column4: A form with no repeated sections — the music continuously develops new material from beginning to end
column5: A form with a repeating chorus
column6: A form using only one theme
column7: A form based on variations
column11: Through-composed music never repeats large sections — it continuously develops. Schubert's 'Erlkönig' is a famous example. It allows the music to follow a dramatic narrative closely. Pop songs are rarely through-composed; classical Lieder often are.
column12: Add a comparison to the advanced version to preview upcoming material.
column14: Walter Piston, Harmony, 5th ed. (Norton). ABRSM Grade 6. Analyse a piece from the standard repertoire to find this concept in context.

column3: What is a 'fugue'?
column4: A polyphonic composition where a main theme (subject) enters in different voices, is developed through imitation, and returns throughout
column5: A fast, virtuosic instrumental piece
column6: A variation form
column7: A song form with a repeated refrain
column11: A fugue begins with a subject entering in one voice, then another voice answers (the 'answer'), then other voices enter in turn. Development uses techniques like inversion, augmentation, diminution, and stretto. Bach's Well-Tempered Clavier contains 48 preludes and fugues.
column12: Add a harmonic context exercise placing this concept within a complete musical progression.
column14: Stefan Kostka & Dorothy Payne, Tonal Harmony, 8th ed. (McGraw-Hill). ABRSM Theory Grade 5. musictheory.net advanced exercises.

column3: What is a 'sequence' in melodic analysis?
column4: A melodic or harmonic pattern repeated at a higher or lower pitch level
column5: A chord progression
column6: A scale pattern
column7: A repeating rhythmic figure
column11: A sequence repeats a melodic or harmonic pattern at a different pitch. Real sequences transpose exactly; tonal sequences adjust intervals to stay in key. Sequences are a primary developmental technique — the circle-of-fifths sequence is ubiquitous in Baroque and Classical music.
column12: Include a transcription or analysis task using a professional recording.
column14: Steven Laitz, The Complete Musician (Oxford). ABRSM Grade 5-6. Berklee Music Theory Books 1-2 (Paul Schmeling). Practise in at least 3 keys.

column3: What is a 'development section' in sonata form?
column4: A section where themes from the exposition are fragmented, transformed, and moved through various keys
column5: A section introducing new themes
column6: A section that returns to the tonic
column7: A section that presents both themes simultaneously
column11: The development section takes themes from the exposition and subjects them to motivic fragmentation, sequence, modulation, harmonic tension, and transformation. It typically moves farthest from the home key before a retransition on the dominant prepares the recapitulation.
column12: Add a composition or arranging exercise requiring creative application of this concept.
column14: Berklee Online Music Theory series. ABRSM Grade 5. iReal Pro — apply harmonic concepts with backing tracks in all 12 keys.

column3: What is an 'exposition' in sonata form?
column4: The opening section that presents the first theme in the tonic and second theme in the dominant (or relative major)
column5: The opening of any piece
column6: A section that develops all themes
column7: The final section of the piece
column11: The exposition introduces contrasting themes: the first theme in the home key (tonic), a transition modulating to a new key, and the second theme in the dominant (if major) or relative major (if minor). This tonal contrast creates the tension resolved in the recapitulation.
column12: Include cross-genre examples showing how this appears in multiple musical styles.
column14: Alan Belkin, Tonal Counterpoint (belkin-online.ca, free). ABRSM Grade 5-6. Adam Neely YouTube for contemporary context.

column3: What is 'strophic form'?
column4: A song form where every verse uses the same melody and harmony (like a hymn)
column5: A form with many different melodies
column6: A form with a repeated chorus only
column7: A three-part ABA form
column11: Strophic form repeats the same music for each stanza of text (A-A-A-A). Hymnals and folk songs are typically strophic. It prioritizes melodic memorability and communal singing over narrative development. Modified strophic form (A-A'-A'') allows slight variations each repeat.
column12: Add a comparison to the advanced version to preview upcoming material.
column14: Walter Piston, Harmony, 5th ed. (Norton). ABRSM Grade 6. Analyse a piece from the standard repertoire to find this concept in context.

column3: What is a 'recapitulation' in sonata form?
column4: The final section that returns the themes in the tonic key — both themes now appear in the same key
column5: A section that introduces a new theme
column6: A section building to maximum tension
column7: A section that moves away from the home key
column11: The recapitulation resolves the tonal conflict of the exposition by presenting both themes in the home key. The second theme, originally in the dominant, now returns in the tonic. This creates a sense of structural resolution and satisfying completion.
column12: Add a harmonic context exercise placing this concept within a complete musical progression.
column14: Stefan Kostka & Dorothy Payne, Tonal Harmony, 8th ed. (McGraw-Hill). ABRSM Theory Grade 5. musictheory.net advanced exercises.

column3: What is a 'codetta'?
column4: A short concluding passage at the end of a section (smaller than a coda, which closes the whole piece)
column5: The same as a coda
column6: The opening phrase of a piece
column7: A short bridge connecting two sections
column11: A codetta is a brief concluding passage closing a section (e.g., the end of the exposition in sonata form). It typically reinforces the tonic with cadential gestures. It's smaller in scale than the full coda that closes the entire movement.
column12: Include a transcription or analysis task using a professional recording.
column14: Steven Laitz, The Complete Musician (Oxford). ABRSM Grade 5-6. Berklee Music Theory Books 1-2 (Paul Schmeling). Practise in at least 3 keys.

column3: What is a 'tritone substitution'?
column4: Replacing a dominant 7th chord with another dominant 7th chord a tritone away — they share the same tritone (3rd and 7th)
column5: Replacing V with IV
column6: Replacing the tonic with a chord a tritone above
column7: Playing two chords a tritone apart simultaneously
column11: G7 and D♭7 share the same tritone (B/C♭ and F). Substituting D♭7 for G7 in ii-V-I creates: Dm7-D♭7-Cmaj7 — a chromatic, descending bass line. The tritone sub adds harmonic sophistication while maintaining the same resolution quality.
column12: Add a transcription exercise: choose a 4-8 bar excerpt from a jazz recording that demonstrates this concept for students to transcribe.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book, Ch. 7-14. David Liebman, A Chromatic Approach to Jazz Harmony and Melody (Advance Music). iReal Pro.

column3: What is 'chord-scale theory'?
column4: The practice of assigning specific scales to each chord in a progression to guide improvisation
column5: Using chords to build scales
column6: A theory about key signatures
column7: A method of writing chord voicings
column11: Chord-scale theory maps a scale to each chord: Cmaj7 → Ionian or Lydian, G7 → Mixolydian or altered, Dm7 → Dorian, etc. Popularized at Berklee College and by Miles Davis, it gave improvisers a systematic way to choose notes for each chord.
column12: Include a play-along application: create an iReal Pro chart or backing track for students to practice this concept in real time.
column14: Aebersold Play-Along Series (Vol. 3, 6, 7, 8). Jerry Coker, Improvising Jazz. Recommended: study how Bill Evans applies this concept in his trio recordings.

column3: What is a 'reharmonization'?
column4: Replacing the original chords of a song with new chords that support the same melody but create different harmonic color
column5: Transposing a song to a new key
column6: Adding notes to chords
column7: Removing chord extensions
column11: Reharmonization replaces original chord changes with alternatives that still support the melody. Techniques include tritone substitution, secondary dominants, chord substitution with relative chords, and chromatic planing. Jazz musicians reharmonize standards to add their personal harmonic voice.
column12: Add a cross-era comparison: contrast how this concept is applied differently in bebop, modal jazz, and contemporary fusion.
column14: Berklee Jazz Harmony (Ted Pease & Ken Pullig). Jazzadvice.com application articles. Recommended: transcribe a 4-bar excerpt demonstrating this concept.

column3: What is a 'minor ii-V-i' progression?
column4: iim7♭5 – V7alt (or V7♭9) – im7 (e.g. Bm7♭5 – E7♭9 – Am7 in A minor)
column5: Dm7 – G7 – Cm7
column6: iim7 – V7 – im7
column7: Cm7 – F7 – B♭m7
column11: The minor ii-V-i uses a half-diminished ii chord and an altered dominant V. In A minor: Bm7♭5 – E7♭9 – Am7 (or Am(maj7)). The Bm7♭5 is built on the 2nd degree of A harmonic or melodic minor. The V7♭9 creates maximum tension before resolving to the tonic minor.
column12: Include an instrument-specific note: explain how a bassist or pianist would specifically apply this concept in their role.
column14: Ron Miller, Modal Jazz Composition and Harmony (Advance Music). iReal Pro app. Recommended listening: John Coltrane late 1950s and early 1960s recordings.

column3: What is a 'bebop scale'?
column4: A major or dominant scale with an added chromatic passing tone that places chord tones on strong beats
column5: A scale with 8 notes
column6: A mode of the melodic minor
column7: An altered dominant scale
column11: The bebop dominant scale adds a ♮7 to the Mixolydian scale (root 2 3 4 5 6 ♭7 ♮7), giving 8 notes. When played in eighth notes starting on beat 1, all chord tones (1, 3, 5, ♭7) fall on downbeats. This rhythmic alignment is the key innovation of the bebop scale.
column12: Add a composition exercise: write or reharmonize a 16-bar jazz passage that uses this concept at least twice.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Piano Book (Sher Music). Joe Henderson 'The Inner Urge' for advanced harmonic application. Berklee Online Jazz Harmony.

column3: What is 'playing 'outside'?
column4: Deliberately playing notes or phrases outside the current key or chord changes for expressive tension
column5: Playing without a rhythm section
column6: Improvising without sheet music
column7: Playing in a different octave than expected
column11: Playing outside' means temporarily using notes or scales foreign to the current harmonic context, then resolving back 'inside.' Techniques include playing a tritone away, superimposing different key centers, or using chromatic lines. John Coltrane and Woody Shaw were masters of outside playing.
column12: Add a transcription exercise: choose a 4-8 bar excerpt from a jazz recording that demonstrates this concept for students to transcribe.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book, Ch. 7-14. David Liebman, A Chromatic Approach to Jazz Harmony and Melody (Advance Music). iReal Pro.

column3: What are 'Coltrane changes'?
column4: A harmonic system substituting the standard ii-V-I with a series of dominant 7th chords moving in major 3rds (dividing the octave into 3 equal parts)
column5: Extended chord voicings used by Coltrane
column6: A set of scales Coltrane used
column7: A type of rhythmic pattern
column11: Coltrane changes (from 'Giant Steps,' 1960) replace a single ii-V-I with three ii-V-I progressions, each a major 3rd apart (B, G, E♭). The roots cycle through three key centers that divide the octave equally. This creates rapid harmonic motion that was revolutionary for jazz improvisation.
column12: Include a play-along application: create an iReal Pro chart or backing track for students to practice this concept in real time.
column14: Aebersold Play-Along Series (Vol. 3, 6, 7, 8). Jerry Coker, Improvising Jazz. Recommended: study how Bill Evans applies this concept in his trio recordings.

column3: What is 'quartal harmony' in jazz?
column4: Chords built by stacking perfect 4ths instead of 3rds — creating an open, ambiguous sound favored by McCoy Tyner
column5: Chords voiced in the 4th octave
column6: The harmony of 4-note chords exclusively
column7: A substitute for the dominant chord
column11: Quartal harmony stacks 4ths (e.g. C-F-B♭-E♭). It avoids the clear major/minor quality of tertian (3rd-based) chords, making it harmonically flexible. McCoy Tyner, Herbie Hancock, and Bill Evans used quartal voicings extensively in post-bop jazz.
column12: Add a cross-era comparison: contrast how this concept is applied differently in bebop, modal jazz, and contemporary fusion.
column14: Berklee Jazz Harmony (Ted Pease & Ken Pullig). Jazzadvice.com application articles. Recommended: transcribe a 4-bar excerpt demonstrating this concept.

column3: What is a 'side-slipping' technique in jazz improvisation?
column4: Temporarily shifting your melodic lines up or down a half step (into a neighboring key) before resolving back
column5: Playing notes a step above the chord
column6: A chromatic scale approach
column7: Playing chromatically descending lines
column11: Side-slipping moves an entire phrase or scale up or down by a semitone into an 'outside' area, then resolves back to the 'inside.' It creates sudden harmonic tension and is used by saxophonists like Sonny Rollins and guitarists like Pat Martino for dramatic effect.
column12: Include an instrument-specific note: explain how a bassist or pianist would specifically apply this concept in their role.
column14: Ron Miller, Modal Jazz Composition and Harmony (Advance Music). iReal Pro app. Recommended listening: John Coltrane late 1950s and early 1960s recordings.

column3: What is a 'pedal point' in jazz context?
column4: A sustained or repeated bass note under changing harmonies — common in modal jazz and vamps
column5: A bossa nova rhythm pattern
column6: A bass line that moves chromatically
column7: An ostinato in the treble
column11: In jazz, pedal points are used in vamps and modal sections (e.g. holding a D bass note while cycling through D Dorian harmonies). It creates hypnotic tension and is foundational in modal jazz (Miles Davis, John Coltrane on 'My Favorite Things').
column12: Add a composition exercise: write or reharmonize a 16-bar jazz passage that uses this concept at least twice.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Piano Book (Sher Music). Joe Henderson 'The Inner Urge' for advanced harmonic application. Berklee Online Jazz Harmony.

column3: What is 'enclosure' (or 'encircling') in jazz melody?
column4: Surrounding a target note with notes a half step above AND a half step below before landing on it
column5: Playing notes an octave apart
column6: Repeating the same note twice before moving
column7: A chromatic scale fragment
column11: Enclosure approaches a target note (usually a chord tone) from a half step above and a half step below (or various combinations). E.g. targeting C: play C#, B, then C. This chromatic approach technique creates bebop-style tension and resolution and is fundamental to jazz phrasing.
column12: Add a transcription exercise: choose a 4-8 bar excerpt from a jazz recording that demonstrates this concept for students to transcribe.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book, Ch. 7-14. David Liebman, A Chromatic Approach to Jazz Harmony and Melody (Advance Music). iReal Pro.

column3: What is 'spread voicing' in jazz piano?
column4: A chord voicing with wide intervals between notes — typically leaving a large gap in the middle register
column5: A voicing with all notes closely together
column6: A voicing using only the outer notes of a chord
column7: A chord voicing technique for big bands only
column11: Spread voicings place chord notes widely across the keyboard, often with a gap between bass and tenor voices but close intervals in the upper voices. Bill Evans' left-hand voicings (e.g. root-7th-3rd-5th spread across two octaves) exemplify this. Spread voicings have a rich, orchestral sound.
column12: Include a play-along application: create an iReal Pro chart or backing track for students to practice this concept in real time.
column14: Aebersold Play-Along Series (Vol. 3, 6, 7, 8). Jerry Coker, Improvising Jazz. Recommended: study how Bill Evans applies this concept in his trio recordings.

column3: How do relative major and minor keys relate on the circle of fifths?
column4: The relative minor is always 3 positions counter-clockwise (or 9 clockwise) from its relative major
column5: They are directly opposite each other
column6: They are adjacent on the circle
column7: The relative minor is always above its major key
column11: On the circle of fifths, the relative minor (starting on scale degree 6) is 3 positions counterclockwise from its relative major. E.g. C major (no sharps/flats) → A minor (no sharps/flats). They share key signatures but differ in tonal center.
column12: Test both directions: key name → accidentals AND accidentals → key name — both are essential for complete mastery.
column14: Stefan Kostka, Tonal Harmony. ABRSM Grade 5 key signature requirements. Tenuto app — advanced key signature exercises. Transposition practice in all 12 keys.

column3: What key has 4 sharps?
column4: E major (and C# minor)
column5: A major
column6: B major
column7: F# major
column11: Following the order F# C# G# D#: 1 sharp = G, 2 = D, 3 = A, 4 = E major. E major sharps: F# C# G# D#. Its relative minor is C# minor. E major is a common key for guitar-based music because of the open string resonances.
column12: Add a parallel minor exercise: identify the parallel minor key and describe how the key signature changes.
column14: ABRSM Grade 5 Theory (AB Guide to Music Theory, Part 2). Circle of fifths modulation. Analyze how Bach's Preludes cycle through related keys.

column3: What key has 4 flats?
column4: A♭ major (and F minor)
column5: D♭ major
column6: E♭ major
column7: B♭ major
column11: Following the order B♭ E♭ A♭ D♭: 1 flat = F, 2 = B♭, 3 = E♭, 4 = A♭ major. A♭ major (A♭ B♭ C D♭ E♭ F G) is a common key in jazz and classical piano music. Its relative minor is F minor.
column12: Include a modulation exercise: identify a pivot chord that connects this key to its dominant key a perfect 5th above.
column14: Berklee Music Theory Book 2. Circle of fifths modulation analysis. ABRSM Grade 5-6. Analyze how Beethoven's development sections move through related keys.

column3: What is the 'parallel major' of A minor?
column4: A major
column5: C major
column6: E major
column7: D major
column11: A parallel key shares the same tonic (root) but different key signatures. A minor and A major both start on A. A major has 3 sharps (F# C# G#); A natural minor has no sharps or flats. Composers frequently move between parallel keys for dramatic effect.
column12: Add an analysis exercise: find this key signature in a published excerpt and confirm the key by locating the leading tone.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book — key signatures in jazz context. Aebersold Play-Along Series — practice in all major and minor keys systematically.

column3: What is a 'key area' in classical analysis?
column4: A section of music that establishes and orbits a particular tonic through its harmonies
column5: A modulation
column6: A chord in a foreign key
column7: A section without a clear key
column11: Key areas are regions in a piece where a particular tonic is established and maintained. In sonata form, the exposition has two key areas (tonic and dominant/relative major). Analyzing key areas reveals the large-scale harmonic architecture of a piece.
column12: Include a transposition task: transpose a simple 4-bar melody from C major into this key, rewriting all note names.
column14: Alan Belkin, Tonal Counterpoint. Key analysis in modulating works. ABRSM Grade 5-6. Schubert Impromptus for key change examples and analysis.

column3: Which major key is enharmonic to B major?
column4: C♭ major
column5: D♭ major
column6: A♭ major
column7: F# major
column11: B major (5 sharps: F# C# G# D# A#) and C♭ major (7 flats: B♭ E♭ A♭ D♭ G♭ C♭ F♭) are enharmonically equivalent. Both sound identical but B major is more practical to read and write in most contexts.
column12: Test both directions: key name → accidentals AND accidentals → key name — both are essential for complete mastery.
column14: Stefan Kostka, Tonal Harmony. ABRSM Grade 5 key signature requirements. Tenuto app — advanced key signature exercises. Transposition practice in all 12 keys.

column3: What key has 6 flats?
column4: G♭ major (enharmonic to F# major)
column5: D♭ major
column6: C♭ major
column7: A♭ major
column11: Following the flat order, 6 flats = B♭ E♭ A♭ D♭ G♭ C♭. The second-to-last flat is G♭, naming the key: G♭ major. G♭ major is enharmonically equivalent to F# major (6 sharps). Its relative minor is E♭ minor (enharmonic to D# minor).
column12: Add a parallel minor exercise: identify the parallel minor key and describe how the key signature changes.
column14: ABRSM Grade 5 Theory (AB Guide to Music Theory, Part 2). Circle of fifths modulation. Analyze how Bach's Preludes cycle through related keys.

column3: What is the 'Circle of Fifths' and what does moving clockwise represent?
column4: Moving clockwise adds one sharp each step and moves up a perfect 5th in key
column5: Moving clockwise adds a flat
column6: Moving clockwise moves down a half step
column7: Moving clockwise represents the order of modes
column11: The Circle of Fifths arranges all 12 major keys in a circle. Moving clockwise: C→G→D→A→E→B/C♭→F#/G♭→D♭→A♭→E♭→B♭→F→C. Each clockwise step adds one sharp or removes one flat. Counterclockwise adds one flat. Adjacent keys share 6 of 7 scale notes.
column12: Include a modulation exercise: identify a pivot chord that connects this key to its dominant key a perfect 5th above.
column14: Berklee Music Theory Book 2. Circle of fifths modulation analysis. ABRSM Grade 5-6. Analyze how Beethoven's development sections move through related keys.

column3: What is the relative minor of B♭ major?
column4: G minor
column5: D minor
column6: F minor
column7: E♭ minor
column11: The relative minor starts on the 6th degree of B♭ major. B♭ major scale: B♭ C D E♭ F G A. The 6th degree is G, so G minor is the relative minor of B♭ major. Both share 2 flats (B♭ and E♭) in their key signature.
column12: Add an analysis exercise: find this key signature in a published excerpt and confirm the key by locating the leading tone.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book — key signatures in jazz context. Aebersold Play-Along Series — practice in all major and minor keys systematically.

column3: What key has 5 sharps?
column4: B major (and G# minor)
column5: E major
column6: F# major
column7: C# major
column11: Following the sharp order F# C# G# D# A#: 5 sharps = B major. B major scale: B C# D# E F# G# A# B. Using the 'last sharp up a half step' rule: last sharp is A# → up a half step = B major. Relative minor is G# minor.
column12: Include a transposition task: transpose a simple 4-bar melody from C major into this key, rewriting all note names.
column14: Alan Belkin, Tonal Counterpoint. Key analysis in modulating works. ABRSM Grade 5-6. Schubert Impromptus for key change examples and analysis.

column3: What key has 3 flats?
column4: E♭ major (and C minor)
column5: B♭ major
column6: A♭ major
column7: G♭ major
column11: 3 flats = B♭ E♭ A♭. Second-to-last flat = E♭, naming the key: E♭ major. E♭ major is extremely common in jazz (transposing for Bb instruments), classical music, and is a natural key for many wind instruments. Relative minor: C minor.
column12: Test both directions: key name → accidentals AND accidentals → key name — both are essential for complete mastery.
column14: Stefan Kostka, Tonal Harmony. ABRSM Grade 5 key signature requirements. Tenuto app — advanced key signature exercises. Transposition practice in all 12 keys.

column3: How do you quickly identify a minor key from a key signature?
column4: Find the relative major from the key signature, then go down a minor 3rd (or find the 6th degree counting down)
column5: Count the sharps and subtract 3
column6: It always has 3 more flats than its parallel major
column7: The last sharp is always the tonic of the minor key
column11: Method 1: Find the relative major, then go down a minor 3rd (3 semitones) to find the tonic of the relative minor. Method 2 (sharp keys): the last sharp is the leading tone of the minor key (e.g. F# → the minor key is G minor? No — last sharp F# is the leading tone of G major, so go to E to find E minor).
column12: Add a parallel minor exercise: identify the parallel minor key and describe how the key signature changes.
column14: ABRSM Grade 5 Theory (AB Guide to Music Theory, Part 2). Circle of fifths modulation. Analyze how Bach's Preludes cycle through related keys.

column3: What is a 'tonicization'?
column4: Briefly treating a non-tonic chord as a temporary tonic using its own dominant — shorter than a full modulation
column5: A full key change
column6: A chromatic passing chord
column7: A pivot chord
column11: Tonicization temporarily emphasizes a non-tonic harmony using a secondary dominant (and often a secondary leading tone chord), without fully leaving the home key. If the effect lasts only 1-2 chords, it's a tonicization; if the new key is sustained and confirmed, it becomes a modulation.
column12: Include a modulation exercise: identify a pivot chord that connects this key to its dominant key a perfect 5th above.
column14: Berklee Music Theory Book 2. Circle of fifths modulation analysis. ABRSM Grade 5-6. Analyze how Beethoven's development sections move through related keys.

column3: What are the 7 modes of the melodic minor scale?
column4: Melodic minor, Dorian ♭2, Lydian Augmented, Lydian Dominant, Mixolydian ♭6, Locrian ♮2, Altered (super Locrian)
column5: Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, Locrian
column6: Harmonic minor, Dorian ♭2, Lydian Aug, Lydian Dom, Phrygian #6, Locrian ♮2, Altered
column7: Melodic minor, Lydian Aug, Lydian Dom, Altered, Locrian ♮2, Phrygian #6, Dorian ♭2
column11: The 7 modes of melodic minor: 1-Melodic minor, 2-Dorian♭2, 3-Lydian Augmented, 4-Lydian Dominant, 5-Mixolydian♭6 (Hindu), 6-Locrian♮2 (half-diminished), 7-Altered (super Locrian). Each is essential in jazz improvisation over specific chord types.
column12: Add a harmonic context: identify which mode of melodic minor or harmonic minor this scale corresponds to and which chord it generates.
column14: George Russell, Lydian Chromatic Concept of Tonal Organization (Concept Publishing). Vincent Persichetti, Twentieth Century Harmony (Norton).

column3: What is Messiaen's 'Mode of Limited Transposition No. 2' and what chord does it relate to?
column4: The diminished (octatonic) scale — it can only be transposed 3 times before repeating; relates to diminished 7th and dom7♭9 chords
column5: The whole tone scale — transposes 2 times only
column6: The pentatonic scale
column7: A 9-note scale used over augmented chords
column11: Messiaen's Mode 2 = diminished (octatonic) scale (W-H-W-H or H-W-H-W). It has only 3 distinct transpositions due to its symmetry (repeats every minor 3rd). Used over diminished 7th chords and dominant 7♭9 chords. Messiaen used it in 'Quartet for the End of Time' and 'Turangalîla.'
column12: Include a transcription task: find a professional recording using this scale and transcribe a 4-bar excerpt for analysis.
column14: Nicolas Slonimsky, Thesaurus of Scales and Melodic Patterns. Olivier Messiaen, The Technique of My Musical Language. Berklee Advanced Harmony.

column3: What is 'Messiaen's Mode of Limited Transposition No. 1'?
column4: The whole tone scale — can only be transposed 2 times before repeating
column5: The diminished scale — transposes 3 times
column6: A 6-note minor scale
column7: An 8-note mode built on major 3rds
column11: The whole tone scale (C D E F# G# A# = 6 notes, all whole steps) is Messiaen's Mode 1. It can only be transposed 2 times before repeating (C whole tone and C# whole tone exhaust all 12 pitches). Used over augmented chords and for dreamy, ambiguous harmonic color.
column12: Add a reharmonization exercise: how does using this scale over an unexpected chord create outside tension that resolves?
column14: Ernst Toch, The Shaping Forces in Music (Dover). Alan Belkin, Tonal Counterpoint. Gérard Grisey 'Partiels' score (IRCAM) for spectral scale use.

column3: What are the modes of the harmonic minor scale?
column4: Harmonic minor, Locrian ♮6, Ionian #5, Dorian #4, Phrygian Dominant, Lydian #2, Altered Diminished (Super Locrian ♭♭7)
column5: Natural minor, Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, Locrian
column6: Harmonic minor, Dorian ♭2, Lydian Dominant, Altered, Locrian ♮2, Lydian Aug, Mixolydian♭6
column7: Harmonic minor, Phrygian #6, Lydian Aug, Lydian Dom, Locrian ♮6, Ionian #5, Dorian #4
column11: The 7 modes of harmonic minor: 1-Harmonic minor, 2-Locrian♮6, 3-Ionian#5 (aug major), 4-Dorian#4 (Ukrainian Dorian), 5-Phrygian Dominant (#3), 6-Lydian#2, 7-Super Locrian♭♭7 (altered dim7). Mode 5 (Phrygian Dominant) is used over V7 in minor keys — the classic 'Spanish' dominant.
column12: Include historical context: identify a classical composer and a jazz musician who both prominently used this scale/mode.
column14: Dmitri Tymoczko, A Geometry of Music (Oxford). Journal of Music Theory — modal harmony articles. Advanced scale geometry and tonal space.

column3: What is 'Phrygian Dominant' (Mode 5 of Harmonic Minor) and where is it used?
column4: A scale like Phrygian but with a major 3rd — used over V7 in minor keys, and in Spanish/flamenco music
column5: A Phrygian scale with a raised 5th
column6: A Dorian scale transposed to the 5th degree
column7: A dominant scale starting on the Phrygian degree
column11: Phrygian Dominant (5th mode of harmonic minor): 1 ♭2 3 4 5 ♭6 ♭7. It has the half-step above the tonic of Phrygian plus a major 3rd, giving it a Spanish/Middle Eastern quality. Perfect over V7 in minor (e.g. E Phrygian Dominant over E7 resolving to Am). Common in flamenco and metal.
column12: Add a voice leading analysis: trace how this scale connects to the next scale in a common progression (e.g., II-V-I).
column14: Paul Hindemith, The Craft of Musical Composition Vol. 1. Joseph Schillinger, The Schillinger System of Musical Composition. Berklee Advanced Improvisation.

column3: What is 'negative harmony' and how does it work?
column4: A concept where a scale or chord is mirrored around an axis (typically between E and E♭ in C major) — major becomes minor and vice versa
column5: Harmony built on negative intervals
column6: A method of reharmonizing with opposite quality chords
column7: Harmony derived from the harmonic series in reverse
column11: Negative harmony reflects pitches around an axis (in C major: axis between E and E♭, or G and its mirror). The major scale reflected becomes the Phrygian scale. A I chord becomes a vm chord in the negative. Jacob Collier popularized this concept; theorist Ernst Levy formalized it.
column12: Add a harmonic context: identify which mode of melodic minor or harmonic minor this scale corresponds to and which chord it generates.
column14: George Russell, Lydian Chromatic Concept of Tonal Organization (Concept Publishing). Vincent Persichetti, Twentieth Century Harmony (Norton).

column3: What is the 'acoustic scale' (overtone scale)?
column4: A major scale with raised 4th and lowered 7th (Lydian Dominant) — matches the natural harmonic series
column5: A scale built from overtones only
column6: The whole tone scale with an added 7th
column7: A scale used in acoustic guitar tuning
column11: The acoustic scale (C D E F# G A B♭) corresponds to the pitches of the overtone series starting on C. It's equivalent to the Lydian Dominant scale — major with ♯4 and ♭7. Used by Bartók and in jazz over dominant chords with #11.
column12: Include a transcription task: find a professional recording using this scale and transcribe a 4-bar excerpt for analysis.
column14: Nicolas Slonimsky, Thesaurus of Scales and Melodic Patterns. Olivier Messiaen, The Technique of My Musical Language. Berklee Advanced Harmony.

column3: What is 'hexatonic' harmony and how does it relate to voice leading?
column4: Combining two augmented triads a minor 3rd apart to create a 6-note 'hexatonic' scale with maximally smooth voice leading
column5: A 6-note scale derived from the major scale
column6: Using 6 different chords in a progression
column7: The whole tone scale used for harmonic analysis
column11: Hexatonic systems (Richard Cohn) combine two augmented triads: e.g. C augmented (C-E-G#) + E♭ augmented (E♭-G-B) = hexatonic {C, E♭, E, G, G#, B}. Voice leading between chords within one hexatonic system is maximally smooth — each voice moves by a half step or stays. Used to analyze neo-Riemannian voice leading in Romantic music.
column12: Add a reharmonization exercise: how does using this scale over an unexpected chord create outside tension that resolves?
column14: Ernst Toch, The Shaping Forces in Music (Dover). Alan Belkin, Tonal Counterpoint. Gérard Grisey 'Partiels' score (IRCAM) for spectral scale use.

column3: What is 'Scriabin's mystic chord' and which scale does it relate to?
column4: A 6-note quartal/quintal chord (C F# B♭ E A D) that relates to the Lydian Dominant scale
column5: A diminished 7th chord
column6: A polychord used by Scriabin
column7: A symmetric 6-note scale
column11: Scriabin's 'mystic chord' = C F# B♭ E A D (built in stacked 4ths). Its pitch content matches the Lydian Dominant scale (C D E F# A B♭ — same 6 notes minus the G). Scriabin built entire late works (Op. 60-74) around this sonority, creating a late-Romantic post-tonal language.
column12: Include historical context: identify a classical composer and a jazz musician who both prominently used this scale/mode.
column14: Dmitri Tymoczko, A Geometry of Music (Oxford). Journal of Music Theory — modal harmony articles. Advanced scale geometry and tonal space.

column3: What is a 'synthetic scale' in music theory?
column4: A scale invented by a composer by choosing specific intervals, not derived from existing modal systems
column5: A scale generated by a computer
column6: A scale using synthetic (electronic) timbres
column7: A scale derived from spectral analysis
column11: Synthetic scales are custom-designed scales outside traditional systems. Examples: Bartók's 'axis system' scales, Messiaen's modes, or Harry Partch's 43-tone just-intonation scales. Composers use synthetic scales to create unique harmonic and melodic worlds not available in standard Western scales.
column12: Add a voice leading analysis: trace how this scale connects to the next scale in a common progression (e.g., II-V-I).
column14: Paul Hindemith, The Craft of Musical Composition Vol. 1. Joseph Schillinger, The Schillinger System of Musical Composition. Berklee Advanced Improvisation.

column3: What is 'spectral music' and how does it approach scales?
column4: Music derived from the acoustic properties of the harmonic series — pitches, rhythms, and textures derived from the spectrum of a specific sound
column5: Music inspired by colors of the spectrum
column6: Music using only the diatonic scale
column7: Music created by analyzing the color spectrum
column11: Spectral composition (Murail, Grisey) derives all musical material from the acoustic properties of specific sounds. The harmonic series of a low C, for example, generates a unique scale of micro-intervals. Spectral music often uses microtonal tuning to match the inharmonicities of real sounds.
column12: Add a harmonic context: identify which mode of melodic minor or harmonic minor this scale corresponds to and which chord it generates.
column14: George Russell, Lydian Chromatic Concept of Tonal Organization (Concept Publishing). Vincent Persichetti, Twentieth Century Harmony (Norton).

column3: What is the 'double harmonic' (Byzantine or Arabic) scale?
column4: A scale with two augmented 2nds: 1 ♭2 3 4 5 ♭6 7 — creating a very exotic sound used in Middle Eastern music
column5: A scale combining two harmonic minor scales
column6: A scale with doubled major 3rds
column7: A Persian scale with a ♭2 and ♭5
column11: The double harmonic scale (C D♭ E F G A♭ B C) has two augmented 2nd intervals (D♭ to E, and A♭ to B), giving it a characteristically Middle Eastern/Byzantine sound. It's the same as the 1st mode of the double harmonic family, also called 'Hijaz Kar' in Arabic maqam.
column12: Include a transcription task: find a professional recording using this scale and transcribe a 4-bar excerpt for analysis.
column14: Nicolas Slonimsky, Thesaurus of Scales and Melodic Patterns. Olivier Messiaen, The Technique of My Musical Language. Berklee Advanced Harmony.

column3: What is 'pantonality' as described by Arnold Schoenberg?
column4: Music that freely moves through all tonal regions without being anchored to any single tonic — expanded tonality rather than atonality
column5: A system using all 12 tones equally
column6: A form of polytonality
column7: A term for 12-tone serial music
column11: Schoenberg preferred the term 'pantonality' over 'atonality' for his early chromatic works (Opp. 10-16). Rather than denying tonality, he felt his music embraced all tonal regions freely. True atonality followed with his 12-tone technique (Serialism), which systematically avoids tonal hierarchy.
column12: Add a reharmonization exercise: how does using this scale over an unexpected chord create outside tension that resolves?
column14: Ernst Toch, The Shaping Forces in Music (Dover). Alan Belkin, Tonal Counterpoint. Gérard Grisey 'Partiels' score (IRCAM) for spectral scale use.

column3: What is the 'harmonic series' and how does it relate to interval consonance?
column4: A series of overtones (multiples of a fundamental frequency) whose simplest ratios (2:1, 3:2, 4:3...) produce the most consonant intervals
column5: A series of chords built from the harmonics of the overtone series
column6: The circle of fifths expressed as a linear series
column7: A progression of intervals from small to large
column11: The harmonic series: fundamental (1f), octave (2f), 5th (3f), double octave (4f), major 3rd (5f)... Simpler frequency ratios = more consonance. Octave = 2:1 (most consonant), 5th = 3:2, 4th = 4:3, major 3rd = 5:4. The closer overtones share harmonics, the more consonant the interval.
column12: Add a spectral context: explain the frequency ratio of this interval in just intonation vs. its equal temperament value.
column14: Dmitri Tymoczko, A Geometry of Music (Oxford). Paul Hindemith, Craft of Musical Composition Vol. 1 — interval tension table. Journal of Music Theory.

column3: What is 'just intonation' and how does it differ from 'equal temperament'?
column4: Just intonation uses pure, mathematically exact frequency ratios; equal temperament divides the octave into 12 equal semitones — slightly 'out of tune' pure intervals but allows playing in all keys
column5: They are the same system with different names
column6: Just intonation has 7 notes; equal temperament has 12
column7: Equal temperament is older than just intonation
column11: Just intonation: intervals tuned to pure ratios (perfect 5th = 3:2 = 702 cents). Equal temperament: perfect 5th = 700 cents (2 cents flat). The 'wolf fifth' problem in just intonation (some keys are out of tune) led to equal temperament's adoption. Modern piano uses equal temperament; choral ensembles and string quartets naturally gravitate toward just intonation.
column12: Include a set theory connection: identify the interval class of this interval and which other interval shares its interval class.
column14: Richard Cohn, Audacious Euphony (Oxford) — neo-Riemannian interval analysis. Pitch Class Set Theory calculator: Ian Ring's scale page.

column3: What is a 'quarter tone' and where is it used?
