column4: A musical interval half the size of a semitone (50 cents) — used in Arabic maqam, Persian dastgah, and microtonal composition
column5: An interval between a semitone and whole tone
column6: A type of tremolo ornament
column7: A grace note lasting a quarter beat
column11: Quarter tones divide the semitone in half (50 cents). Arabic maqam and Persian classical music use quartertones as standard intervals. 20th-century composers (Scelsi, Haba, Wyschnegradsky) used quartertone systems. Quarter-tone piano and microtonal instruments are needed for accurate performance.
column12: Add a voice leading analysis: in which chromatic progressions does this interval appear as a critical step-wise resolution move?
column14: Hans Zender, spectral interval analysis. Gérard Grisey scores (IRCAM). Interval analysis using the harmonic series: identify partials 1-16.

column3: What is the 'Pythagorean comma'?
column4: The small discrepancy (≈23.5 cents) between 12 perfect 5ths (3:2 ratio) stacked and 7 octaves — the 'wolf' interval problem
column5: A half step in Pythagorean tuning
column6: The difference between a major 3rd and two minor 3rds
column7: An interval derived from Pythagorean scales
column11: If you tune 12 pure 5ths (each 3:2), you arrive 23.5 cents sharp of the starting octave. This Pythagorean comma is why pure 5ths can't fit neatly into 12 equal divisions of the octave. Various temperament systems (mean-tone, well-tempered, equal-tempered) distribute this comma differently across the keys.
column12: Include a jazz context: identify an upper structure triad voicing or extension where this interval plays a defining role.
column14: Heinrich Schenker, Harmony (MIT Press). Schenker Documents Online — interval analysis at deep structural levels. Cambridge Handbook of Music Psychology.

column3: What is a 'tritone' in terms of the harmonic series and its tendency?
column4: The tritone is distant from the fundamental in the harmonic series (appears at overtone 7 or above) — creating maximum instability and strong directional pull
column5: The tritone is the 2nd overtone
column6: The tritone is consonant in the harmonic series
column7: The tritone appears as the 5th overtone
column11: The tritone (7:5 or 10:7 ratio depending on spelling) appears high in the harmonic series, making it the most dissonant interval in tonal music. It's the only interval that equally divides the octave (6 semitones each way), giving it no preferred direction of resolution without harmonic context.
column12: Add a counterpoint rule: explain the convention governing parallel motion involving this interval in four-voice SATB writing.
column14: Elliott Carter, Harmony Book (Carl Fischer). Extended interval applications. Xenakis, Formalized Music — stochastic interval distributions.

column3: What is 'interval class'?
column4: The unordered distance between two pitch classes (0-6 semitones) — used in post-tonal analysis to group intervals and their inversions
column5: The category (perfect, major, minor) of an interval
column6: The number of semitones in an interval
column7: The quality of an interval
column11: Interval class (IC) measures the shortest distance between two pitch classes, irrespective of direction. IC ranges from 0-6: IC1 = minor 2nd/major 7th, IC2 = major 2nd/minor 7th, IC3 = minor 3rd/major 6th, IC4 = major 3rd/minor 6th, IC5 = P4/P5, IC6 = tritone. Used in pitch-class set theory (Forte).
column12: Add a spectral context: explain the frequency ratio of this interval in just intonation vs. its equal temperament value.
column14: Dmitri Tymoczko, A Geometry of Music (Oxford). Paul Hindemith, Craft of Musical Composition Vol. 1 — interval tension table. Journal of Music Theory.

column3: What is the 'Forte number' system for classifying interval collections?
column4: A catalogue of all 208 distinct pitch-class sets, each numbered (e.g. [0,1,2] = 3-1) — used for post-tonal analysis
column5: A numbering system for intervals
column6: A system for analyzing tonal intervals
column7: A Roman numeral system for advanced harmony
column11: Allen Forte's pitch-class set theory identifies all possible collections of pitches (pitch-class sets) and assigns them Forte numbers (e.g. 3-11 = [0,3,7] = minor triad). This allows analyzing atonal music systematically by comparing interval content (interval vectors) rather than tonal function.
column12: Include a set theory connection: identify the interval class of this interval and which other interval shares its interval class.
column14: Richard Cohn, Audacious Euphony (Oxford) — neo-Riemannian interval analysis. Pitch Class Set Theory calculator: Ian Ring's scale page.

column3: What does the 'interval vector' of a pitch-class set reveal?
column4: The count of each interval class (1-6) present within a set — revealing its total interval content
column5: The order of intervals in a scale
column6: The number of notes in a chord
column7: The harmonic function of a chord
column11: The interval vector lists how many times each IC (1-6) appears in a pitch-class set. E.g. a major triad [0,4,7] has vector <001110>: 0 IC1, 0 IC2, 1 IC3 (minor 3rd), 1 IC4 (major 3rd), 1 IC5 (P5), 0 IC6. Sets with the same interval vector are 'Z-related.'
column12: Add a voice leading analysis: in which chromatic progressions does this interval appear as a critical step-wise resolution move?
column14: Hans Zender, spectral interval analysis. Gérard Grisey scores (IRCAM). Interval analysis using the harmonic series: identify partials 1-16.

column3: What is 'microtonal music'?
column4: Music using intervals smaller than the semitone — including quarter tones, sixth tones, 31-EDO, 72-EDO, or just intonation
column5: Music using very soft dynamics
column6: Music with very rapid note changes
column7: Music using only minor intervals
column11: Microtonal music uses pitch divisions smaller than 12-EDO semitones. Systems include: 24-EDO (quarter tones), 31-EDO (close to 1/4-comma meantone), 72-EDO, and just intonation. Composers like Ben Johnston, Harry Partch, and Georg Friedrich Haas have created extensive microtonal works.
column12: Include a jazz context: identify an upper structure triad voicing or extension where this interval plays a defining role.
column14: Heinrich Schenker, Harmony (MIT Press). Schenker Documents Online — interval analysis at deep structural levels. Cambridge Handbook of Music Psychology.

column3: What is the difference between 'chromatic' and 'diatonic' half steps?
column4: Chromatic half step: same letter name (C to C#); diatonic half step: different letter names (E to F)
column5: They are acoustically different intervals
column6: A chromatic half step is larger than a diatonic one in equal temperament
column7: A diatonic half step is a whole tone
column11: In tonal theory, a chromatic half step (augmented unison: C to C#) moves within the same letter name. A diatonic half step (minor 2nd: E to F, B to C) moves between different letter names. In equal temperament they're the same size (100 cents), but their notation and function differ in voice leading.
column12: Add a counterpoint rule: explain the convention governing parallel motion involving this interval in four-voice SATB writing.
column14: Elliott Carter, Harmony Book (Carl Fischer). Extended interval applications. Xenakis, Formalized Music — stochastic interval distributions.

column3: What is 'enharmonic reinterpretation' and how was it used in Romantic music?
column4: Respelling an interval or chord as its enharmonic equivalent to pivot into a different harmonic context
column5: Playing two notes with different names
column6: A type of modulation using augmented chords
column7: A notational shortcut
column11: Enharmonic reinterpretation respells a chord to pivot into a new key. E.g. the diminished 7th Cdim7 (C-E♭-G♭-A) = Ebdim7 = F#dim7 = Adim7 — any note can become the root. Schubert and Liszt used enharmonic reinterpretation of dim7 and German Aug6/dom7 chords for startling key changes.
column12: Add a spectral context: explain the frequency ratio of this interval in just intonation vs. its equal temperament value.
column14: Dmitri Tymoczko, A Geometry of Music (Oxford). Paul Hindemith, Craft of Musical Composition Vol. 1 — interval tension table. Journal of Music Theory.

column3: What is 'spectral analysis' in the context of intervals and timbre?
column4: Analyzing the overtone content of a sound to derive compositional material — pitch classes and their relationships emerge from the harmonic spectrum
column5: Analyzing the color of a note
column6: Using a spectrum analyzer to tune intervals
column7: A method for measuring consonance
column11: Spectral composers (Murail, Grisey, Dufourt) analyze the overtone spectrum of specific sounds. The partials' frequency ratios generate the pitch and interval material. For example, the partials of a low E create a specific set of microtonal pitches that Grisey used in 'Partiels' (1975).
column12: Include a set theory connection: identify the interval class of this interval and which other interval shares its interval class.
column14: Richard Cohn, Audacious Euphony (Oxford) — neo-Riemannian interval analysis. Pitch Class Set Theory calculator: Ian Ring's scale page.

column3: What are 'Coltrane changes' and how do they substitute for ii-V-I?
column4: A series of three ii-V-I progressions whose tonics are a major 3rd apart (B, G, E♭), dividing the octave into 3 equal parts
column5: A single dominant 7th chord with extensions replacing ii-V-I
column6: A series of tritone substitutions
column7: A chord progression moving by minor 3rds
column11: In 'Giant Steps,' Coltrane replaced a single ii-V-I (e.g. ii-V-I in B) with three ii-Vs — each a major 3rd apart (B, G, E♭) — using the cycle of major 3rds. Each tonal center gets only 2-3 beats, creating extremely rapid harmonic motion. It remains one of the most challenging progressions for jazz improvisers.
column12: Add a reharmonization exercise: substitute this chord into a standard I-vi-ii-V and describe the harmonic effect produced.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book, Ch. 16-22 (advanced harmony). George Russell, Lydian Chromatic Concept. Vincent Persichetti, Twentieth Century Harmony.

column3: What is a 'neo-Riemannian transformation' (L, P, R operations)?
column4: Chord transformations (Leading-tone exchange, Parallel, Relative) that move smoothly between triads by changing one note by a half or whole step
column5: Roman numeral analysis in reverse
column6: A substitute for the circle of fifths
column7: Transformations exclusive to augmented chords
column11: Neo-Riemannian operations: P (parallel: C major↔C minor, changes 3rd), R (relative: C major↔A minor, changes 5th), L (leading-tone exchange: C major↔E minor, changes root). These transformations generate chromatic voice leading with minimal pitch movement — used to analyze Romantic chromatic harmony and film music.
column12: Include a voicing challenge: create three distinct jazz voicings (rootless, drop 2, upper structure) of this chord type for piano.
column14: Herb Pomeroy, Line Writing (Berklee). Upper structure voicings: Herbie Hancock transcriptions (Hal Leonard). Inner Urge: Joe Henderson analysis.

column3: What is an 'augmented 6th chord' (specifically the French Augmented 6th)?
column4: A 4-note chord built with both a major 2nd and augmented 4th above the bass: ♭6-1-2-♯4 (in major/minor) — resolves to V
column5: A chord with an augmented 5th and 6th
column6: A chord used only in French Baroque music
column7: An augmented triad with an added 6th
column11: The French Aug6 (in C minor: A♭-C-D-F#) has the augmented 6th interval (A♭ to F#) plus the 2nd degree (D), giving it 4 distinctive notes. The Aug6 expands outward to the octave on G (V). Unlike the Italian (3 notes) and German (4 notes, includes 5th), the French version includes the major 2nd from the bass.
column12: Add a scale-matching task: list all scales from which this chord type can be derived and name the mode each represents.
column14: Reginald Morris, Harmony (Oxford). Alan Belkin, Tonal Counterpoint. Berklee Online Advanced Jazz Harmony (Bertrand Amar). Film score voicing: John Williams.

column3: What is 'polytonal' harmony?
column4: Music that establishes two or more tonal centers simultaneously (e.g. C major and F# major together)
column5: Harmony using multiple chord types
column6: Harmony moving through many keys sequentially
column7: Atonal harmony that sounds like it has a key
column11: Polytonality superimposes two or more key centers. Stravinsky's 'Petrushka' famously combines C major and F# major triads (the 'Petrushka chord'). Bitonality is the most common form (two keys). Bartók and Milhaud used polytonality extensively in the early 20th century.
column12: Include a spectral note: explain why this chord quality sounds the way it does in terms of the interval ratios between its tones.
column14: Dmitri Tymoczko, A Geometry of Music (Oxford). Pitch-class set theory applied to jazz chord voicings. New Grove Dictionary of Jazz — harmonic analysis.

column3: What is a 'cluster chord'?
column4: A chord built from several adjacent (chromatic or diatonic) notes played simultaneously, creating dense dissonance
column5: A chord built from a scale
column6: A 3-note chord in second inversion
column7: A chord with all extensions played together
column11: Cluster chords pack adjacent semitones or diatonic steps together (e.g. C-D♭-D-E♭-E on the piano). Henry Cowell pioneered clusters on piano in the 1920s. Penderecki used massive orchestral clusters in works like 'Threnody for the Victims of Hiroshima.' They create highly dissonant, textural sound masses.
column12: Add a counterpoint analysis: describe how this chord functions in a four-voice chorale under common-practice voice leading rules.
column14: Craig Fraedrich, Upper Structure Triads for Piano (Hal Leonard). Jeff Beal, Chromatic Harmony and Voice Leading (Hal Leonard). Journal of Jazz Studies.

column3: What is 'spectral harmony'?
column4: Harmony derived from the acoustic overtone series of a specific pitch — using the partials as chord tones, often in microtonal tuning
column5: Harmony associated with a specific color or emotion
column6: A type of quartal harmony
column7: Harmony using the natural acoustic resonances of instruments
column11: Spectral harmony extracts chord tones from the overtone series. The first 16 partials of a low C produce: C, G, E, B♭, D, F#, A♭, B... (increasingly microtonal). Composers like Murail and Grisey built entire harmonic vocabularies from these spectra, blurring the line between timbre and harmony.
column12: Add a reharmonization exercise: substitute this chord into a standard I-vi-ii-V and describe the harmonic effect produced.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book, Ch. 16-22 (advanced harmony). George Russell, Lydian Chromatic Concept. Vincent Persichetti, Twentieth Century Harmony.

column3: What is 'secundal harmony'?
column4: Harmony built by stacking major or minor 2nds (seconds) instead of 3rds — creating cluster-like, dense chords
column5: Harmony using two chords simultaneously
column6: Harmony built on the 2nd scale degree
column7: A substitute for tertian harmony using fourths
column11: Secundal harmony stacks 2nds to create dense chords. Bartók used stacked 2nds extensively in his string quartets. While quartal harmony uses 4ths and tertian uses 3rds, secundal harmony creates maximally close-voiced, dissonant sonorities that can suggest tone clusters or rich chromatic textures.
column12: Include a voicing challenge: create three distinct jazz voicings (rootless, drop 2, upper structure) of this chord type for piano.
column14: Herb Pomeroy, Line Writing (Berklee). Upper structure voicings: Herbie Hancock transcriptions (Hal Leonard). Inner Urge: Joe Henderson analysis.

column3: What is 'serial (twelve-tone) harmony'?
column4: A compositional method using a fixed ordered row of all 12 pitch classes — no pitch repeats until all 12 are stated
column5: A harmony using all 12 notes simultaneously
column6: A method of reharmonizing jazz standards
column7: An extension of ii-V-I harmony
column11: Twelve-tone technique (Schoenberg, 1921) organizes all 12 pitch classes into a fixed 'row' (Grundgestalt). The row can be used in its original form, retrograde (backwards), inversion (upside down), or retrograde inversion — and all 12 transpositions of each = 48 row forms. Harmony emerges from vertical arrangements of row segments.
column12: Add a scale-matching task: list all scales from which this chord type can be derived and name the mode each represents.
column14: Reginald Morris, Harmony (Oxford). Alan Belkin, Tonal Counterpoint. Berklee Online Advanced Jazz Harmony (Bertrand Amar). Film score voicing: John Williams.

column3: What is an 'upper structure triad'?
column4: A complete triad placed above the guide tones of a dominant chord (root-3rd-♭7) to specify upper extensions — e.g. B♭ major over G7 adds 9th, 11th, and 13th
column5: A triad in first inversion
column6: A triad built on the upper notes of a chord only
column7: A polychord used in classical music exclusively
column11: Upper structure triads specify dominant chord extensions by placing a triad in the right hand over a shell voicing (root, 3rd, ♭7) in the left. Common for G7: B♭ major = 9, 11, 13; A♭ major = ♭9, ♯11, ♭13 (altered). This technique is standard in jazz piano and used by McCoy Tyner, Herbie Hancock, and Chick Corea.
column12: Include a spectral note: explain why this chord quality sounds the way it does in terms of the interval ratios between its tones.
column14: Dmitri Tymoczko, A Geometry of Music (Oxford). Pitch-class set theory applied to jazz chord voicings. New Grove Dictionary of Jazz — harmonic analysis.

column3: What is a 'quartal voicing' in jazz piano and why is it preferred?
column4: A chord voiced by stacking perfect 4ths — creates ambiguous, open quality that works over many different chord types
column5: A chord voicing using only 4-note chords
column6: The Herbie Hancock method of block chords
column7: A voicing technique from the Baroque period
column11: Quartal voicings (e.g. Bb-Eb-Ab-Db) avoid the clear major/minor definition of tertian voicings, making them harmonically flexible. A single quartal voicing can function as part of multiple different chord types depending on the bass note. McCoy Tyner used them as a signature sound in John Coltrane's quartet.
column12: Add a counterpoint analysis: describe how this chord functions in a four-voice chorale under common-practice voice leading rules.
column14: Craig Fraedrich, Upper Structure Triads for Piano (Hal Leonard). Jeff Beal, Chromatic Harmony and Voice Leading (Hal Leonard). Journal of Jazz Studies.

column3: What is 'planing' (parallel motion in chords)?
column4: Moving a chord shape in parallel motion — all voices move by the same interval — creating harmonic color without traditional voice leading
column5: A type of contrary motion
column6: Moving chords by step while the melody holds still
column7: A modulation technique using parallel keys
column11: Planing (chromatic planing or diatonic planing) moves all chord voices in the same direction by the same interval. Debussy used planing extensively ('La Cathédrale engloutie' parallel open 5ths; 'Préludes' parallel major 9th chords). It creates a rich, impressionistic sound that avoids traditional functional harmony.
column12: Add a reharmonization exercise: substitute this chord into a standard I-vi-ii-V and describe the harmonic effect produced.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book, Ch. 16-22 (advanced harmony). George Russell, Lydian Chromatic Concept. Vincent Persichetti, Twentieth Century Harmony.

column3: What is 'trichord' in post-tonal theory?
column4: A 3-note pitch-class set — the fundamental unit of analysis in Forte's pitch-class set theory
column5: A 3-note chord
column6: A scale fragment of 3 consecutive notes
column7: The first 3 notes of a tone row
column11: In pitch-class set theory, a trichord is any 3-note subset classified by its prime form (normal order packed to the left, most compact form). The 12 trichord types (3-1 through 3-12 in Forte notation) are the basic building blocks. The major triad = [0,4,7] = Forte 3-11 (same as minor triad inverted).
column12: Include a voicing challenge: create three distinct jazz voicings (rootless, drop 2, upper structure) of this chord type for piano.
column14: Herb Pomeroy, Line Writing (Berklee). Upper structure voicings: Herbie Hancock transcriptions (Hal Leonard). Inner Urge: Joe Henderson analysis.

column3: What is 'harmonic rhythm'?
column4: The rate at which chords change in a piece of music — slow harmonic rhythm creates stability, fast creates tension and momentum
column5: The rhythm played by the harmonic instruments
column6: The meter of a piece
column7: The rate at which a chord is strummed
column11: Harmonic rhythm is a crucial compositional parameter. In Bach chorales, harmonic rhythm is typically 1-2 chords per beat. In modal jazz, it slows to 1 chord per many bars. Fast harmonic rhythm (Coltrane changes: 2 chords/bar) creates extreme tension; slow harmonic rhythm (pedal points) creates space and stillness.
column12: Add a scale-matching task: list all scales from which this chord type can be derived and name the mode each represents.
column14: Reginald Morris, Harmony (Oxford). Alan Belkin, Tonal Counterpoint. Berklee Online Advanced Jazz Harmony (Bertrand Amar). Film score voicing: John Williams.

column3: What is 'Schenkerian analysis' in music theory?
column4: A method of hierarchical tonal analysis showing how surface complexity reduces to fundamental voice-leading structures (Ursatz)
column5: A Roman numeral analysis system
column6: A method for analyzing jazz harmony
column7: A form analysis system created by Schenker
column11: Heinrich Schenker's theory reveals that tonal pieces, regardless of length or complexity, reduce to a 'fundamental structure' (Ursatz): a soprano descent from scale degree 3, 5, or 8 to 1 (Urlinie) over a bass arpeggiation I-V-I (Bassbrechung). Middle-ground and foreground layers elaborate this background structure.
column12: Add a Schenkerian reduction: show how this harmonic motion maps onto a deeper structural level using a simple graphic sketch.
column14: Heinrich Schenker, Free Composition (Schirmer). Cambridge History of Western Music Theory. Dmitri Tymoczko, A Geometry of Music (Oxford).

column3: What is the 'Urlinie' in Schenkerian theory?
column4: The fundamental melodic descent in the soprano from a high structural note (3̂, 5̂, or 8̂) to the tonic (1̂) at the deepest structural level
column5: The bass voice in a chorale
column6: The opening theme of a sonata
column7: A descending chromatic line
column11: The Urlinie (fundamental line) is Schenker's concept of the deepest melodic motion in a tonal work. It descends by step through scale degrees: 3̂-2̂-1̂ (most common), 5̂-4̂-3̂-2̂-1̂, or 8̂-7̂-...-1̂. This descent, supported by the Bassbrechung (I-V-I), constitutes the Ursatz — the irreducible tonal background.
column12: Include a neo-Riemannian extension: how does this concept function when applied to chromatic mediant relationships?
column14: William Caplin, Classical Form (Oxford). Aldwell & Schachter, Harmony and Voice Leading, 4th ed. Journal of Music Theory — analytical articles.

column3: What is 'common-tone modulation'?
column4: A modulation that holds one or more notes common to both keys while respelling them in the new harmonic context
column5: A modulation using the pivot chord method
column6: A modulation moving through the circle of 5ths
column7: A modulation using chromatic alteration only
column11: Common-tone modulation sustains a note (or notes) shared between old and new keys while the harmony shifts. E.g. moving from E major to C major: the common tone G# (in E major) = A♭ (in C minor) — the enharmonic respelling creates the pivot. Used extensively in Schubert ('Wanderer Fantasy') and Liszt.
column12: Add an acoustic explanation: explain why this harmonic motion is acoustically logical using the overtone series as a reference.
column14: Richard Cohn, Audacious Euphony: Chromaticism and the Triad's Second Nature (Oxford). Journal of the American Musicological Society.

column3: What is 'enharmonic modulation' via the German Augmented 6th chord?
column4: The German Aug6 chord is enharmonically identical to a dominant 7th chord, allowing modulation between distant keys by reinterpreting which chord it is
column5: A modulation using the whole tone scale
column6: A modulation by reinterpreting diminished 7th chords only
column7: A modulation via a shared tritone
column11: The German Aug6 (e.g. A♭-C-E♭-F# in C minor) is enharmonically identical to E♭7 (E♭-G-B♭-D♭=C#). By respelling F# as G♭ (♭7 of E♭7), the chord becomes E♭7 and resolves to A♭ major — a modulation from C minor to A♭ major via enharmonic reinterpretation. This was a favorite Romantic modulation technique.
column12: Include a comparative historical analysis: show how Bach, Brahms, and Bill Evans each use this concept differently.
column14: Patrick McCreless, Contemporary Music Theory. Dmitri Tymoczko chord geometry. Cambridge Handbook of Music Theory. Neo-Riemannian Operations.

column3: What is 'tonal pairing' in Schenkerian analysis?
column4: Two competing tonal areas (usually major and minor, or two keys a 3rd apart) that create large-scale tonal ambiguity and dialogue throughout a work
column5: Two keys used simultaneously
column6: A pair of themes in contrasting keys
column7: The relationship between tonic and dominant
column11: Tonal pairing (Robert Bailey's concept) describes large-scale tonal ambiguity where two tonal areas compete for primacy. Wagner's 'Tristan und Isolde' pairs A minor and C major throughout. The pairing creates long-range tension resolved only at the work's very end. Related to Schoenberg's concept of 'monotonality.'
column12: Add a film scoring application: identify a specific film score moment where this harmonic device creates a defined emotional effect.
column14: Walter Everett, The Foundations of Rock (Oxford) — harmonic function in pop. Journal of the Society for Music Theory. Susan McClary, Feminine Endings.

column3: What is 'prolongation' in Schenkerian theory?
column4: The elaboration of a single harmony over an extended time through neighbor notes, passing tones, arpeggiation, and other techniques — making it 'last longer' structurally
column5: A long sustained note
column6: A section that delays resolution
column7: A pedal point in the bass
column11: Prolongation is how Schenker explains musical length — a I chord can be 'prolonged' through a V that's a passing chord (not structurally significant), returning to I. An entire development section may prolong the dominant. Understanding prolongation is key to distinguishing surface chord changes from structural harmonies.
column12: Add a Schenkerian reduction: show how this harmonic motion maps onto a deeper structural level using a simple graphic sketch.
column14: Heinrich Schenker, Free Composition (Schirmer). Cambridge History of Western Music Theory. Dmitri Tymoczko, A Geometry of Music (Oxford).

column3: What is 'hexatonic pole' relationship (neo-Riemannian)?
column4: Two triads that share no common tones but are connected through an L-P-L or P-L-P transformation chain — e.g. C major and A♭ minor
column5: Two triads a tritone apart
column6: Two triads sharing all notes except the root
column7: The dominant and subdominant relationship
column11: In neo-Riemannian theory, hexatonic poles are the most 'distant' triads within a hexatonic cycle — sharing no common tones. C major and A♭ minor (hexatonic poles) are connected by a L-P-L chain. Their voice leading moves each note by a half step. This relationship creates uncanny, 'otherworldly' Romantic harmonic progressions (Schubert, Brahms, film music).
column12: Include a neo-Riemannian extension: how does this concept function when applied to chromatic mediant relationships?
column14: William Caplin, Classical Form (Oxford). Aldwell & Schachter, Harmony and Voice Leading, 4th ed. Journal of Music Theory — analytical articles.

column3: What is 'functional harmony' and how does it contrast with 'non-functional' harmony?
column4: Functional: chords have specific tonic/subdominant/dominant roles and progress logically; Non-functional: chords are chosen for color, voice leading, or texture without directional pull
column5: Functional uses only Roman numerals; non-functional does not
column6: Functional harmony has a time signature; non-functional does not
column7: They are the same — just different names
column11: In functional harmony (common practice, jazz), chords have roles (T, SD, D) and progress with directional logic. Non-functional harmony (Debussy, impressionism, modal, spectral) uses chords for their color and voice leading qualities without implying a tonal goal. Debussy said chords had the right to exist independently, like colors on a canvas.
column12: Add an acoustic explanation: explain why this harmonic motion is acoustically logical using the overtone series as a reference.
column14: Richard Cohn, Audacious Euphony: Chromaticism and the Triad's Second Nature (Oxford). Journal of the American Musicological Society.

column3: What is 'chromaticism' and how did it evolve from the Baroque to the late Romantic period?
column4: The use of chromatic (non-diatonic) notes, chords, and modulations — increasing from rare ornamental use in Baroque to pervasive harmonic language dissolving tonality by Wagner/Liszt
column5: A term for playing in chromatic scales only
column6: The use of half steps in a melody only
column7: A type of modulation
column11: Baroque chromaticism was ornamental (chromatic passing tones, Neapolitan chords). Classical expanded to secondary dominants and remote modulations. Romantic chromaticism (Schubert, Chopin, Liszt, Wagner) increasingly weakened tonal centers through enharmonic reinterpretation and extended chords. Wagner's 'Tristan' (1865) pushed chromaticism to the point of near-tonality dissolution, paving the way for 20th-century atonality.
column12: Include a comparative historical analysis: show how Bach, Brahms, and Bill Evans each use this concept differently.
column14: Patrick McCreless, Contemporary Music Theory. Dmitri Tymoczko chord geometry. Cambridge Handbook of Music Theory. Neo-Riemannian Operations.

column3: What is 'counterpoint' and what are the 5 species?
column4: The art of combining independent melodic lines; species: 1st (note against note), 2nd (2 notes against 1), 3rd (4 against 1), 4th (syncopated/suspensions), 5th (florid, combining all)
column5: The art of writing chord progressions; 5 types: major, minor, diminished, augmented, dominant
column6: Writing 5 separate melodies simultaneously
column7: A method of Roman numeral analysis
column11: Species counterpoint (Fux, 'Gradus ad Parnassum,' 1725) trains voice leading through 5 progressively complex species: 1st (1:1), 2nd (2:1), 3rd (4:1), 4th (syncopations/suspensions), 5th (free/florid, combining all). It's the foundation of traditional voice leading and part-writing pedagogy, from conservatories to Schoenberg's teaching.
column12: Add a film scoring application: identify a specific film score moment where this harmonic device creates a defined emotional effect.
column14: Walter Everett, The Foundations of Rock (Oxford) — harmonic function in pop. Journal of the Society for Music Theory. Susan McClary, Feminine Endings.

column3: What is the 'double period' in phrase structure?
column4: A 16-bar unit of two periods (8+8 bars) where the first period ends with a half cadence and the second with an authentic cadence
column5: A period that lasts twice as long as expected
column6: A 16-bar jazz form
column7: A period with two contrasting themes
column11: A period = 8 bars (antecedent 4 bars + consequent 4 bars). A double period extends this to 16 bars: two periods where the first consequent ends with a HC (keeping tension alive) and only the second consequent ends with a PAC (resolving). Common in Classical-era sonata expositions and themes.
column12: Add a Schenkerian reduction: show how this harmonic motion maps onto a deeper structural level using a simple graphic sketch.
column14: Heinrich Schenker, Free Composition (Schirmer). Cambridge History of Western Music Theory. Dmitri Tymoczko, A Geometry of Music (Oxford).

column3: What is 'parsimonious voice leading' in neo-Riemannian theory?
column4: Voice leading where each voice moves by the smallest possible interval (0, 1, or 2 semitones) between chords
column5: Voice leading with no more than 4 voices
column6: Voice leading that avoids all dissonance
column7: Voice leading derived from Parsifal by Wagner
column11: Parsimonious voice leading (Richard Cohn) minimizes voice movement between chords. Triads connected by P, L, or R operations change only one note and by only 1-2 semitones. This principle explains the appeal of chromatic mediant progressions in Romantic music — they sound smooth despite moving to harmonically distant chords.
column12: Include a neo-Riemannian extension: how does this concept function when applied to chromatic mediant relationships?
column14: William Caplin, Classical Form (Oxford). Aldwell & Schachter, Harmony and Voice Leading, 4th ed. Journal of Music Theory — analytical articles.

column3: What is 'Elliott Carter's metric modulation' and how does it work in practice?
column4: A compositional technique where the new tempo is derived from a subdivided or augmented value of the previous tempo — creating mathematically precise tempo changes
column5: A gradual ritardando followed by a new time signature
column6: A sudden tempo change without rhythmic preparation
column7: A type of polyrhythm between two instruments
column11: Carter's metric modulation: e.g. at ♩=120, triplet eighths pulse at 80 BPM. If the composer designates those triplet eighths as the new quarter note, tempo becomes ♩=80 — a 3:2 ratio. This creates seamless yet structurally significant tempo changes. 'String Quartet No. 1' (1951) pioneered this systematic use.
column12: Add an academic resource citation for deeper independent study.
column14: Dmitri Tymoczko, A Geometry of Music (Oxford University Press). Heinrich Schenker, Free Composition (Schirmer). Journal of Music Theory (Yale).

column3: What is 'Messiaen's theory of non-retrogradable rhythms'?
column4: Rhythmic palindromes — rhythms that read the same forwards and backwards (e.g. 3-1-2-1-3) — one of his 'modes of limited transposition' equivalents in rhythm
column5: Rhythms that cannot be played backwards without sounding different
column6: Rhythms derived from Hindu tala systems
column7: Irregular rhythms that resist any regular subdivision
column11: Messiaen identified 'non-retrogradable rhythms' (rhythmic palindromes) as one of his fundamental compositional principles. Like his modes of limited transposition, they represent a kind of rhythmic symmetry — the retrograde is identical to the original. He saw them as embodying cosmic, timeless qualities.
column12: Include a historical analysis showing how this concept evolved across musical eras.
column14: Richard Cohn, Audacious Euphony (Oxford). Cambridge History of Western Music Theory. The MIT Press Music Theory Sourcebook.

column3: What is 'aksak' rhythm in ethnomusicology?
column4: Asymmetric meter in Balkan and Turkish folk music built from uneven groupings of 2s and 3s (e.g. 2+2+3) felt as a limping pulse
column5: A type of African polyrhythm
column6: A Greek word for syncopation
column7: A slow, even rhythm used in ritual music
column11: Aksak' (Turkish: 'limping') describes the asymmetric meters of Balkan and Anatolian folk music — rhythms like 7/8 (2+2+3), 9/8 (2+2+2+3), 11/8. Bartók transcribed hundreds of such rhythms from folk music, bringing them into Western contemporary composition.
column12: Add a composition exercise requiring mastery of this concept in a real musical context.
column14: Aldwell, Schachter & Cadwallader, Harmony and Voice Leading, 4th ed. (Cengage). Arnold Schoenberg, Structural Functions of Harmony (Norton).

column3: What is 'rhythmic augmentation and diminution'?
column4: Augmentation: multiplying all note values (slowing the rhythm); Diminution: dividing all values (speeding it up) — used in fugues and variations to develop themes
column5: Augmentation adds notes to a rhythm; diminution removes them
column6: Two types of tuplets
column7: A form of articulation
column11: Rhythmic augmentation doubles (or multiplies) note values — a half note becomes a whole note. Diminution halves (or divides) them — a quarter becomes an eighth. These are classical development techniques used in fugues (Bach uses both in the Well-Tempered Clavier) and in Romantic thematic development.
column12: Include a cross-disciplinary connection to acoustics, mathematics, or music cognition.
column14: Music Theory Online (MTO) — peer-reviewed journal (societymusictheory.org). Journal of the American Musicological Society. Cambridge Music Handbooks.

column3: What is a 'rhythmic ostinato'?
column4: A rhythmic pattern that repeats persistently throughout a section or piece, often in the bass or percussion
column5: A tempo marking
column6: An improvised rhythmic figure
column7: A name for 4/4 time in some traditions
column11: An ostinato is any persistently repeated pattern. A rhythmic ostinato repeats the same rhythmic cell (not necessarily the same pitches) throughout. Stravinsky used relentless rhythmic ostinati in 'The Rite of Spring.' Steve Reich's phase music builds entirely from rhythmic ostinati gradually shifting out of alignment.
column12: Add a pedagogical note: how would you explain this concept to a complete beginner?
column14: Paul Hindemith, The Craft of Musical Composition (Schott). Vincent Persichetti, Twentieth Century Harmony (Norton). Reginald Morris, Harmony (Oxford).

column3: What is 'phasing' as used by Steve Reich?
column4: A minimalist technique where two or more identical patterns gradually shift out of rhythmic alignment by tiny increments, creating evolving composite rhythms
column5: Using delay effects in electronic music
column6: Two instruments playing at different tempos
column7: A gradual ritardando in one voice only
column11: Reich's phasing technique (from 'Piano Phase,' 'Violin Phase') starts two performers playing identical patterns in unison, then one gradually accelerates until they are one sixteenth note out of phase, creating new composite rhythms. The piece cycles through all phase relationships before realigning. It's a landmark of minimalism.
column12: Add an academic resource citation for deeper independent study.
column14: Dmitri Tymoczko, A Geometry of Music (Oxford University Press). Heinrich Schenker, Free Composition (Schirmer). Journal of Music Theory (Yale).

column3: What is 'talea' in isorhythmic motets?
column4: A repeating rhythmic pattern in medieval isorhythmic motets — the rhythmic pattern (talea) cycles independently of the repeating pitch pattern (color)
column5: A type of Indian tala
column6: A Baroque ornament
column7: A rhythmic palindrome
column11: In 14th-century isorhythmic motets (Philippe de Vitry, Machaut), the tenor voice uses a repeating rhythmic pattern (talea) and a repeating melodic pattern (color) of different lengths. Their independent cycling creates complex, long-range structural relationships. Messiaen revived the concept of cyclic rhythmic/pitch independence in the 20th century.
column12: Include a historical analysis showing how this concept evolved across musical eras.
column14: Richard Cohn, Audacious Euphony (Oxford). Cambridge History of Western Music Theory. The MIT Press Music Theory Sourcebook.

column3: What is 'tala' in Indian classical music?
column4: A rhythmic cycle of fixed duration (measured in beats called matras) that provides the metric framework for improvisation
column5: A type of scale
column6: A type of ornament
column7: A drone instrument
column11: Tala is the rhythmic framework of Indian classical music — a cycle of specific beat groups (vibhags) measured in matras (beats). Common talas: Teentaal (16 matras: 4+4+4+4), Ektaal (12), Rupak (7: 3+2+2). The tabla plays the tala while soloists improvise, always returning to 'sam' (beat 1) together.
column12: Add a composition exercise requiring mastery of this concept in a real musical context.
column14: Aldwell, Schachter & Cadwallader, Harmony and Voice Leading, 4th ed. (Cengage). Arnold Schoenberg, Structural Functions of Harmony (Norton).

column3: What is 'stratification' in rhythm?
column4: Layering multiple independent rhythmic streams simultaneously that remain distinct rather than blending — associated with Stravinsky
column5: Gradually adding voices to a rhythmic texture
column6: A type of polyrhythm in African drumming
column7: The gradual introduction of new rhythmic layers
column11: Rhythmic stratification (Pieter van den Toorn's analysis of Stravinsky) describes how Stravinsky layers completely independent rhythmic streams that don't integrate into a single composite rhythm. Each layer maintains its own metric identity. This gives Stravinsky's music its distinctive, mechanistic, puzzle-like rhythmic quality.
column12: Include a cross-disciplinary connection to acoustics, mathematics, or music cognition.
column14: Music Theory Online (MTO) — peer-reviewed journal (societymusictheory.org). Journal of the American Musicological Society. Cambridge Music Handbooks.

column3: What is 'proportional notation'?
column4: A type of graphic score notation where horizontal space represents time proportionally rather than using standard note values and stems
column5: A notation system using proportional rhythms only
column6: A method of notating polyrhythms
column7: Bartók's notation for complex tuplets
column11: Proportional (or 'space-time') notation uses horizontal distance to indicate duration — further right = later, wider spacing = longer. Used by Ligeti, Brown, and others in experimental scores where precise rhythmic notation would be impractical or overly prescriptive. It gives performers interpretive freedom in timing.
column12: Add a pedagogical note: how would you explain this concept to a complete beginner?
column14: Paul Hindemith, The Craft of Musical Composition (Schott). Vincent Persichetti, Twentieth Century Harmony (Norton). Reginald Morris, Harmony (Oxford).

column3: What is the 'Golden Ratio' (φ = 1.618) and how has it been used in musical form and rhythm?
column4: A mathematical proportion found in nature (φ ≈ 1.618) — composers like Bartók structured climaxes, phrase lengths, and rhythmic values at golden ratio proportions
column5: A type of interval ratio
column6: A tuning system based on φ
column7: A rhythmic augmentation ratio
column11: The Golden Ratio divides a length so that the ratio of the whole to the larger part equals the ratio of the larger to the smaller (both ≈ 1.618). Ernő Lendvai's analysis of Bartók found climaxes and structural points placed at golden ratio proportions in works like 'Music for Strings, Percussion and Celesta.' Whether intentional or intuitive, the golden ratio is a compelling structural principle.
column12: Add an academic resource citation for deeper independent study.
column14: Dmitri Tymoczko, A Geometry of Music (Oxford University Press). Heinrich Schenker, Free Composition (Schirmer). Journal of Music Theory (Yale).

column3: What is 'complex meter' or 'changing meter'?
column4: Music that changes time signature frequently (sometimes every bar) — creating an irregular, unpredictable metric surface
column5: Music that uses only odd time signatures
column6: Music without any discernible pulse
column7: A type of compound meter with changing subdivisions
column11: Changing meter (found in Stravinsky, Bartók, Messiaen, and Hindemith) shifts the time signature bar by bar or phrase by phrase. 'The Rite of Spring' famously has rapidly changing meters: 4/4, 3/4, 5/4, 3/16, etc. This creates the illusion of a flexible, speech-like, or dance-like unpredictable pulse.
column12: Include a historical analysis showing how this concept evolved across musical eras.
column14: Richard Cohn, Audacious Euphony (Oxford). Cambridge History of Western Music Theory. The MIT Press Music Theory Sourcebook.

column3: What is 'Nancarrow's player piano studies' and why are they significant rhythmically?
column4: Conlon Nancarrow programmed player pianos to perform polytemporal music — multiple simultaneous independent tempos impossible for human performers — at extreme speeds
column5: A series of studies in changing meter
column6: A set of etudes in unusual time signatures
column7: Studies in metric modulation for two pianos
column11: Conlon Nancarrow (1912-1997) punched rolls for player pianos to perform rhythmically superhuman music — e.g. Study No. 21 uses a tempo ratio of 3:√2 between two pianos. He explored polytemporal canons where each voice has a different and continuously changing tempo ratio. His work prefigured computer music and remains unperformable by humans.
column12: Add a composition exercise requiring mastery of this concept in a real musical context.
column14: Aldwell, Schachter & Cadwallader, Harmony and Voice Leading, 4th ed. (Cengage). Arnold Schoenberg, Structural Functions of Harmony (Norton).

column3: What is 'developing variation' (Schoenberg's concept)?
column4: A compositional technique where a motivic kernel is continuously transformed throughout a piece — each variation 'develops' out of the previous one without literal repetition
column5: Theme and variations form
column6: A form using a 12-tone row
column7: A Baroque variation technique
column11: Schoenberg's 'developing variation' (Durchführende Variation) describes how Brahms transforms a basic motivic shape throughout a work — changing its intervals, rhythm, or register while maintaining its essential identity. It's distinguished from theme-and-variations (which keeps the theme recognizable) by more continuous, organic transformation.
column12: Add an academic resource citation for deeper independent study.
column14: Dmitri Tymoczko, A Geometry of Music (Oxford University Press). Heinrich Schenker, Free Composition (Schirmer). Journal of Music Theory (Yale).

column3: What is 'moment form' (Stockhausen's concept)?
column4: A compositional form of self-contained 'moments' with no hierarchical sequence — each moment is complete in itself, the order can vary
column5: A form based on key moments in a narrative
column6: A type of rondo form
column7: A through-composed form without repetition
column11: Karlheinz Stockhausen's 'Moment Form' (Momentform, from 'Kontakte' and 'Momente') builds music from self-contained 'moments' — sections complete in themselves with no beginning/middle/end relationship. The form resists hierarchical narrative, creating an experience of perpetual present rather than directed development.
column12: Include a historical analysis showing how this concept evolved across musical eras.
column14: Richard Cohn, Audacious Euphony (Oxford). Cambridge History of Western Music Theory. The MIT Press Music Theory Sourcebook.

column3: What is an 'arch form' (ABCBA)?
column4: A palindromic musical structure where sections appear in reverse order after a central point — e.g. A B C B A
column5: A form using only ascending melodic arches
column6: A Baroque binary form
column7: A form with 5 identical sections
column11: Arch form (ABCBA) creates a palindrome at the formal level — material returns in reverse order after the midpoint. Bartók used arch form in several string quartets and the 'Music for Strings, Percussion and Celesta.' The form creates large-scale symmetry and balance, with C as the formal and often emotional apex.
column12: Add a composition exercise requiring mastery of this concept in a real musical context.
column14: Aldwell, Schachter & Cadwallader, Harmony and Voice Leading, 4th ed. (Cengage). Arnold Schoenberg, Structural Functions of Harmony (Norton).

column3: What is 'open form' in 20th-century music?
column4: A form where the performer or conductor determines the order and/or selection of sections — the score provides material but not a fixed sequence
column5: A form without time signatures
column6: A form with no repeats
column7: A form that ends without a cadence
column11: Open form (or 'mobile form') allows performers to choose the order of sections. Earle Brown, Stockhausen ('Klavierstück XI'), and Boulez ('Third Piano Sonata') gave performers multiple paths through the material. This challenges the traditional composer-as-sole-author model and embraces performance-time decision-making.
column12: Include a cross-disciplinary connection to acoustics, mathematics, or music cognition.
column14: Music Theory Online (MTO) — peer-reviewed journal (societymusictheory.org). Journal of the American Musicological Society. Cambridge Music Handbooks.

column3: What is 'process music' (minimalism)?
column4: Music built on a single, clearly stated process that slowly transforms over a long duration — associated with Reich, Glass, and Riley
column5: Music about industrial processes
column6: Music that becomes gradually louder
column7: Music using only one chord
column11: Process music (Steve Reich's essay 'Music as a Gradual Process,' 1968) makes the compositional process audible and transparent. Works like 'Piano Phase' or 'Music for 18 Musicians' derive their form entirely from a single process (phasing, additive rhythm) unfolding in real time. The listener hears the process, not hidden structure.
column12: Add a pedagogical note: how would you explain this concept to a complete beginner?
column14: Paul Hindemith, The Craft of Musical Composition (Schott). Vincent Persichetti, Twentieth Century Harmony (Norton). Reginald Morris, Harmony (Oxford).

column3: What is 'spectral form' and how does it differ from traditional forms?
column4: Form derived from the evolution of a sound's overtone spectrum over time — the macro-structure mirrors the micro-structure of acoustic timbre
column5: A form using only spectrum analysis tools
column6: A term for through-composed music
column7: A form that changes timbre every section
column11: In spectral music (Grisey, Murail), form emerges from the transformation of spectral states rather than from thematic development. 'Partiels' by Grisey (1975) begins with the dense spectrum of a trombone's low E, then slowly 'decays' toward silence — the macro-form mirrors the acoustic decay of the original sound.
column12: Add an academic resource citation for deeper independent study.
column14: Dmitri Tymoczko, A Geometry of Music (Oxford University Press). Heinrich Schenker, Free Composition (Schirmer). Journal of Music Theory (Yale).

column3: What is 'stratified form' in Stravinsky?
column4: Layers of simultaneous musical objects (blocks) that are juxtaposed, repeated, and interleaved without developmental transition
column5: A form using multiple simultaneous melodies
column6: A term for polyphonic music
column7: A type of rondo form with variable refrains
column11: Stravinsky's 'block form' (Jonathan Cross's term: 'stratified, interlock, and juxtapose') juxtaposes distinct musical blocks — different in texture, key, tempo, and character — without traditional transition or development. 'The Rite of Spring' is the seminal example. This anti-developmental approach influenced Messiaen and later minimalism.
column12: Include a historical analysis showing how this concept evolved across musical eras.
column14: Richard Cohn, Audacious Euphony (Oxford). Cambridge History of Western Music Theory. The MIT Press Music Theory Sourcebook.

column3: What is 'tonal pairing' in large-scale formal analysis?
column4: A long-range tonal relationship where two keys (often a 3rd apart) compete for dominance throughout a work — resolving only at the end
column5: Two themes in contrasting keys
column6: A modulation to the parallel minor
column7: The alternation of tonic and dominant areas
column11: Robert Bailey's 'tonal pairing' concept (from Wagner analysis) describes a large-scale formal process where two tonal areas (e.g. B minor/D major in 'Tristan') create sustained ambiguity. The resolution to one definitive tonic at the work's close provides structural closure. Related to Schoenberg's concept of 'monotonality' versus 'extended tonality.'
column12: Add a composition exercise requiring mastery of this concept in a real musical context.
column14: Aldwell, Schachter & Cadwallader, Harmony and Voice Leading, 4th ed. (Cengage). Arnold Schoenberg, Structural Functions of Harmony (Norton).

column3: What is the 'sonata-rondo' hybrid form?
column4: A 7-section form (A-B-A-C-A-B-A) combining rondo's recurring refrain with sonata's key scheme — B returns in the tonic in the second appearance
column5: A rondo form with developmental episodes
column6: A sonata form using a rondo theme
column7: A form with both an exposition and a refrain
column11: Sonata-rondo (common in Beethoven and Haydn finales) combines rondo (recurring A) with sonata (B section first in dominant, then in tonic at recapitulation). Structure: A (tonic) – B (dominant) – A – C (development) – A – B (tonic) – A. The retransition and B's tonal correction from dominant to tonic distinguish it from pure rondo.
column12: Include a cross-disciplinary connection to acoustics, mathematics, or music cognition.
column14: Music Theory Online (MTO) — peer-reviewed journal (societymusictheory.org). Journal of the American Musicological Society. Cambridge Music Handbooks.

column3: What is 'motivic saturation'?
column4: A compositional technique where a single motif pervades every layer of the texture simultaneously — melody, bass, harmony, and inner voices all state the motif
column5: Using a motif more than 3 times
column6: A type of imitative counterpoint
column7: A form of repetition in minimalism
column11: Motivic saturation (found in late Beethoven and Brahms) creates tight formal unity by embedding a single motif in all voices simultaneously. In the finale of Beethoven's Op. 131 String Quartet, the opening motif saturates every part at the climax. It creates extraordinary textural density where every voice participates in the motivic argument.
column12: Add a pedagogical note: how would you explain this concept to a complete beginner?
column14: Paul Hindemith, The Craft of Musical Composition (Schott). Vincent Persichetti, Twentieth Century Harmony (Norton). Reginald Morris, Harmony (Oxford).

column3: What is 'textural form'?
column4: A large-scale formal process articulated by changes in texture (density, register, instrumentation) rather than thematic or tonal contrast
column5: A form defined by the types of chords used
column6: A form using only one instrument
column7: A type of through-composed form
column11: Textural form (found in Ligeti, Xenakis, Penderecki) creates large-scale shape through changes in texture: from dense to sparse, high to low, complex to simple. In Ligeti's 'Atmosphères' there are no themes or chord progressions — the entire 10-minute arc is defined by textural evolution alone.
column12: Add an academic resource citation for deeper independent study.
column14: Dmitri Tymoczko, A Geometry of Music (Oxford University Press). Heinrich Schenker, Free Composition (Schirmer). Journal of Music Theory (Yale).

column3: What is a 'retransition'?
column4: A passage leading back from the development to the recapitulation in sonata form — typically building on the dominant in preparation for the tonic's return
column5: A coda at the end of a sonata
column6: A bridge between two themes
column7: The return of the second theme
column11: The retransition is one of the most dramatic moments in sonata form — after the development's tonal instability, the retransition arrives on the dominant (V), often with a dominant pedal, building anticipation for the tonic's return at the recapitulation. Beethoven used retransitions as climactic moments of maximum tension and expectation.
column12: Include a historical analysis showing how this concept evolved across musical eras.
column14: Richard Cohn, Audacious Euphony (Oxford). Cambridge History of Western Music Theory. The MIT Press Music Theory Sourcebook.

column3: What is 'Coltrane's Giant Steps' harmonic system and how does it function?
column4: A cycle of major 3rds dividing the octave into 3 equal parts (B – G – E♭), with each tonal center approached by its own ii-V — creating extremely rapid modulation
column5: A series of ii-V-I progressions in one key
column6: A system using tritone substitutions exclusively
column7: A harmonic system based on the whole tone scale
column11: In 'Giant Steps' (1960), Coltrane cycles through 3 tonal centers a major 3rd apart (B, G, E♭), each preceded by its dominant. The motion completes in 2 bars: BΔ | D7 GΔ | Bb7 EbΔ | Am7 D7 | GΔ... Each chord lasts only 2 beats, demanding that improvisers navigate rapid key centers in real time.
column12: Add a Levine cross-reference: cite the specific chapter in Mark Levine's The Jazz Theory Book that covers this concept in depth.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book (complete reference). George Russell, Lydian Chromatic Concept (Concept Publishing). Berklee Advanced Jazz Harmony.

column3: What is 'free jazz' and how does it approach form and harmony?
column4: An approach to jazz from the late 1950s onwards that abandons fixed chord changes, meters, and forms — improvisation is completely spontaneous and collective
column5: Jazz without written music
column6: Jazz played without a rhythm section
column7: Jazz in unusual time signatures only
column11: Free jazz (Ornette Coleman, Cecil Taylor, Albert Ayler, late Coltrane) abandons the fixed harmonic and formal constraints of bebop: no chord changes, no fixed meter, no predetermined structure. 'Free Jazz' (Coleman, 1960) with double quartet launched the movement. It prioritizes expression, timbre, and collective improvisation over harmonic grammar.
column12: Include a historical development note: trace how this concept evolved from swing → bebop → post-bop → contemporary jazz.
column14: David Liebman, A Chromatic Approach to Jazz Harmony and Melody (Advance Music). Barry Harris workshop materials. Berklee Jazz Piano Voicings.

column3: What is 'harmonic superimposition' in jazz improvisation?
column4: Playing lines based on a chord or scale not in the written chord changes — deliberately creating 'outside' sounds that eventually resolve
column5: Playing two chord symbols simultaneously
column6: A method of sight-reading jazz charts
column7: A technique exclusive to piano voicings
column11: Harmonic superimposition temporarily plays over a different harmonic area than written. E.g. over Cmaj7, superimposing E♭ major (a minor 3rd above) creates a Lydian-type sound with ♭3, ♭5, and ♭6 tensions. Coltrane and Woody Shaw mastered this technique — going 'out' then resolving 'in' for dramatic effect.
column12: Add a transcription analysis: identify how a named jazz master (Evans, Coltrane, Monk) uses this concept in a specific recording.
column14: Paul Bley and David Lee, Stopping Time (Véhicule Press). ECM Records catalog for advanced jazz harmony examples. Journal of Jazz Studies (Rutgers).

column3: What is 'pentatonic superimposition' in modern jazz?
column4: Using pentatonic scales not derived from the current chord to create tensions — e.g. Db pentatonic over Cmaj7 creates 4 alteration tensions
column5: Playing only pentatonic scales in a solo
column6: Using two pentatonic scales simultaneously
column7: A method of avoiding pentatonic clichés
column11: Pentatonic superimposition (McCoy Tyner's innovation) plays a pentatonic scale rooted elsewhere than the chord. E.g. B♭ minor pentatonic over a G7 chord = G B♭ C D F — tensions ♭3, 11, 5, ♭7 — a Mixolydian sound. E♭ major pentatonic over Cmaj7 = E♭ G A♭ B♭ D — all tensions. Pentatonics are easy to hear but provide sophisticated harmonic information.
column12: Include a cross-genre application: how has this jazz concept influenced contemporary pop, R&B, neo-soul, or film scoring?
column14: Jerry Bergonzi, Inside Improvisation Series (Advance Music). Ted Pease, Jazz Composition (Berklee). Transcription of Herbie Hancock or Chick Corea recommended.

column3: What is the 'Lydian Chromatic Concept' (George Russell)?
column4: A theory that Lydian (with raised 4th) is the tonic of all tonal gravity rather than Ionian — based on the overtone series — providing a scale-based system for improvisation before chord-scale theory
column5: A theory that all chords are based on Lydian
column6: A type of reharmonization system
column7: A method of ear training using the Lydian mode
column11: George Russell's 'Lydian Chromatic Concept of Tonal Organization' (1953) argued that the Lydian scale (major + ♯4) better represents the natural acoustic properties of a tonic than Ionian, since the 4th partial (G over C) produces G#/A♭ rather than G. The concept influenced Miles Davis and Bill Evans and was a precursor to chord-scale theory.
column12: Add a curriculum sequencing note: at which stage of jazz education should this concept be introduced and what prerequisites apply?
column14: Matt Otto, Melodic Minor (mattotto.org). Ben Monder, Modern Chord Voicings (Mel Bay). International Society for Jazz Research publications.

column3: What is 'Herbie Hancock's Maiden Voyage' harmonic approach?
column4: Using suspended dominant chords (no 3rd, add 9th and 11th) over pedal points — creating modal, floating harmony without clear ii-V resolution
column5: A system of quartal voicings over ii-V-I
column6: A reharmonization of 'Maiden Voyage' using tritone substitutions
column7: A use of Lydian Dominant over every chord
column11: Maiden Voyage' (1965) uses 4 suspended dominant chords (7sus4 with added 9ths and 13ths) cycling over pedal basses — D7sus, F7sus, E♭7sus, F7sus... There are no ii-V-I resolutions; each chord floats in modal suspension. The approach created a new kind of jazz harmony: atmospheric, impressionistic, 'Miles Davis-influenced' modal jazz.
column12: Add a Levine cross-reference: cite the specific chapter in Mark Levine's The Jazz Theory Book that covers this concept in depth.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book (complete reference). George Russell, Lydian Chromatic Concept (Concept Publishing). Berklee Advanced Jazz Harmony.

column3: What is 'multiphonics' in jazz saxophone improvisation?
column4: A technique where a saxophonist (or other wind player) produces two or more pitches simultaneously by specific fingerings and embouchure adjustments
column5: A technique for playing very fast passages
column6: A type of vibrato
column7: Playing in unison with the rhythm section
column11: Multiphonics produce two or more simultaneous pitches from a single wind instrument — a technique developed in avant-garde and free jazz contexts. Eric Dolphy and John Coltrane explored this. The resulting sounds are harmonically complex and timbral, blurring the boundary between pitch and noise — essential in free and experimental jazz.
column12: Include a historical development note: trace how this concept evolved from swing → bebop → post-bop → contemporary jazz.
column14: David Liebman, A Chromatic Approach to Jazz Harmony and Melody (Advance Music). Barry Harris workshop materials. Berklee Jazz Piano Voicings.

column3: What is 'Wes Montgomery's octave technique'?
column4: Playing melodies simultaneously in octaves (both the note and the same note an octave higher) with the thumb — creating a warm, orchestral sound
column5: Playing notes a 10th apart
column6: A fingerpicking technique on guitar
column7: A type of chord melody playing
column11: Wes Montgomery's signature technique plays melodies in parallel octaves using the thumb (not a pick) on guitar. The thumb's flesh creates a warm, dark tone, and the simultaneous octaves produce a lush, full sound. His three-level improvisational approach — single lines, octaves, block chords — became the template for jazz guitar playing.
column12: Add a transcription analysis: identify how a named jazz master (Evans, Coltrane, Monk) uses this concept in a specific recording.
column14: Paul Bley and David Lee, Stopping Time (Véhicule Press). ECM Records catalog for advanced jazz harmony examples. Journal of Jazz Studies (Rutgers).

column3: What is the 'rhythm changes' and what is its harmonic structure?
column4: A 32-bar AABA form based on the chord changes of Gershwin's 'I Got Rhythm' — widely used as a vehicle for bebop improvisation
column5: A set of rhythmic patterns used in jazz
column6: A type of metric modulation
column7: A jazz standard in 5/4 time
column11: Rhythm changes (from 'I Got Rhythm,' 1930): A section = I vi ii V / I vi ii V / I7 IV iv I / ii V I. The bridge moves through dominant 7th chords a 4th apart: III7 VI7 II7 V7. Bebop musicians (Parker, Gillespie) composed dozens of 'contrafacts' (new melodies over rhythm changes). It's the most extensively used form in bebop after the blues.
column12: Include a cross-genre application: how has this jazz concept influenced contemporary pop, R&B, neo-soul, or film scoring?
column14: Jerry Bergonzi, Inside Improvisation Series (Advance Music). Ted Pease, Jazz Composition (Berklee). Transcription of Herbie Hancock or Chick Corea recommended.

column3: What is 'harmonic generalization' in jazz improvisation?
column4: Playing a single scale or approach over multiple chords that share common tones or functions rather than switching scales on every chord
column5: Playing the major scale over every chord
column6: Ignoring chord changes entirely
column7: A simplified approach to chord-scale theory
column11: Harmonic generalization reduces chord-by-chord scale switching by finding a single scale that works across multiple chords. E.g. over a ii-V-I in C (Dm7-G7-Cmaj7), all three chords can be covered with the G Mixolydian or C major scale. Jazz education emphasizes both specific chord-scale matching AND these 'generalization' strategies.
column12: Add a curriculum sequencing note: at which stage of jazz education should this concept be introduced and what prerequisites apply?
column14: Matt Otto, Melodic Minor (mattotto.org). Ben Monder, Modern Chord Voicings (Mel Bay). International Society for Jazz Research publications.

column3: What is 'bebop harmony' and how does it differ from swing harmony?
column4: Bebop extended ii-V-I with chromatic approach chords, altered dominants, and rapid harmonic motion; swing used simpler, slower-moving diatonic changes
column5: They are identical — bebop only changed rhythm
column6: Bebop used fewer chords but more complex rhythms
column7: Swing used chromatic harmony; bebop simplified it
column11: Bebop harmony (Parker, Gillespie, Monk) introduced: faster harmonic rhythm (2 chords per bar common), liberal use of altered dominant chords (7♭9, 7#9, 7♭13), tritone substitutions, chromatic approach chords, and reharmonization of jazz standards. The harmonic complexity demanded advanced theory knowledge and set jazz apart from popular music.
column12: Add a Levine cross-reference: cite the specific chapter in Mark Levine's The Jazz Theory Book that covers this concept in depth.
column14: Mark Levine, The Jazz Theory Book (complete reference). George Russell, Lydian Chromatic Concept (Concept Publishing). Berklee Advanced Jazz Harmony.

column3: What is 'post-bop' and what distinguishes it from bebop?
column4: Post-bop (mid-1950s-1960s) blended bebop harmony with modal jazz, free elements, and new compositional approaches — less formulaic than bebop, more personal and exploratory
column5: Post-bop is slower than bebop
column6: Post-bop uses simpler harmony
column7: Post-bop abandoned improvisation
column11: Post-bop (Miles Davis's second great quintet, Coltrane's quartet, Herbie Hancock, Wayne Shorter) took bebop's harmonic sophistication as a starting point, then added modal elements, metric freedom, through-composed sections, and freer collective improvisation. Wayne Shorter's compositions ('E.S.P.,' 'Infant Eyes') exemplify post-bop's ambiguous, non-functional harmonic language.
column12: Include a historical development note: trace how this concept evolved from swing → bebop → post-bop → contemporary jazz.
column14: David Liebman, A Chromatic Approach to Jazz Harmony and Melody (Advance Music). Barry Harris workshop materials. Berklee Jazz Piano Voicings.

column3: How does the 'well-temperament' system (Bach's era) differ from equal temperament?
column4: Well-temperament tunes each key slightly differently so all keys are usable but some have more 'color' than others; equal temperament tunes all semitones identically
column5: They are the same system
column6: Well-temperament is just another name for just intonation
column7: Well-temperament uses more than 12 notes per octave
column11: Well-temperament (Werckmeister, Kirnberger, Vallotti) distributes the Pythagorean comma unevenly, making all 24 keys usable (unlike mean-tone temperament) but giving each key a distinctive character. This may have been Bach's motivation for the 'Well-Tempered Clavier' — showcasing all 24 major and minor keys. Equal temperament makes all keys identical in character.
column12: Add an enharmonic key note: identify the enharmonic equivalent key and explain when each spelling is conventionally preferred.
column14: Heinrich Schenker, Free Composition (Schirmer) — tonal regions. Arnold Schoenberg, Structural Functions of Harmony (Norton) — regions chart.

column3: What is 'monotonality' as described by Schoenberg?
column4: The concept that even chromatic, modulatory music is fundamentally in one key — apparent modulations are deep-level prolongations of the home key
column5: Music that stays in one key entirely
column6: A theory that all keys reduce to C major
column7: A harmonic system using one scale throughout
column11: Schoenberg's 'monotonality' (from 'Structural Functions of Harmony') argued that chromatic modulations don't truly leave the home key — they are prolongations of regions within it. A modulation to the relative major is really just elaborating the 'relative major region' within the home key's tonal field. This explains how extended tonality can remain 'in one key' while covering vast harmonic territory.
column12: Include a Schoenberg regions reference: place this key on the harmonic regions chart relative to the home key of C major.
column14: Dmitri Tymoczko, A Geometry of Music — key distance metrics. Richard Cohn, Audacious Euphony — hexatonic poles and key relationships.

column3: What is the 'axis system' in Bartók's theory (as analyzed by Ernő Lendvai)?
column4: A tonal system where all 12 keys group into 3 functional axes (tonic, subdominant, dominant) at tritone pairs, creating harmonic substitutions at the tritone and minor 3rd
column5: A system of pitch axes for serial music
column6: A method of aligning the circle of 5ths with a grid
column7: A system of modal axis substitutions
column11: Lendvai's axis analysis of Bartók groups the 12 tonics into three functional axes: Tonic axis (C, E♭, F#, A), Subdominant axis (F, A♭, B, D), Dominant axis (G, B♭, D♭, E). Any key can substitute for another within its axis. This explains Bartók's tritone-related key areas and his use of 'tonic' functions at multiple pitch levels.
column12: Add a modulation analysis: identify three different pivot chords that could connect this key to its relative minor.
column14: Walter Piston, Harmony, 5th ed. Ch. 20-22 (chromatic modulation). Aldwell & Schachter, Harmony and Voice Leading Ch. 30-32. Journal of Music Theory.

column3: What is the 'Tonnetz' (Tone Network) in music theory?
column4: A 2D lattice of pitch classes arranged by 5ths and 3rds — used to visualize neo-Riemannian transformations and harmonic proximity
column5: A type of pitch class set
column6: A system for visualizing scales
column7: A chart of all possible chord voicings
column11: The Tonnetz (Euler, 19th century; revived by neo-Riemannian theorists) arranges pitches in a 2D lattice: horizontal axis = 5ths, diagonal axes = 3rds. Triads are triangles on the Tonnetz; P, L, R operations flip a triangle across one edge. This geometric model reveals the underlying topology of tonal harmony and voice-leading distances.
column12: Include a borrowed chord note: identify chords borrowed from the parallel minor that commonly appear in this key.
column14: David Beach, translation of Schenker's writings. Arnold Schoenberg, Theory of Harmony (UC Press). Journal of the American Musicological Society.

column3: What is a 'key area' vs. a 'tonal center' in post-tonal analysis?
column4: Key area: a region with a clear major/minor tonal center (functional harmony); tonal center: a pitch that acts as a reference point through repetition, registral prominence, or other non-functional means
column5: They are interchangeable terms
column6: A key area is one bar long; tonal center is a whole section
column7: Key area uses sharps/flats; tonal center does not
column11: In tonal music, key areas use functional harmony to establish a center. In post-tonal or modal music, a 'tonal center' (or 'centric pitch') can be established through non-functional means: pedal points, registral placement, metric accent, or frequent return. Hindemith's theory of 'root' focused on these acoustic rather than functional principles.
column12: Add a historical context note: name a landmark classical work composed in this key and discuss its expressive associations.
column14: Alan Forte, The Structure of Atonal Music (Yale). Nicolas Cook, A Guide to Music Analysis (Norton). Cambridge Music Handbooks series.

column3: What is 'pandiatonicism'?
column4: Using all 7 notes of a diatonic scale freely without functional harmonic progressions — chords are formed by accident of voice leading rather than by function
column5: Music using all 12 notes without functional harmony
column6: A term for extremely complex chromatic harmony
column7: Diatonic music without any accidentals
column11: Pandiatonicism (Slonimsky's term) uses diatonic scale notes in any combination without traditional functional voice leading — treating diatonic notes as a kind of 'white-note' chromatic language. Stravinsky used pandiatonic techniques in 'Petrushka' and the 'Symphony of Psalms.' It creates a distinctly modern, clear-toned sound that sounds 'tonal' but refuses harmonic direction.
column12: Add an enharmonic key note: identify the enharmonic equivalent key and explain when each spelling is conventionally preferred.
column14: Heinrich Schenker, Free Composition (Schirmer) — tonal regions. Arnold Schoenberg, Structural Functions of Harmony (Norton) — regions chart.

column3: What is the 'tritone' structural axis in jazz key relationships?
column4: The tritone creates a symmetry axis — keys a tritone apart can substitute for each other (tritone substitution) because they share the same tritone interval internally
column5: The tritone separates major from minor keys
column6: The tritone is the halfway point of the circle of 5ths
column7: The tritone axis is the relationship between parallel keys
column11: The tritone divides the octave symmetrically. In jazz, this symmetry allows tritone substitution: D♭7 substitutes for G7 because both share the same tritone (F and B/C♭). More broadly, keys a tritone apart (C and F#) create a structural axis — many jazz composers (Coltrane, Shorter) exploit this symmetry for large-scale harmonic planning.
column12: Include a Schoenberg regions reference: place this key on the harmonic regions chart relative to the home key of C major.
column14: Dmitri Tymoczko, A Geometry of Music — key distance metrics. Richard Cohn, Audacious Euphony — hexatonic poles and key relationships.

column3: What is 'bimodality' in Bartók's harmonic language?
column4: The simultaneous use of major and minor (or two different modes) on the same tonic — creating complex, ambiguous harmonies
column5: The use of two different keys simultaneously
column6: A term for modal mixture
column7: Using the same scale in two different octaves
column11: Bartók's bimodality (also called 'polymodality') superimposes two different modes or scales sharing the same tonic — e.g. C major + C Phrygian, creating harmonies with both major 3rd and minor 2nd. This creates rich, ambiguous sonorities that are distinctly modal yet not traditionally tonal. Found throughout his string quartets and concertos.
column12: Add a modulation analysis: identify three different pivot chords that could connect this key to its relative minor.
column14: Walter Piston, Harmony, 5th ed. Ch. 20-22 (chromatic modulation). Aldwell & Schachter, Harmony and Voice Leading Ch. 30-32. Journal of Music Theory.

column3: What is the 'Neapolitan relationship' between two keys?
column4: A harmonic relationship where one key is built on the ♭II degree of another — e.g. D♭ major as the Neapolitan of C major
column5: Two keys a half step apart
column6: Two keys sharing a common dominant
column7: Two keys with enharmonically spelled key signatures
column11: The Neapolitan relationship (between a key and the major key a major 7th below / minor 2nd above) creates one of the most dramatic key-change gestures in classical music. Schubert frequently juxtaposes tonic and Neapolitan areas (e.g. C major and D♭ major). The half-step bass motion and common-tone voice leading make these shifts viscerally arresting.
column12: Include a borrowed chord note: identify chords borrowed from the parallel minor that commonly appear in this key.
column14: David Beach, translation of Schenker's writings. Arnold Schoenberg, Theory of Harmony (UC Press). Journal of the American Musicological Society.

column3: What is 'just intonation' tuning of key signatures and how does it differ from equal temperament?
column4: Just intonation tunes intervals to pure frequency ratios, making each key sound pure within itself but creating 'wolf intervals' between distant keys; equal temperament compromises all intervals to work equally in all keys
column5: They are the same tuning
column6: Just intonation uses microtones; equal temperament does not
column7: Just intonation adds extra notes to each key signature
column11: In just intonation, a perfect 5th = 3:2 = 701.955 cents; in equal temperament, it's 700 cents. Just intonation makes C major sound pure but creates problems when modulating — keys far on the circle accumulate tuning discrepancies. Equal temperament (12-EDO) sacrifices the purity of individual intervals so all 12 major and minor keys are equally usable.
column12: Add a historical context note: name a landmark classical work composed in this key and discuss its expressive associations.
column14: Alan Forte, The Structure of Atonal Music (Yale). Nicolas Cook, A Guide to Music Analysis (Norton). Cambridge Music Handbooks series.

column3: What is the 'relative tonic' concept in minor key theory?
column4: The idea that minor keys often function with their relative major as a secondary tonal center — the relative major acts as a rest point within the minor key's harmonic orbit
column5: The tonic of the relative minor
column6: A chord acting as a temporary tonic
column7: A type of modal mixture
column11: In minor key pieces, the relative major often serves as a secondary tonal goal, not just a key for modulation. In A minor, C major frequently acts as a secondary resting point. This explains why many minor-key works sound like they're 'in two keys' — they exploit the shared tonal orbit between a minor key and its relative major.
